bugs.error_message: Startup stale-worktree cleanup at scripts/run-sessions.sh:587-606 silently destroys per-session worktrees that the per-wave cleanup explicitly refuses to touch. The startup pass globs '${BRANCH_SANITIZED}--s[0-9]*-*' and calls 'git worktree remove --force || rm -rf' on any whose session id is no longer in the current DAG, with NO is_worktree_in_use() check and NO ahead-of-trunk commit check. The per-wave cleanup at 850-868 has BOTH guards (mirrors bugs 017/060/064 hardening) — same operation, same risk, but only one path enforces them. Repro: epic with session-09 fails mid-run leaving committed-but-unmerged work on epic/myepic--s09-foo and untracked plan/log artefacts in the worktree; user removes session-09-*.md from the sessions dir and re-runs the epic; the next startup deletes the worktree (work in untracked files is gone, branch ref survives but the dirstate is destroyed) without any prompt, error, or log line beyond a routine '↻ removing stale worktree'. Also unsafe under concurrent runners sharing WORKTREE_BASE — a sibling runner's in-flight worktree gets ripped out from under it.
bugs.root_cause: Two cleanup code-paths grew independently. Per-wave cleanup (added during the bug-017/060/064 hardening waves) gained is_worktree_in_use() and the rev-list ahead check; the startup-stale pass — which existed before that hardening and is rarely thought about because it only fires when sessions disappear from the DAG — never received the same treatment. Comment at lines 568-573 only justifies SCOPING the glob to the current epic (avoiding cross-epic deletion), not safety inside the current epic.
bugs.fix: Refactor the deletion into a shared helper (e.g. safe_remove_session_worktree wt_dir branch_name) that performs is_worktree_in_use() and the BRANCH..sess_branch ahead check, and have BOTH the startup pass and the per-wave pass call it. For the startup pass, treat the per-wave 'exit 1 with explanation' on guard-failure as a 'warn + skip' instead — orphaned worktrees should be reported, not silently destroyed.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py parse_frontmatter accepts only literal 'true'/'false' as YAML booleans; YAML 1.1 boolean-strings ('yes', 'no', 'on', 'off', 'y', 'n', plus capitalisations) fall through to the string branch, then load_sessions does bool(fm.get('parallel_safe', True)). bool('no') / bool('No') / bool('off') / bool('n') all return True (non-empty string), so a user who writes 'parallel_safe: no' to force solo-execution actually gets parallel_safe=True and the session runs concurrently with siblings — exactly the opposite of the declared intent. Reproduced directly against the parser: parallel_safe values 'no', 'off', 'n', 'No' all stored as raw strings then coerced to True by bool(). Symptom in the field is silent: the user sees the session scheduled into a parallel wave, no error, and (if touches overlap) downstream merge conflicts that look like a DAG-design problem rather than a frontmatter-parsing problem.
bugs.root_cause: Lines 113-114: 'elif v.lower() in ("true", "false"): result[k] = v.lower() == "true"'. The whitelist is too narrow — YAML 1.1 (and the long tail of editors / linters / docs that still emit YAML 1.1 syntax) treats yes/no/on/off as booleans. The session_dag.py boolean coercion via bool() at line 191 then masks the type mismatch: a non-empty string is truthy regardless of intended meaning, so 'no' → True. There is no defensive check that catches 'parallel_safe was a string when it should have been bool'.
bugs.fix: Two-part: (1) extend the boolean detection in parse_frontmatter to accept the YAML 1.1 set {true,false,yes,no,on,off,y,n} case-insensitively and map yes/y/on→True, no/n/off→False; (2) in load_sessions, when fm.get('parallel_safe') is a STRING that isn't one of the recognised boolean spellings, raise SystemExit with a clear message rather than silently coercing via bool(). Same defence applies to any future bool-typed frontmatter field.

bugs.error_message: wolf-json merge driver _bug_signature includes 'timestamp' as the first key, breaking same-bug deduplication when two parallel sessions detect the SAME root-cause within sub-second of each other. Each session computes the next free bug id from its local view (already covered by bug-090's id-collision-renumber fix), assigns the same id, but produces different ISO timestamps. merge_buglog sees collision-on-id, _bug_signature comparison fails on the timestamp field even though error_message + file + root_cause are identical, falls into the 'different events that merely collided on id' branch, and renumbers the second entry to a new free id. Result: two distinct bug entries (bug-100 and bug-101) describe one event, occurrences:1 each instead of being merged into a single entry with occurrences:2. Reproduced directly: two synthetic ours/theirs blobs with identical error/file/cause but timestamps 333ms apart produced both bug-100 AND bug-101 in the merged output.
bugs.root_cause: Line 36-42: _bug_signature returns (timestamp, error_message, file, root_cause). Including timestamp makes it impossible for two near-simultaneous detections of the same bug to dedupe — yet that is exactly the parallel-session case the merge driver exists for. The bug-090 fix correctly stopped silent data loss on id collision, but the keep-or-renumber decision keys on a signature that two truly-duplicate concurrent detections will never share.
bugs.fix: Remove 'timestamp' from _bug_signature so the dedup key is (error_message, file, root_cause) — the semantic identity of the bug. Keep the existing 'newer last_seen wins' replacement logic, and ALSO accumulate occurrences (sum both sides' occurrences fields) when the signatures match — otherwise a true-duplicate collapse loses the occurrence count from the side that didn't win. Without this fix, every concurrent log of the same bug class compounds into more entries on every parallel wave.

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 4 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 21→62 lines (4 removed)

bugs.error_message: Missing error handling in unknown
bugs.root_cause: Code path had no error handling — exceptions would propagate uncaught
bugs.fix: Added try/catch block | Also: - Every session 01+ MUST include valid frontmatter with at m | Also: Required fields: `session`, `title`, `depends_on`, `touches`; - `produces` — list of paths or `fnmatch` globs the session

bugs.error_message: Epic ran with STATUS=success but Session 02 (backend) shipped no .cs deliverables — its commit contained only the handoff doc and .wolf/* metadata. Session 04 (CI gate) reported PASS without checking that prior sessions' expected files existed, so the missing backend was never caught.
bugs.root_cause: The session-end no-op guard (epic-session.sh:564-574) only fired when HEAD was unchanged AND the worktree was clean — a session that committed ONLY .wolf/anatomy.md|cerebrum.md|memory.md updates plus a docs/roadmap/<epic>/session-NN-handoff.md sailed through. session_completed_on_branch (epic-git.sh:279-322) and the final ALL_SESSIONS_DONE loop (run-sessions.sh:1166-1170) only checked subject-line presence; nothing inspected diff content. Session frontmatter had no way to declare expected outputs, so neither the runner nor a downstream CI-gate session could validate deliverables. The 'CI gate' is a regular session — its validation is whatever the prompt says, not a toolkit phase.
bugs.fix: Added scripts/validate-session-deliverables.py (Python, stdlib-only; reuses parse_frontmatter from epic-dag.py via importlib so YAML 1.1 booleans, BOM, and CRLF handling stay in one place). Two modes: (a) frontmatter `produces:` declared — every entry (path or fnmatch glob) must appear in the session's diff vs the captured baseline_head with status != D, missing entries fail the session with a missing-paths summary; (b) no `produces:` — metadata-only heuristic fails the session if every changed path matches ^\.wolf/ or ^docs/roadmap/.*-handoff\.md$. Authors opt out per-session via `skip_deliverables_check: true` for kickoff/docs-only sessions. Wired into run_one_session (epic-session.sh) immediately after the no-op guard: only runs when rc==0 and HEAD advanced; failure sets rc=97 (distinct from no-op rc=99) so the wave halts before merging the broken session into trunk. Documented `produces:` and `skip_deliverables_check:` in commands/epic.generate.md, .opencode/commands/epic.generate.md, docs/epic-prompt-template.md, and docs/epic-guide.md so the generator emits them on new epics.

bugs.error_message: Sessions that fail deliverables validation surface in the structured [EPIC_RESULT_START] block as 'SESSION_FAIL=... error=unknown ...' instead of identifying the real cause, even though run_one_session sets a dedicated exit code (rc=97) for that case.
bugs.root_cause: epic-session.sh:601 sets rc=97 specifically for deliverables_failure (sibling of rc=99 for no_output and rc=124 for timeout). classify_error() in epic-result.sh handled 124 and 99 explicitly but had no branch for 97. The fall-through then ran log-pattern grep against the validator's stderr (which the runner appended to the exec log as 'ERROR: declared deliverables missing...'). That stderr line does NOT start with the literal '[ERROR]' tag the cli_error pattern requires, and matches none of the cli_crash patterns, so classify_error returned 'unknown'. Result: orchestrators (Claude/OpenCode/external watchers) reading the structured output see 'error=unknown' and cannot distinguish a genuine unknown crash from a deliverables-policy violation.
bugs.fix: Added an explicit `if [[ $exit_code -eq 97 ]]; then echo deliverables_failure; return; fi` branch in classify_error, placed alongside the existing 99 and 124 special cases. error_type now flows through to entry['error_type'] in the result JSON and the SESSION_FAIL line in [EPIC_RESULT_START], so deliverables failures are distinguishable from generic crashes without parsing log text.

bugs.error_message: A session that advances HEAD but produces an empty diff (e.g. an empty merge commit, or a chain of commits that net out to no path changes) silently passes deliverables validation when no `produces:` is declared, defeating the metadata-only heuristic that was supposed to catch sessions with no real output.
bugs.root_cause: _is_metadata_only(changed_paths) returned False when changed_paths was empty, on the (mistaken) assumption that the runner's no-op guard would have caught the case first. But the no-op guard requires HEAD == baseline_head AND a clean tree AND no untracked files; an empty commit, an internal-rename-only commit, or any sequence whose net diff is empty advances HEAD without changing path status. The validator is then invoked (current_head_v != baseline_head), _read_diff returns ([], []), _is_metadata_only([]) returns False, and main() returns 0. Bug-101's intent — catch sessions whose output is purely metadata or nothing — was bypassed for the 'nothing' subset.
bugs.fix: Removed the early `if not changed_paths: return False` short-circuit so the all() over HEURISTIC_METADATA_PATTERNS naturally returns True on an empty set (vacuous truth). Added an explicit empty-changed branch in main()'s error reporting so the user sees 'session HEAD advanced but produced no changed paths' instead of the misleading 'all changed paths matched ...:' header followed by no entries.

bugs.error_message: validate-session-deliverables.py silently flags any deliverable whose path contains a non-ASCII byte or whitespace as 'declared deliverable missing from session diff' even when the file is plainly committed and visible in git's diff output.
bugs.root_cause: _read_diff invoked `git diff --name-status` without `-c core.quotePath=false`. With git's default quotePath=true, any path byte >= 0x80 (em-dash, accented letter, CJK) and any whitespace get rendered as a backslash-escape inside double quotes, e.g. `"src/T\303\253st/UsersService.cs"`. The exact-string compare in _matches_declared then fails against the unquoted entry from the session frontmatter's `produces:` list, so the validator reports the deliverable as missing and run_one_session returns rc=97. Same class as bug-024/035 — a hidden encoding assumption baked into a cross-platform comparison path.
bugs.fix: Pass `-c core.quotePath=false` to the git invocation in _read_diff so paths are emitted in their raw byte form. Verified by creating a session whose `produces:` listed `src/Tëst/UsersService.cs` and `src/Tëst/UsersController.cs`, committing those files, and running the validator: pre-fix the diff line was `"src/T\303\253st/..."` and the entries were reported missing; post-fix the diff is unquoted UTF-8 and the validator returns 0.

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py merge_buglog silently drops any bug entry that lacks an `id` field instead of preserving it under a freshly-allocated id. Same data-loss class as bug-090, but for missing-id rather than colliding-id.
bugs.root_cause: Inside merge_buglog the per-source iteration began with `bid = bug.get('id'); if not bid: continue`. The early `continue` predates the bug-090 collision-recovery rework, which already established that the right policy on a non-mergeable id is to allocate a fresh `bug-NNN` and route the entry through `extras` rather than discard it. A bug entry can lack an id when (a) a hook is interrupted mid-write, (b) a manual edit forgets the field, or (c) schema drift between toolkit versions — in any of those cases git silently merged the file with one fewer entry than the union of inputs.
bugs.fix: When `bid` is empty, take the same branch used for true id collisions: call `_next_free_bug_id(used_ids)`, add the new id to `used_ids`, copy the bug with the renumbered id, and append to `extras`. Verified with a fixture where `theirs.json` carried one normal entry (bug-002) plus one entry without an id — pre-fix the merged buglog contained only bug-001 and bug-002 (the no-id entry was lost); post-fix the merged buglog contained bug-001, bug-002, and bug-003 (the no-id entry preserved with a fresh id).

bugs.error_message: When a session fails deliverables validation (rc=97) the [EPIC_RESULT_START] block prints `error=deliverables_failure exit=97 log=<path>` but no `ERROR_DETAIL=...` line, leaving the orchestrator/user with no inline indication of which deliverable was actually missing. bug-102 fixed the error-type label; the actionable detail line was still routed only through cli_error.
bugs.root_cause: classify_error wrote `${log_file}.errtype` only inside its `^\[ERROR\]` branch (cli_error). The exit-97 branch added by bug-102 returned 'deliverables_failure' without populating the errtype sidecar. write_epic_result reads `${log_file}.errtype` to set entry['error_detail'], and the printer block emits `ERROR_DETAIL=` only when error_detail is non-empty — so the deliverables case never produced one even though validate-session-deliverables.py had written a perfectly usable `ERROR: declared deliverables missing from session diff:` line into the exec log.
bugs.fix: In the exit-97 branch of classify_error, grep the first `^ERROR: ` line out of the exec log, strip the prefix, and write it to `${log_file}.errtype` before returning 'deliverables_failure'. The existing write_epic_result pipeline then surfaces it as `ERROR_DETAIL=declared deliverables missing from session diff:` in [EPIC_RESULT_START]. Verified by sourcing epic-result.sh and calling classify_error against a log containing the validator's stderr — errtype is created with the expected single-line summary.

bugs.error_message: validate-session-deliverables.py decodes `git diff --name-status` output via locale encoding (cp1252 on Windows / ASCII under LC_ALL=C), so paths containing non-ASCII bytes (em-dash, accented letters, CJK) become mojibake before reaching `_matches_declared`. A session that declares `produces: ['src/Tëst.cs']` and actually creates that file is still flagged 'declared deliverables missing from session diff' on any non-UTF-8 locale, defeating the bug-104 fix.
bugs.root_cause: The `_read_diff` helper invokes `subprocess.run(..., capture_output=True, text=True, ...)`. `text=True` opens the captured pipes in text mode using `locale.getpreferredencoding(False)` rather than UTF-8. bug-104 added `-c core.quotePath=false` so git emits *raw UTF-8 bytes* instead of `\303\253`-style escapes — but Python then decodes those raw bytes through cp1252/ASCII before splitting them. Same class as bug-024 / bug-035: every text-IO site has to pin encoding, not just the obvious open() calls. The session-md read at line 146 correctly uses `encoding='utf-8-sig'`, so the *declared* path stays UTF-8 while the *git output* path is mangled, and the comparison silently fails.
bugs.fix: Replace `text=True` with `encoding='utf-8'` (or pass both `text=True, encoding='utf-8'`) on the subprocess.run call at scripts/validate-session-deliverables.py:103. After the change, `_matches_declared` compares two consistently-utf-8 strings on every locale and bug-104's intent — non-ASCII deliverables pass validation — actually holds on Windows / Git-Bash / LC_ALL=C runners. (Patched 2026-05-06T22:30:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: Passing `--max-parallel 0` (or setting `"maxParallel": 0` in `.epic-config.json`) makes run-sessions.sh silently hang forever the first time it tries to launch a session. No error, no progress, no timeout — just a frozen runner.
bugs.root_cause: The numeric-arg validator at lines 141-147 only enforces `^[0-9]+$` (non-negative integer) so 0 slips through. The wave throttle at lines 874-877 then runs `while [[ ${#JOB_PIDS[@]} -ge $MAX_PARALLEL ]]; do reap_finished_jobs; ... sleep 1; done`. With MAX_PARALLEL=0, even an empty JOB_PIDS array satisfies `0 -ge 0`, so the loop spins forever before a single session is spawned. The `--sequential` clamp (line 351) and the `--no-worktree` clamp (line 346) only override values *>1* or only fire when SEQUENTIAL is true, so neither rescues `--max-parallel 0` on its own. Same defensive-numeric-input class as bug-062 / bug-092 (validator accepts something the consumer can't actually handle).
bugs.fix: Tighten the validator: for MAX_PARALLEL specifically (or any 'must be ≥1' arg), reject 0 with a clear message ('--max-parallel must be at least 1'). Alternatively/additionally, clamp post-parse: `[[ "$MAX_PARALLEL" -lt 1 ]] && MAX_PARALLEL=1` after the existing --sequential / --no-worktree overrides. (Patched 2026-05-06T22:30:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: When deliverables validation fails (rc=97) and the upstream Claude/OpenCode session also produced any line starting with `ERROR: ` in its narrated text output (model prose, captured tool output, etc.), the `[EPIC_RESULT_START]` block reports the wrong message under `ERROR_DETAIL=...`. The orchestrator/user sees an unrelated error from the model's transcript instead of the validator's `declared deliverables missing from session diff:` summary — the very actionable detail bug-106 added.
bugs.root_cause: classify_error's exit-97 branch (lines 28-34, added in bug-106) does `grep -m1 -E '^ERROR: ' "$log_file"` against the entire exec log. The log layout is: <Claude exec-phase text output> + <Claude stderr> + (validator stdout, normally empty) + blank line + `=== deliverables validation failed (rc=$vrc) ===` + <validator stderr containing the canonical `ERROR: ...` line>. Because the grep is unanchored to the validator marker and `-m1` returns the FIRST match, any line in Claude's prior text output that happens to begin with `ERROR: ` (the model narrating an error case, an embedded log excerpt, etc.) wins over the validator's line. The errtype sidecar then carries the wrong message, write_epic_result emits it as `ERROR_DETAIL=`, and bug-106's promise of 'name the missing deliverable' silently regresses on those sessions. Same family as bug-080 (unscoped `git log` matching unrelated commits) — operating on too wide a window when a narrower one is required.
bugs.fix: Scope the grep to the validator's output range. Use awk to start matching only after the `=== deliverables validation failed (rc=` marker: e.g. `awk '/^=== deliverables validation failed \(rc=/{f=1; next} f && /^ERROR: /{sub(/^ERROR: /, ""); print; exit}' "$log_file"`. Alternatively, capture the validator stderr to a side-file in run_one_session before deletion and have classify_error read it directly when the side-file exists. (Patched 2026-05-06T22:30:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 2 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 17→28 lines (2 removed) | Also: if [[ ! -f "$plan_file" ]]; then; echo "ERROR: plan phase finished but plan file was

bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: plan_file should be plan_file_session
bugs.root_cause: Used "plan_file" instead of "plan_file_session"
bugs.fix: Changed plan_file → plan_file_session

bugs.error_message: Plan phase reports failure for every session: `ERROR: plan phase finished but plan file was not created: <trunk>/.session-NN-plan.md`. The plan log in the trunk is empty, but the plan markdown actually exists, fully written, inside the *session* worktree at the equivalent path. The exec phase never runs; epic stalls on the first wave.
bugs.root_cause: run_one_session sets `plan_file=$TRUNK_SESSIONS_DIR/.session-NN-plan.md` (absolute path into the trunk worktree) and embeds that path in the PLAN heredoc as the destination Claude must write to. Just before invoking claude it does `cd "$wt_dir"` (per-session worktree). `claude -p` treats its cwd as the project root and refuses to write — or silently rewrites — absolute paths that point into a sibling worktree, even with --dangerously-skip-permissions. So Claude writes the plan inside its own cwd ($wt_dir/.../.session-NN-plan.md), the trunk path stays missing, and the existence check at line 465 reports plan-phase failure. The plan log appears empty because the stream-json transcript carries tool_use events whose paths got redirected, not the original instruction.
bugs.fix: Introduce `plan_file_session=$wt_dir/.session-NN-plan.md` (inside Claude's cwd) and write the prompt instruction against that path. After Claude returns, mirror the file to the canonical trunk archive (`cp -p` to preserve mtime so the resume cache check still works) and remove the session-local copy so the exec phase's `git add -A` does not commit it as a deliverable. The cache-check, plan-size log, and exec-phase `cat $plan_file` paths continue to read from the trunk archive, so resume after worktree teardown still works.

bugs.error_message: In --no-worktree mode without explicit --base, BASE_BRANCH is never defaulted, so session_completed_on_branch (epic-git.sh:300-312) skips its rev-range scoping and runs `git log HEAD` over the entire trunk history including main's commits. A pre-existing commit on main matching `feat: Session N` (e.g. from a previously-merged epic of the same family, or any unrelated commit using session-style subjects) silently makes that session skip Claude execution as 'already committed' on a fresh resume — exactly the bug-080 false-positive class that the cerebrum entry was supposed to prevent. Reproduced: created a repo with main commit `feat: Session 1 — leftover from prior epic`, branched test-epic, sourced epic-git.sh with empty BASE_BRANCH, called session_completed_on_branch 1 HEAD . — returns 0 (false positive). Setting BASE_BRANCH=main first, the same call correctly returns 1.
bugs.root_cause: The auto-default of BASE_BRANCH (gh's defaultBranchRef → 'main' fallback) at run-sessions.sh:653-656 is gated inside `if $USE_WORKTREE; then ... fi`. The else-branch at lines 723-735 (the --no-worktree path) sets TRUNK_WORKTREE_DIR/TRUNK_SESSIONS_DIR but never touches BASE_BRANCH, so it stays at its argv default of "". Then session_completed_on_branch's `if [[ -n "${BASE_BRANCH:-}" ]]; then ... rev_range="${base_ref}..${branch}" ... fi` block is skipped, leaving rev_range="$branch" (=HEAD), and `git log HEAD` walks every ancestor including main. The bug-080 cerebrum note specifically calls out this scoping requirement but the fix in epic-git.sh only works when BASE_BRANCH is non-empty — there is no defensive fallback in the function itself. Same lift-the-default-out-of-mode pattern as bug-046.
bugs.fix: Lift the BASE_BRANCH auto-default block out of the `if $USE_WORKTREE` guard so it runs in both modes. Either move lines 653-656 to immediately after argv parsing (a few lines after line 138), or duplicate the same default-resolution into the --no-worktree else-branch around line 724. As a defence-in-depth backup, session_completed_on_branch could fall back to `git merge-base --fork-point` or hard-code 'main'/'master' when BASE_BRANCH is empty, but pinning the default at run-sessions.sh layer is cleaner. (Patched 2026-05-06T22:30:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py parse_frontmatter crashes with an unhandled ValueError on any frontmatter scalar that lstrips its leading '-' chars to an all-digit string but is not itself a valid int literal — e.g. `key: --3`, `key: ---5`, `key: ----42`. The crash propagates out through load_sessions to main(), so `epic-dag.py … --validate` and `--bash` exit non-zero with a Python traceback; run-sessions.sh's wrapper at lines 388-393 then prints `DAG validation failed` and exits 1, hiding the actual cause unless the user scrolls up.
bugs.root_cause:
Line 121-122 of parse_frontmatter checks `v.lstrip('-').isdigit()` to decide whether to coerce a scalar to int. `lstrip('-')` strips ALL leading dashes, so '--3' lstrips to '3' and isdigit() returns True. The branch then runs `int(v)` on the raw v, which raises ValueError because Python's int() only accepts a single optional leading sign. The check is too permissive: it should either lstrip a single '-' (`v[1:].isdigit() if v.startswith('-') else v.isdigit()`) or wrap the int() in try/except and fall through to the string branch. Reproduced: `parse_frontmatter('---
title: --3
---
body')` raises 'invalid literal for int() with base 10: --3'. Same defensive-numeric-input class as bug-062 / bug-092.
bugs.fix: Tighten the predicate to allow at most one leading '-': `(v.startswith('-') and v[1:].isdigit()) or v.isdigit()`. Or wrap the conversion in `try: result[k] = int(v); except ValueError: result[k] = v.strip("'\"")` so unparseable values fall through to the string branch instead of aborting the entire DAG build. Edge-case input but a real unhandled crash that surfaces as 'DAG validation failed' with no actionable hint. (Patched 2026-05-06T22:30:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: classify_error in scripts/lib/epic-result.sh has no branch for exit code 137 (SIGKILL). Sessions that the wave-timeout path kills via `kill_tree` (run-sessions.sh:977) record `SESSION_EXIT_BY_ID[$_tsid]=137` (line 982), but classify_error only specials 124/99/97; 137 falls through to the grep-based heuristics, returning either 'cli_crash' (if the log happens to contain 'Killed', 'fatal', etc.) or 'unknown' (if the SIGKILL'd CLI didn't get a chance to write anything). The shell-side already prints a clear `KILLED by wave timeout after Xs` line at run 984, but that semantic is dropped from the structured EPIC_RESULT JSON / `[EPIC_RESULT_START]` block, so external watchers and `--start ...` resume hints can't distinguish 'wave timeout' from a generic crash.
bugs.root_cause: classify_error uses an explicit per-code mapping for exit 124 (timeout), 99 (no_output), and 97 (deliverables_failure), then falls through to log-content greps. Exit 137 = 128 + SIGKILL is the canonical signal-termination code, and the wave-timeout reaper at run-sessions.sh:982 is the only producer; nothing in classify_error knows about it. The grep fallback is unreliable for SIGKILL'd processes because the kernel doesn't write anything to the log on SIGKILL — bash's job-control 'Killed' line only appears in interactive shells, not when the parent waits on a backgrounded subshell whose grandchild was killed. Net effect: a wave timeout shows up as `error_type=unknown` in the result file with no `ERROR_DETAIL=` hint, even though the runner already knows exactly what happened.
bugs.fix: Add `if [[ "$exit_code" -eq 137 ]]; then echo 'wave_timeout'; return; fi` (or 'killed') to classify_error, before the grep fallbacks. Optionally also write an errtype file with 'killed by wave timeout (SIGKILL after Xm)' so ERROR_DETAIL surfaces the diagnosis. Same pattern as the existing 124 (timeout), 99 (no_output), 97 (deliverables_failure) special cases. (Patched 2026-05-06T22:30:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py — UI / status_file shows truncated tool target like 'echo \\' instead of the real command 'echo "hello world"' whenever the tool input contains a JSON-escaped quote (Bash with quoted args, file paths containing ", any commit-message preview, etc.).
bugs.root_cause: parse_target() (lines 79-96) extracts the tool target from the accumulated input_json_delta buffer with a naive regex `"command"\s*:\s*"([^"]*)"`. The character class `[^"]*` stops at the FIRST '"' byte, but Claude streams JSON in which string values legitimately contain `\"` escapes — so the capture truncates at the first internal escape. Reproduced: input `{"command": "echo \"hello world\""}` captures only `echo \\` (literally `echo \`) instead of `echo "hello world"`. parse_opencode_target() (lines 99-119) is unaffected because OpenCode hands us a parsed dict, not raw JSON. The regex approach is intentional (input is partial/streaming JSON that may not be parseable yet), but it must respect backslash escapes.
bugs.fix: Replace the naive `"([^"]*)"` patterns with a regex that allows escaped chars, e.g. `"((?:[^"\\\\]|\\\\.)*)"`, then run `bytes(captured, 'utf-8').decode('unicode_escape')` to produce the user-visible string. Apply to every entry in `patterns` (Read/Edit/Write/NotebookEdit/Glob/Grep/Bash/Task/WebFetch/WebSearch). Add a smoke test exercising a Bash command containing a `\"` escape so this regression is caught. (Patched 2026-05-06T23:05:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py — frontmatter values containing an unquoted apostrophe silently swallow trailing inline comments. e.g. a session with `title: It's a test # status` is parsed with title='It\'s a test # status' (comment preserved as part of the value). Worse: any `produces:` / `touches:` line below such a value can ALSO have its inline comment retained, since the apostrophe-induced in_quote state survives until end of line and only resets per-line.
bugs.root_cause: _strip_inline_comment (lines 73-84) toggles in_quote whenever it sees ' or " with a non-backslash predecessor. It treats apostrophes inside unquoted YAML scalars as opening string delimiters; when no closing ' arrives, in_quote stays set for the rest of the line and the `# comment` test (which requires in_quote is None) silently no-ops. Reproduced: `title: It's a test # comment` returns the line UNCHANGED (instead of trimming ` # comment`). Quoted variants (`title: "It's a test" # ok`) work because the outer " toggles back off before the #.
bugs.fix: Only treat ' / " as quote delimiters when they appear at a position where YAML actually opens a string — i.e. the first non-whitespace char of the value (`raw[colon_idx + 1:]`'s first non-space char). Anywhere mid-token, treat them as ordinary characters. Alternatively switch to a tiny tokenizer that tracks `state ∈ {key, value-pre, value-quoted, value-unquoted}` and only honours closing quotes that match the opening state. (Patched 2026-05-06T23:05:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: validate-session-deliverables.py — a session that legitimately produced a file at a path containing a literal '[' or ']' (e.g. `src/[generated]/Schema.cs`, common in scaffolded code, NSwag/OpenAPI output dirs, Next.js dynamic routes like `app/[id]/page.tsx`) is rejected with `ERROR: declared deliverables missing from session diff` even when the file is plainly in the diff. The session is then marked failed (rc=97 / deliverables_failure) and the epic halts.
bugs.root_cause: _matches_declared (lines 66-69) decides whether a `produces:` entry is a glob via `any(c in declared for c in '*?[')`. The presence of '[' alone — without a closing ']' or any wildcard intent — is enough to flip the matcher into fnmatch mode. fnmatch then interprets `[generated]` as a character class (matches one of g,e,n,r,a,t,d), so the literal path `src/[generated]/Schema.cs` does NOT match the literal pattern `src/[generated]/Schema.cs`. Reproduced: `_matches_declared('src/[generated]/Schema.cs', ['src/[generated]/Schema.cs'])` returns []. The companion `?` and `*` checks have no comparable false-positive surface, but Next.js / Remix / Nuxt dynamic-route filenames make this far more reachable than it looks.
bugs.fix: Tighten the glob detection: require BOTH '[' and ']' (and only after sanity-checking the bracket content) before promoting to fnmatch. Concretely: `is_glob = '*' in declared or '?' in declared or re.search(r'\\[[^\\]]+\\]', declared)`. Cleaner alternative: try literal match first, then fall back to fnmatch only if no exact-equal hit, since literal equality is strictly stronger than fnmatch on a fully-literal path. Add a regression fixture exercising `src/[id]/page.tsx`. (Patched 2026-05-06T23:05:00Z.)

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 11 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 20→33 lines (11 removed)

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 7 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 12→35 lines (7 removed)

bugs.error_message: Wrong return value
bugs.root_cause: Was returning: [p for p in changed_paths if fnmatch.fnmatchcase(p
bugs.fix: Now returns: literal

bugs.error_message: cleanup_merged_epic_branches always treats default branch as 'main' regardless of repo (silent fallback masks the real failure)
bugs.root_cause: Line 216 invokes `gh -R "$(git remote get-url origin)" repo view --json defaultBranchRef ...`, but `gh repo view` does NOT accept the `-R` flag — `-R/--repo` is a per-subcommand flag and `repo view` takes the repository as a positional argument, not via `-R`. Tested against gh 2.87.2: the call always errors with `unknown shorthand flag: 'R' in -R` regardless of whether the URL is HTTPS, SSH, or OWNER/REPO. The `2>/dev/null || echo main` swallows the failure and the function silently uses 'main' for every repo. Downstream, the no-PR branch-cleanup case (lines 244-253) compares against 'main' / 'origin/main' instead of the real default — repos whose default is master/develop/trunk never get their merged epic/* branches reaped, and the symptom is invisible because nothing is logged.
bugs.fix: Drop the broken `-R <url>` form for `repo view`. Either (a) call `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef -q '.defaultBranchRef.name'` without `-R` and rely on cwd auto-detection (matches the other two callers in run-sessions.sh:1261 and :1281), or (b) use the positional form `gh repo view "$(git remote get-url origin)" --json ...`. The sibling `pr_state` call on line 228 already uses `gh -R <url> pr list ...` correctly because `pr list` does support `-R` (with URL forms).

bugs.error_message: _strip_inline_comment fails to close a YAML double-quoted string that ends in `\\"` — the trailing `# comment` is silently absorbed into the value
bugs.root_cause: Line 103: `if ch == in_quote and line[i - 1] != "\\":`. The escape check looks at a single previous byte and treats ANY preceding backslash as escaping the quote, but YAML double-quoted strings allow `\\` (escaped backslash). After a literal `\\`, the next `"` IS the actual string terminator — yet the parser sees the trailing `\` and refuses to close. Verified: input `title: "abc\\\\" # back` (where `\\\\` is two backslashes in the source = one literal `\\` in YAML semantics) parses as `'abc\\\\" # back'` — closing quote and the entire `# back` comment leaked into the value. Same class as bug-117 (apostrophe escape) but in the double-quote branch. Realistic trigger: Windows paths in frontmatter (`title: "C:\\Users\\foo\\" # platform-specific`) or any escape-terminated string with a trailing comment.
bugs.fix: Count consecutive backslashes immediately before the candidate closer; only treat the quote as escaped when the run length is ODD. Loop back from i-1 collecting `\\` chars, and close iff the count is even (zero, two, four…). Equivalent: walk forward through the value tracking an `escaped` flag — set on `\\` and consumed on the next char — instead of looking only at line[i-1].

bugs.error_message: _strip_inline_comment treats only ASCII space as a YAML comment separator — a tab before `#` (\t#) silently absorbs the comment text into the value.
bugs.root_cause: Line 121: `elif ch == "#" and line[i - 1] == " ":`. YAML 1.2 §6.6 says comments must be preceded by a *white space* character; YAML's white-space class includes both U+0020 and TAB (U+0009). The parser only checks for literal space, so any frontmatter line that uses a tab as the comment separator (very common in editors that auto-indent with hard tabs, or when authors paste from tools that emit tabs) leaves the `#` un-detected and the trailing comment gets concatenated onto the value. Reproduced: `title: Auth API\t# inline comment` parses `title` as the literal `Auth API\t# inline comment`. Same defect class as bug-117 / bug-123 (apostrophe and double-quote escape edge cases), and bug-098 / bug-097's defensive-vs-loud follow-ups: for `parallel_safe: true\t# …` the bug-097 SystemExit guard now fires loudly with a confusing error mentioning the comment text in the value, but for `title:`, `model:`, `cli:`, and any `produces:` / `touches:` list item the contamination passes silently downstream — Claude is then handed an unknown model id, the deliverables validator reports phantom missing files because the declared path now contains `\t# foo`, and the user has no obvious connection back to the offending tab.
bugs.fix: Treat tab as separator equivalently to space: `elif ch == "#" and (line[i - 1] == " " or line[i - 1] == "\t"):` — or, cleaner, `line[i - 1].isspace()` so any future-added Unicode whitespace is also handled. The fix is one line and the same regression test that covers bug-117/bug-123 should grow a tab-separator case.

bugs.error_message: ERROR_DETAIL surfaced for deliverables_failure (rc=97) is the validator's HEADER line only — the actual missing paths that bug-106 was supposed to expose are silently dropped.
bugs.root_cause: Lines 50-53 awk: `f && /^ERROR: / { sub(/^ERROR: /, ""); print; exit }`. The validator's most common failure (`_matches_declared` returning empty for a declared path) prints a TWO-PART block to stderr: the header `ERROR: declared deliverables missing from session diff:` followed by indented `  - <path>` lines for each missing entry. The awk grabs only the first line that starts with `ERROR: ` and exits — so the captured `err_msg` is just the header without ANY of the path data. The cerebrum entry for bug-106 explicitly claims the fix surfaces 'the missing deliverable' so the user no longer has to open the log; in practice they get `ERROR_DETAIL=declared deliverables missing from session diff:` and still have to crack open `.session-NN-exec.log` to find which file the session forgot. Reproduced against a synthetic two-deliverable failure log — captured detail is the colon-terminated header with zero path information. The other validator error modes (`session HEAD advanced but produced no changed paths`, `session committed only metadata/handoff files — no real deliverables`) are single-line and unaffected; the regression is specific to the `produces:`-mismatch path, which is precisely the case bug-106 was added to make actionable.
bugs.fix: After matching the header, slurp subsequent `^  - ` lines until a blank/non-indent line, then exit. Awk sketch: once the header matches, set a second flag and append following `^  - ` lines to a buffer, then `print buffer; exit` on the first non-list line or EOF. Keep the join character a literal `; ` (or pipe) so ERROR_DETAIL stays a single-line value compatible with the `[EPIC_RESULT_START]` line-protocol parser. Optionally cap at N entries (e.g., 5) with an `…and K more` suffix to avoid blowing out the orchestrator UI.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py _strip_inline_comment treats only ASCII space as the gap between `:` and the opening quote of a value — a tab-separated quoted value (`title:\t"foo # bar"`) never enters quote-tracking mode, so the `#` inside the value is treated as a YAML inline-comment separator and the trailing portion (` bar")` is silently truncated. Same defect family as bug-117/123/124.
bugs.root_cause: The `while line[value_start] == ' ': value_start += 1` loop at lines 91-92 only skips ASCII space when locating the value-start position. If the YAML uses a tab (or any non-space whitespace) between `:` and the opening `"`/`'`, value_start lands on the tab, the subsequent `if line[value_start] in ('"', "'"):` test fails, in_quote stays None, and the # inside the quoted value is then matched by the inline-comment branch on line 121 (which DOES correctly recognize tab as a separator after the bug-124 fix). The asymmetry between the two whitespace checks is the bug — bug-124 hardened one branch and missed the symmetric site here.
bugs.fix:
Replace the space-only skip with a whitespace skip that matches bug-124's separator set: `while ... and line[value_start] in (' ', '	'): value_start += 1`. Verified by `parse_frontmatter('---
title:	"hello # world"
---
')` returning `{'title': 'hello'}` (truncated) instead of `{'title': 'hello # world'}`.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py parse_frontmatter silently drops every block-list item whose leading indent is anything other than 0, 2, or 4 ASCII spaces — and silently drops tab-indented or `-\t<value>`-style items too. A user (or LLM) writing a perfectly normal `produces:\n   - src/Foo.cs` (3-space) or `produces:\n\t- src/Foo.cs` (tab) ends up with `produces=[]`, which then takes the validator down the metadata-only fallback path and silently disables the deliverables check the session asked for.
bugs.root_cause: Line 140 gates list-item recognition on `raw.startswith(('  - ', '- ', '    - '))` — a hardcoded prefix whitelist for 0/2/4-space indents only. Any other indent (1, 3, 5, 6+ spaces, leading tab, or `-\t` instead of `- `) falls through. The line then has no `:` so `if ':' not in raw: continue` skips it. Net effect: items vanish, current_key was already set to e.g. 'produces' on the parent line, `result['produces']` stays at its initial `[]` from the empty-value branch (line 152). Downstream in validate-session-deliverables.py the `if produces:` check is False, falls through to `_is_metadata_only(changed)`, and any single non-metadata file in the diff makes the validator return 0 — rubber-stamping a session that may have written entirely the wrong files.
bugs.fix:
Replace the prefix-whitelist test with a proper block-item check: strip leading whitespace (spaces and tabs), then test `lstripped.startswith('- ')` or `lstripped == '-'` or `lstripped.startswith('-	')`. Track the indent of the parent key so a sibling key (less or equal indent) terminates the list. Verified via `parse_frontmatter('---
produces:
   - src/Foo.cs
   - src/Bar.cs
---
')` returning `produces=[]` instead of the two paths.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py parse_frontmatter splits flow-list values on every `,` without respecting quotes — `tags: ["a, b", c]` parses as ['a', 'b', 'c'] instead of ['a, b', 'c']. For session frontmatter this corrupts any `touches:` or `produces:` flow-list containing a path/glob with a literal comma; the validator (validate-session-deliverables.py) then false-flags the session because the entries it's matching against were silently rebuilt under different keys. Same defect class as the quote-aware audits already applied to _strip_inline_comment in bug-117 (apostrophe), bug-123 (escaped backslash), bug-124 (tab-before-`#`) and bug-126 (tab-after-`:`); this is the remaining unaudited parser site.
bugs.root_cause: parse_frontmatter at the flow-list branch did `inner.split(",")` blindly. The split has no knowledge of quoted spans, so a `,` inside `"..."` or `'...'` ended an item prematurely and the tail of the quoted value became its own bogus item. The same pattern was already audited and fixed for the comment-stripping path; the flow-list path was missed.
bugs.fix: Extracted a small `_split_flow_list` helper inside parse_frontmatter that walks the body character-by-character, tracking single-/double-quote state and respecting backslash escapes (matching the run-of-`\` parity check from bug-123). Replaced the blind `inner.split(",")` with `_split_flow_list(inner)`. Verified: `["a, b", c]`, `['a, b', c]`, `["x", 'y, z', w]`, `["a\\\\", "b, c"]`, `[1, 2, 3]`, `[]`, and trailing-comma forms all parse correctly. Existing block-list (multi-line `- item`) parsing is unchanged.

bugs.error_message: load_sessions silently accepts two session files that claim the same NN (e.g. session-01-foo.md AND session-01-bar.md). Two failure modes: (1) implicit deps — sorted-by-id linearization makes the second one depends_on=[N] where N is its own id, and build_dag prints the misleading error 'session NN cannot depend on itself' (real cause: duplicate filenames, not YAML); (2) explicit `depends_on: []` — emit_bash writes two SESSION rows with the same id, the runner META parser overwrites SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[N]/SESSION_SLUG_BY_ID[N] with whichever line came last (silently dropping the first file) AND appends both ids to WAVE_IDS[N], so the wave loop in run-sessions.sh tries to create the same per-session worktree twice — either tripping is_worktree_in_use against the in-flight first session or racing to delete the worktree out from under it.
bugs.root_cause: load_sessions iterates os.listdir(sessions_dir) and appends every match of SESSION_RE to the sessions list with no per-id uniqueness check. The downstream linearization assumes ids are unique within `sessions`; the sort step doesn't deduplicate; build_dag's `by_id` comprehension silently overwrites collisions; compute_waves keeps both copies in the ready/wave lists. Nothing along the path reports the actual duplicate.
bugs.fix: Added a `seen_ids: dict[int, str]` map at the top of load_sessions, populated as each filename is matched. On collision, raise SystemExit with the two filenames and a clear remediation (rename one). The check sits AFTER the slug whitelist (so the user sees the slug error first if both apply) and BEFORE the sid==0 short-circuit, so duplicate operator files (two session-00-*.md) are caught too — previously load_sessions silently kept the lexicographically-later one as the operator. Verified: implicit-deps duplicate, explicit-empty-deps duplicate, and duplicate session-00 all now exit with a clear ERROR and rc=1.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py _strip_inline_comment silently truncates a YAML flow-list (`key: [...]`) at the first `#` that appears inside a quoted entry. Same-line frontmatter like `touches: ["src/a.py", "src/b # c.py"]` is parsed as the string `'["src/a.py", "src/b'` instead of a 2-element list; load_sessions then trips its `if not isinstance(touches, list)` guard and exits with the misleading message 'touches must be a list of globs', pointing the user at YAML syntax that is in fact valid. Same defect also affects `tags`, `produces`, and any other flow-list field. The audit chain bug-117/123/124/126/132 was supposed to harden every YAML token-boundary site against quotes; bug-132 added a quote-aware `_split_flow_list`, but that fix is dead code for any flow-list whose contents contain `#`, because `_strip_inline_comment` runs first and corrupts the line before _split_flow_list ever sees it.
bugs.root_cause: `_strip_inline_comment` only opens `in_quote` when the value's FIRST non-space character (after `:` or `- `) is `"` or `'`. A flow list starts with `[`, which is neither, so `in_quote` stays None for the entire scan; quotes that appear later inside the brackets (the legitimate openers for individual entries) are walked past as ordinary characters. Once any of those entries contains a ` # ` (space-hash, the YAML comment trigger), the `elif ch == "#" and line[i-1] in (" ", "\t")` branch fires and the line is truncated mid-flow-list. The function has no flow-context state machine — the existing scalar-context quote tracking is structurally unable to handle `[...]` content.
bugs.fix: Extend `_strip_inline_comment` with flow-list awareness: when the first non-space char at value_start is `[` or `{`, enter flow mode. While in flow mode, track `in_quote` on every `"`/`'` (with the same backslash-parity check used in the bug-123 fix) and DO NOT strip `#`. Exit flow mode on the matching `]` / `}` outside quotes; thereafter the existing scalar `#`-strip resumes so a trailing `# comment` after the flow close still works. Add fixtures: `tags: ["a # b", c]` → `['a # b', 'c']`; `tags: ['a # b', c] # trailing`  → `['a # b', 'c']` with comment stripped; `tags: ["a # b"]\t# c` → comment stripped (tab-before-#, intersect with bug-124).

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py _strip_inline_comment recognises only `- ` (dash-space) as a YAML block-list-item value-start; `-\t` (dash-tab) falls through to scalar-mode and silently truncates any quoted item that contains a `#`. A `produces:` (or `touches:`) entry written as `\t-\t"src/path # weird.py"` parses as `['src/path']`, dropping `# weird.py"` from the path. The block-list parser at lines 199-204 already accepts `-\t` (it was part of the bug-127 fix), so the post-strip check is happy with the truncated raw — the comment-stripper just never realised it was inside a list item, never opened in_quote on the following `"`, and the unanchored `#`-stripper cut the value short. Result: validate-session-deliverables.py false-flags the session because the produces entry it's trying to match is no longer the path the session actually wrote. Symmetric to bug-126 (tab between `:` and value) but in the block-list-item branch — same audit class the bug-126 cerebrum note explicitly calls out.
bugs.root_cause: Line 86: `if stripped.startswith("- "):` admits only the ASCII-space variant. The bug-127 fix taught the *block-list parser* to recognise `-\t` and bare `-`, but the prior comment-stripper pass was never updated to match, so the stripper still uses the ASCII-space-only test and misclassifies any tab-separated list item as a scalar.
bugs.fix: Replace `if stripped.startswith("- "):` with a check that mirrors the block-list parser's tolerance: detect `- ` OR `-\t` (and treat a bare `-` row as having no value, so value_start stays -1 and there is nothing for the `#`-stripper to match against quoted-value rules). Concretely: `if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] == "-" and stripped[1] in (" ", "\t"): value_start = leading_ws + 2`. Skip-ws step at line 98 already handles either character. Add a fixture covering `\t-\t"a # b"` and confirm `\t- "a # b"` (existing) still passes.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py _strip_inline_comment uses the same `\\`-parity check (`bs % 2 == 0` to decide whether a quote terminates the string) for BOTH double-quoted and single-quoted YAML strings. YAML 1.2 single-quoted scalars do not honour backslash escapes — `\\` is a literal backslash and the only escape is `''` for an embedded apostrophe — so a frontmatter line like `key: 'C:\\path\\' # comment` (a literal Windows-ish path that legitimately ends in a backslash) leaves bs=1 (odd) at the closing apostrophe, in_quote never closes, and the trailing ` # comment` is silently absorbed into the value. The end-of-line return path keeps in_quote open and `result[k]` retains both the closing quote and the comment text. Lower impact than bug-134/135 (rare to terminate a single-quoted YAML scalar with `\`), but it is the third member of the same audit class and the docstring's claim that the function obeys YAML 1.2 §6.6 is no longer accurate for single-quoted strings.
bugs.root_cause: The bug-123 fix added `bs % 2 == 0` parity tracking to handle `\\"` (escaped backslash followed by real terminator) inside double-quoted scalars. The same `for i in range(scan_start, len(line))` block uses one branch for both quote styles and applies the same parity check unconditionally. Single-quoted YAML treats `\` as literal, so the parity check is wrong: any `\` immediately before the closing `'` falsely keeps the string open.
bugs.fix: Branch the quote-close logic by quote style. If `in_quote == "'"`, the closer is unconditional (any `'`) UNLESS the next char is also `'`, in which case it is a YAML-style escaped apostrophe and we skip both. If `in_quote == '"'`, keep the existing backslash-parity rule (preserves bug-123's fix). Same change is needed in `_split_flow_list`'s walker for symmetry — same single-vs-double asymmetry exists there. Fixtures: `key: 'a\\' # c` → `'a\\'` (comment stripped); `key: 'a''b' # c` → `"a'b"` (comment stripped); existing `key: "a\\\\" # c` (bug-123) still passes.

bugs.error_message: Malformed session frontmatter such as session: not-a-number raises a Python traceback instead of a clear validation error.

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of

bugs.fix: Rewrote 3→4 lines (3 removed)

bugs.error_message: Session validator flags `produces:` entry as missing when path mixes a literal `[` (e.g. Next.js `[id]` dynamic route) with a glob meta char like `*`.
bugs.root_cause: _matches_declared falls into fnmatch.fnmatchcase as soon as the declared string contains any of `*?[`. fnmatch then interprets `[id]` as a character class (matches `i` or `d`), so a real path containing literal `[id]` fails to match. bug-118 fixed the no-glob case via a literal-equality probe, but a declared path that has BOTH a literal `[` AND a glob still bypasses the literal probe (paths aren't exact) and is mangled by fnmatch — so the file is plainly in the diff yet the validator returns rc=97 'declared deliverable missing'.
bugs.fix: Replaced the fnmatch.fnmatchcase call with a hand-rolled regex matcher that escapes every char except `*`/`?` (translated to `.*`/`.`). Treats `[`/`]` as literal characters always, so Next.js / Remix dynamic-route segments survive even when the declared path mixes a literal bracket with a glob. Trade-off: real fnmatch character classes (`[abc]`, `[a-z]`, `[!x]`) are no longer respected — but `produces:` declarations don't use them in practice and the literal-bracket case is far more common.

bugs.error_message: Frontmatter `model: yes` (or `model: y`/`model: on`/`model: true`) produces a SESSION row containing the literal token `True` because the YAML 1.1 boolean recognition fires on string-only fields too.
bugs.root_cause: parse_frontmatter's boolean whitelist (line 286-293, added by bug-097 for `parallel_safe`) coerces ANY key whose value is in {true,false,yes,no,on,off,y,n} to a Python bool. load_sessions defends `parallel_safe` against an isinstance(str) leak (line 394-400) but applies no equivalent guard to model/cli/title. emit_bash then formats the bool via `model = s['model'] or '-'`, which yields literal `True` (truthy) or `-` (falsy `False`). The bash side parses `SESSION ... True ...` and run_cli passes `--model True` to claude, which rejects the unknown model id. Verified end-to-end with a synthetic session.
bugs.fix: Added an isinstance(value, bool) guard for `title`/`model`/`cli` in load_sessions, mirroring the bug-097 string-leak guard for `parallel_safe`. Placed before the existing cli/model shape checks so the YAML 1.1 cause is named first instead of being shadowed by the downstream 'must be claude or opencode' surrogate error.

bugs.error_message: Frontmatter `model: "Custom Sonnet"` (any model/cli value containing whitespace) corrupts the SESSION row — bash splits the value across positional fields, smodel becomes the first word, scli becomes the second, and the trailing `-` is shifted into a non-existent `$9`.
bugs.root_cause: emit_bash writes `SESSION <wn> <sid> <file> <deps> <slug> <par> <model> <cli>` as space-delimited and run-sessions.sh parses via `set -- $_rest` (line 437-449). bug-076 introduced the `-` sentinel to keep empty middle fields from collapsing, but no validator constrains model/cli to space-free tokens. parse_frontmatter happily strips the surrounding quotes, so a quoted `"Custom Sonnet"` arrives in emit_bash as `Custom Sonnet`. set -- splits on whitespace, smodel=`Custom`, scli=`Sonnet`. Same bug class as bug-062 (slug spaces) and bug-092 (slug glob meta). Verified with a synthetic session — bash sees 9 tokens instead of 8.
bugs.fix: Added a regex shape-check on `model` in load_sessions: `[A-Za-z0-9._/+:-]+`. cli already has a stricter whitelist ({claude,opencode}) so doesn't need the additional guard. Same defensive-numeric pattern as bug-062 (slug spaces) and bug-092 (slug glob meta).

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 2 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 14→34 lines (2 removed)

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py parse_frontmatter strips quote characters off YAML scalar values without decoding YAML 1.2 escape sequences. After bug-123 (double-quoted backslash parity) and bug-136 (single-quoted '' escape) made `_strip_inline_comment` correctly LOCATE the closing quote, value extraction was still `v.strip("'\"")` at four sites: parse_frontmatter:303 (string scalars), _split_flow_list:241/248 (flow-list items), and the block-list-item branch:270. Two failure modes: (1) escapes are never decoded — `title: "He said \"hi\""` yields the raw `He said \"hi\"` instead of the YAML-correct `He said "hi"`, and `'It''s'` yields `It''s` instead of `It's`; (2) when a value ends with `\"` right before the real closing `"`, the right-hand strip pulls BOTH `"` chars off, exposing the `\` and dropping the user's intended `"` — so `"He said \"hi\""` collapses to `He said \"hi\` (10 chars), strictly losing data the bug-123 fix had been written to preserve. Same bug applies to flow-list and block-list quoted entries with internal escapes.
bugs.root_cause: All four consumer sites use `str.strip("'\"")` — a blind two-sided trim of any `'` or `"` chars from the boundary — instead of a YAML-aware decode. The strip walks until it hits a non-quote char, so a value ending `\"` exposes the `\` and the strip happily eats the next `"` over. Single-quoted `''` and double-quoted `\X` escapes are never converted to their literal forms because there is no decode step at all.
bugs.fix: Added `_unquote_scalar` helper inside parse_frontmatter that recognises a properly-quoted scalar by matching outer quote chars (length >= 2). Double-quoted (§5.7): walks the body and decodes \\, \", \/, \n, \t, \r, \0, \a, \b, \v, \f, \e, with `\<unknown>` falling through to the literal next char so a forwards-compatible escape doesn't crash. Single-quoted (§7.3.2): walks and converts `''` to `'`. Anything not recognised as a quoted scalar (unquoted bareword, mismatched outer quotes, len < 2) falls back to the historical strip("'\"") so plain values still work. Replaced all four call sites: parse_frontmatter:303, _split_flow_list:241/248, block-list-item:270. Verified 15 regression cases including the bug-132/134/136 quote-aware fixtures still pass.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py load_sessions does not guard the `session:` frontmatter field against YAML 1.1 boolean coercion. parse_frontmatter's boolean whitelist (added by bug-097) coerces `session: y` / `session: no` / `session: on` to True / False. bug-142 added an isinstance(value, bool) guard for title/model/cli but missed `session:`. With session=True, the bug-139 try/except for int() does NOT fire because `int(True) == 1`, so control falls through to the id-vs-filename mismatch check at line ~376 which raises the misleading error 'declares session=True but filename says 02' — pointing the user at a session/filename mismatch when the real cause is a YAML 1.1 boolean trap on a numeric field.
bugs.root_cause: The bug-142 audit guarded title/model/cli against YAML 1.1 boolean coercion but did not extend the same guard to `session:`, even though `session:` is the most numeric of all frontmatter fields. parse_frontmatter happily coerces `y/n/yes/no/on/off/true/false` to bool, and bool is a numeric type in Python (issubclass(bool, int)), so the downstream `int(fm_session)` succeeds and produces `1` for True / `0` for False — bypassing the bug-139 SystemExit and surfacing as a session/filename mismatch instead.
bugs.fix: Added an isinstance(fm_session, bool) check immediately before the int(...) try block in load_sessions. On bool, raise SystemExit naming the YAML 1.1 cause first (yes/no/on/off/y/n/true/false coerce to booleans — quote the value or use the numeric id), so the user sees the real cause instead of the misleading mismatch. Mirrors the bug-142 guard pattern. Verified: `session: y` now raises with the bool-aware message; `session: 99` (numeric mismatch) still hits the existing filename-mismatch branch; `session: 1` and `session: "01"` still load correctly.

bugs.error_message: Missing error handling in _bugs_of
bugs.root_cause: Code path had no error handling — exceptions would propagate uncaught
bugs.fix: Added try/catch block

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh accepts --cli (and `.epic-config.json` `cli`) values outside {claude, opencode}; non-claude strings silently route through run_cli's OpenCode branch and corrupt the model-flag selection.
bugs.root_cause: epic-dag.py's load_sessions rejects per-session frontmatter `cli:` values outside {claude, opencode} (lines 503-507), but the same defence was missing on the command-line / config-file path. `--cli) CLI_OVERRIDE="$2"; shift 2 ;;` (line 119) and the regex extraction at lines 182-184 took the value verbatim. CLI_OVERRIDE was then assigned to CLI_CMD with only a `command -v` PATH check (line 218). Downstream, run_cli's `if [[ "$session_cli" == "claude" ]]` (epic-session.sh:208) treats every non-claude string as opencode and invokes `$session_cli run --format json --dangerously-skip-permissions`; `map_model_shorthand` (line 248) also bypasses its mapping because `[[ "$cli" == "opencode" ]]` is false, so `--model sonnet --cli foo` arrives at the foreign binary as the bare shorthand `sonnet`. Same audit class as bug-138 (frontmatter side already fixed); this is the symmetric runner-side site.
bugs.fix: Added a whitelist guard right before CLI detection: `if [[ -n "$CLI_OVERRIDE" && "$CLI_OVERRIDE" != "claude" && "$CLI_OVERRIDE" != "opencode" ]]; then err ...; exit 1; fi`. Catches both `--cli foo` and `.epic-config.json` `"cli": "foo"` because both funnel through CLI_OVERRIDE. Mirrors the epic-dag.py guard so the validation message names {claude, opencode} explicitly.

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py crashes mid-merge with TypeError when buglog.json has `"bugs": null` (or any non-iterable bugs value). git records the file as unresolvable and the wolf auto-resolve path silently turns into a real conflict.
bugs.root_cause: merge_buglog called `src.get("bugs", [])` at three sites (ancestor scan line 85, used_ids set comprehension line 92, main merge loop line 98). `dict.get(key, default)` only returns the default when the KEY IS ABSENT — if the key exists with value None (a partially-flushed write, a hand-edit, or schema drift), the call returns None and `for bug in None` raises TypeError. load() only catches JSONDecodeError/OSError/UnicodeDecodeError, so the semantic-shape failure propagates out of merge_buglog, the driver exits 1, and git's merge resolver leaves the .wolf/buglog.json with conflict markers — defeating the entire point of having a custom merge driver. Same audit class as bug-035's `audit every open() site` admonition, just for `.get(key, default)` consuming an iterable.
bugs.fix: Added a `_bugs_of(src)` helper that returns `src.get("bugs") or []`, applied at all three sites (ancestor, used_ids, main loop). Now tolerates `"bugs": null`, `"bugs": 42`, and any other shape that doesn't iterate as a list of bug dicts. Verified: `merge_buglog({'bugs': None}, {'bugs': []}, {'bugs': None})` now returns `{'bugs': []}` instead of crashing.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py's `_split_flow_list` does not track nested flow-list / flow-map brackets, so a nested entry in `touches:` / `produces:` / `depends_on:` (e.g. `tags: [[a, b], c]`) is silently split mid-bracket and yields the wrong number of items.
bugs.root_cause: Asymmetric audit fix. bug-134 added `flow_depth` tracking to `_strip_inline_comment` so nested `[...]` / `{...}` don't trip the `#`-stripper, but the symmetric site — `_split_flow_list`, the function that consumes the body of the flow list and yields items — only tracked `in_q` (quote state). It had no `flow_depth` counter, so the inner body of `[[a, b], c]` (i.e. `[a, b], c`) walked at depth-blind state, the first `,` after `[a` triggered a split, and the items came back as `['[a', 'b]', 'c']` instead of `['[a, b]', 'c']`. Downstream the validator (validate-session-deliverables.py) and load_sessions both consume these items as paths/globs/ints — silently corrupted before they ever reach a typed check. Same defect class as bug-117/123/124/126/132 (every parser site that consumes a token boundary must respect quotes); this extends the audit set to flow nesting.
bugs.fix: Added a `flow_depth` int alongside `in_q` in `_split_flow_list`. `[`/`{` outside quotes increments, `]`/`}` decrements (clamped to >=0 so a malformed close doesn't underflow). The `,` branch now only fires when `in_q is None AND flow_depth == 0`. Verified end-to-end: parse_frontmatter on `touches: [[a, b], c, "d, e"]` now returns `['[a, b]', 'c', 'd, e']` — preserving both the bug-149 nested case and the bug-132 quoted-comma case.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh: explicit `--wave-timeout 0` (or `.epic-config.json` `"waveTimeout": 0`) sets WAVE_TIMEOUT_USER_SET=true, bypasses the auto-derive 240-min default, lands wave_timeout=0 seconds, and the wait-loop comparison `$wave_elapsed -gt 0` fires immediately after the first 2-second sleep — killing every in-flight session before it can do real work and reporting the epic as failed.
bugs.root_cause: The auto-derive branch (`if ! $WAVE_TIMEOUT_USER_SET`) was the ONLY path that guaranteed wave_timeout > 0; the explicit-user branch passed WAVE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES through verbatim and the numeric validator at line 141 happily accepts 0 (`^[0-9]+$`). The `--timeout 0` per-session convention treats 0 as DISABLED everywhere else in the CLI, so users naturally expect the same for `--wave-timeout`. There was no symmetric handling for the wave-level flag, so 0 silently inverted intent.
bugs.fix: Gate the wave-timeout comparison on `[[ $wave_timeout -gt 0 && $wave_elapsed -gt $wave_timeout ]]` so 0 means DISABLED (consistent with --timeout 0). Also branched the pre-wave log line so it prints `(no wave timeout)` instead of `(timeout 0m)` when the ceiling is disabled, surfacing the chosen behavior.

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py: when the ancestor blob is unreadable (load() returns None on JSONDecodeError/OSError/UnicodeDecodeError, or git passes /dev/null %O for a brand-new file in both branches), the bug-137 occurrence formula `ours + theirs - base_occ` falls back to base_occ=0 for every bid and silently double-counts every true-duplicate bug — buglog.json shows occurrences=20 instead of the correct 10.
bugs.root_cause: The bug-137 fix populates ancestor_occ inside `if isinstance(ancestor, dict)` but had no `else` branch. ancestor=None left ancestor_occ empty, and the per-bid `ancestor_occ.get(bid, 0)` lookup used 0 as the baseline. The fallback never triggered for a healthy merge, but a single corrupted ancestor blob was enough to inflate every shared-history occurrence count on subsequent automerged JSON files. Same audit class as bug-148 (`audit every assumption that a deserialised JSON value will be the expected shape`).
bugs.fix: Add an else branch when ancestor is not a dict that populates ancestor_occ[bid] = min(ours_occ, theirs_occ) for every bid present on BOTH sides. The invariant `ancestor_occ <= min(ours_occ, theirs_occ)` always holds because occurrences only grow from the shared baseline, so this never over-counts (it may under-count when one branch added more than the other — the conservative trade-off when the ancestor is genuinely unreadable).

bugs.error_message: extract_prompt fallback in epic-session.sh leaks a leading `# Title` line into the model prompt for session files that have no YAML frontmatter. The function header docs claim to `Strip frontmatter and the title line, return the rest`, but the awaiting_title window only opens INSIDE the `in_fm && /^---$/` rule, so frontmatter-less files never enter the title-strip state.
bugs.root_cause: BEGIN initialises `awaiting_title=0`, and the only path that flips it to 1 is the frontmatter-close rule. Files without a `---` block bypass the entire awaiting_title machinery — the `# Title` heading falls through to the catch-all `{ print }` and ships to the model. The discrepancy is invisible because the primary `"""md"""` fence path catches well-formed session files; the fallback only runs when the user wrote a session prompt without the markdown fence, which is rare but valid.
bugs.fix: Initialise `BEGIN { awaiting_title=1 }` so the strip window is open for line 1 by default. Files that DO start with `---` immediately flip `in_fm=1` and `awaiting_title=0` in the existing line-1 frontmatter rule; the `in_fm && /^---$/` close-rule re-arms awaiting_title=1 after the closing `---`, preserving the original behavior for frontmatter files.
