bugs.error_message: build_handoff_section silently injects an unrelated epic's handoff when a DAG parent in the current epic didn't write one. find_handoff_for(pid) checks docs/roadmap/<current-epic>/session-NN-handoff.md first, then falls back to globbing docs/roadmap/*/session-NN-handoff.md across every roadmap dir in the repo and returns the alphabetically-first match. Reproduced end-to-end: epic-A wrote session-02-handoff.md; epic-B's session 02 did not; find_handoff_for 2 (run with EPIC_NAME_SLUG=epic-B) returned epic-A's path. The dependent session's prompt then includes a '## Previous Session Handoffs' header introducing 'These handoff documents come from your DAG parents. Treat their file paths as the only memory of prior work' followed by content from a completely unrelated epic — the model has no way to detect the mismatch and treats the foreign epic's narrative as authoritative context.
bugs.root_cause: find_handoff_for has two probe loops: a single-element loop scoped to $EPIC_NAME_SLUG, and a fallback `for cand in $REPO_ROOT/docs/roadmap/*/session-NN-handoff.md` that walks every sibling roadmap dir. The DAG dependency model is intra-epic by construction (depends_on takes integer session ids within the current epic), so the second loop is unreachable as a real cross-epic dependency expression — its only practical firing condition is 'parent in the current epic produced no handoff', which is exactly the case where the model should be told nothing rather than handed an unrelated artifact. The header text intensifies the harm by claiming the injected document is 'memory of prior work'.
bugs.fix: Pending: drop the cross-epic fallback loop in find_handoff_for so the function returns empty when the current-epic probe fails. build_handoff_section already handles an empty result correctly (the bug-141-class guard on line 151 suppresses the section header when no entries were collected), so the change is purely subtractive. If genuine cross-epic handoffs are ever needed, they should be opt-in via explicit configuration with a logged 'fell back to epic X' notice, not a silent alphabetical-first match.

bugs.error_message: scripts/wolf-merge/resolve-wolf.sh fails with `pathspec '.wolf/<file>' did not match any file(s) known to git` and aborts under set -e when invoked from any subdirectory of the repo. The script is documented as the manual fallback for resolving in-progress merge/rebase conflicts in .wolf/ files, so this surfaces precisely when the user is mid-incident and least equipped to debug it. Demonstrated empirically: from cwd `scripts/lib/`, `git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U` returns repo-root-relative paths (`.wolf/hooks/_session.json`), but `git checkout --ours -- .wolf/hooks/_session.json` from the same cwd errors with rc=1 and the pathspec-not-found message. The same mismatch hits the `git add -- $f` line two statements later. Net effect: zero `.wolf/` conflicts get resolved, the merge/rebase stays paused with conflict markers in place, and set -euo pipefail kills the script before the `commit --no-edit` / `rebase --continue` step at the bottom even runs.
bugs.root_cause: Lines 40, 58, 59 mix two different git path resolution conventions in the same script. `git diff --name-only` (and `git status --porcelain`, `git ls-files`) print paths from the repo top-level regardless of the invoking cwd — verified in this repo's tree from both `/Users/aramirez/Code/epic-toolkit` and `scripts/lib/`. `git checkout -- <pathspec>` and `git add -- <pathspec>`, by contrast, treat the pathspec as a CWD-relative path unless it begins with `:/`. So a path that's truthful at the diff layer becomes wrong-by-one-segment at the checkout layer the moment cwd is anywhere except the repo root. The sibling helper `auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts` in scripts/lib/epic-git.sh (line 117-127) doesn't have the bug because it threads `git -C "$workdir"` through every call — the runner only invokes it from a known-good cwd. resolve-wolf.sh, designed for ad-hoc terminal use, has no such anchoring: it captures REPO_ROOT at line 15 but never `cd` to it, never passes `-C "$REPO_ROOT"`, and never prefixes pathspecs with `:/` (the `:/path` form makes git resolve relative to the top-level). Same defect class as the cd-and-cwd audits in run-sessions.sh (e.g. bug-089's `$0`-vs-cwd path resolution) but in the manual recovery tool.
bugs.fix: Added `cd "$REPO_ROOT"` immediately after the REPO_ROOT/GIT_DIR capture (before the SIDE detection and any git pathspec call). This anchors every subsequent `git diff --name-only`, `git checkout --$SIDE -- $f`, and `git add -- $f` to a consistent path-resolution origin (the repo top-level), so the diff output and the checkout pathspec interpret the same path. Chose cd over the `:/$f` pathspec form because the script invokes git directly in five places (lines 40, 58, 59, 65, 68) and the cd is a single one-line change versus prefixing every pathspec; chose cd over `git -C` for the same reason. As a bonus the user-facing `printf '  %s\n' $NON_WOLF` error message now prints paths the user can copy and act on directly without first having to translate them into cwd-relative form. The auto-resolver in lib/epic-git.sh remains untouched — it already uses `-C "$workdir"` correctly.

bugs.error_message: validate-session-deliverables.py's `_matches_declared` glob-to-regex builder converts `**` to `.*.*`, which still requires the immediately-following literal `/` to be present in the candidate path. The zero-segment case that globstar (`**`) is supposed to express — gitignore / npm-glob / extended-glob convention: `app/**/page.tsx` matches `app/page.tsx`, `app/foo/page.tsx`, AND `app/a/b/page.tsx` — silently fails for the zero-segment shape. A session that legitimately declares `produces: app/**/page.tsx` and produces a top-level `app/page.tsx` (very common in Next.js, Remix, Nuxt scaffolding) gets rc=97 from the validator with `declared deliverables missing from session diff: app/**/page.tsx`, and run-sessions.sh marks the session failed and halts the wave — even though the diff plainly contains the deliverable. Reproduced empirically against the live function: `app/**/page.tsx` rejects `app/page.tsx` (no match), and identical zero-segment failures hit `src/**/*.py` vs `src/foo.py` and `**/test.py` vs `test.py`.
bugs.root_cause: bug-141 replaced `fnmatch.fnmatchcase` with a hand-rolled regex builder so literal `[id]`-style segments (Next.js dynamic routes) survive. The builder loops over the declared pattern char-by-char with `* → .*`, `? → .`, others escaped — perfectly fine for single `*`, but globstar sees TWO consecutive `*` chars and emits `.*.*` rather than the globstar-aware `(?:.*/)?` / `(?:/.*)?/` / `(?:/.*)?` / `.*` quartet that standard glob libraries (Python `glob` with `recursive=True`, gitignore, minimatch) use. The regex `app/.*.*/page\.tsx` requires `app/` + something + `/page.tsx`, so the zero-segment match silently fails. Same audit class as bug-118 / bug-141: every dual-meaning glob meta needs to be evaluated for ALL the contexts that meta can appear in, not just the first one the test corpus covered. The bug-141 cerebrum entry called out the `[abc]` character-class trade-off explicitly but did not enumerate `**`'s zero-segment case, which is just as load-bearing for real-world `produces:` declarations.
bugs.fix: Pre-rewrite the four globstar contexts before the char loop using NUL-byte placeholders (which can't appear in real paths so collisions are impossible): `/**/` in middle → `(?:/.*)?/` (eats the leading `/`), `**/` at start → `(?:.*/)?` (zero or more directory prefix), `/**` at end → `(?:/.*)?` (zero or more directory suffix), bare `**` → `.*`. Then run the existing char loop, treating each placeholder as one-token-emit. Verified against 18 test cases including the three repro shapes (`app/**/page.tsx`, `src/**/*.py`, `**/test.py`), four negative cases that should NOT match, and six regression cases (single `*`, literal `[id]`, plain literal paths) — all pass.

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py corrupts multi-byte UTF-8 codepoints split across stdin chunk boundaries; em-dashes / smart quotes / accented chars in Claude's stream-json output (and in tool-target file paths) land in the session log and the live UI as `�` replacement chars whenever the 64KB read window happens to fall mid-codepoint.
bugs.root_cause: The reader thread does `chunk = stdin_buffer.read1(65536)` and the main loop does `line_buf += chunk.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")`. Each chunk is decoded in isolation, so a 2/3/4-byte UTF-8 sequence that starts in chunk N and finishes in chunk N+1 is processed as two separate fragments — both incomplete, both `errors="replace"` substitutes `�` for. The corrupted text then propagates through `json.loads` (still valid Unicode) into `delta.text` and into the log file, and through `parse_target` into the live-UI `target` column.
bugs.fix: Replace per-chunk `bytes.decode(...)` with `codecs.getincrementaldecoder("utf-8")(errors="replace")`. The incremental decoder buffers partial multi-byte sequences across `decode(chunk)` calls and only emits a codepoint once all its bytes have arrived; on EOF we call `decode(b"", final=True)` to surface any genuinely-truncated trailing bytes (and only those get replaced).

bugs.error_message: scripts/wolf-merge/resolve-wolf.sh passes an unquoted `$NON_WOLF` to `printf` when listing non-.wolf/ conflicts, so any conflicted path that contains whitespace or shell-glob meta (`* ? [`) is word-split and pathname-expanded against cwd before the operator sees it — fragmenting paths like `app/[id]/page.tsx` or substituting cwd-matching files for `*.tmp`-style names, hiding the real conflict in the same diagnostic the script just exited 1 to surface.
bugs.root_cause: Original code: `printf '  %s\n' $NON_WOLF >&2`. The unquoted form was deliberate — it relies on bash word-splitting to turn each newline-separated path into its own `%s` argument so they print one-per-line. But word-splitting also subjects each token to pathname expansion (when the token contains `* ? [`), and splits any path that contains literal whitespace into multiple bogus tokens. The diagnostic is the only signal the user gets before the script exits 1, so corrupting it defeats the script's own bail-out intent.
bugs.fix: Iterate via `while IFS= read -r _f; do printf '  %s\n' "$_f" >&2; done <<< "$NON_WOLF"`. read-r preserves backslashes literally and IFS= keeps leading/trailing whitespace; the quoted `"$_f"` skips both word-splitting and globbing. Same pattern auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts in lib/epic-git.sh already uses; the standalone resolver was the remaining unaudited site.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py load_sessions silently coerces `parallel_safe:` (empty value) to False via bool([]), inverting the documented default of True. Block-list children under the same key (typo where user accidentally indented sub-items) coerce to True via bool([item]) while the items are thrown away. Either way the runner serializes (or parallelizes) waves contrary to user intent with no error surfaced.
bugs.root_cause: The bug-097 guard only rejects str-typed parallel_safe values; it doesn't reject lists. parse_frontmatter assigns `[]` whenever a key column has no scalar value (either empty `key:` or `key:` followed by `  - item` rows). bool([]) is False, bool([item]) is True — both are silent coercions of malformed YAML into a boolean, exactly the failure mode the bug-097/142 guards were supposed to prevent on every fm.get-typed-value site.
bugs.fix: Add a list/dict/None guard alongside the existing isinstance-str check. Refuse anything that isn't already a bool or int (YAML 1.1 boolean whitelist + numeric 0/1 still pass). Audit class extension — every parser site that consumes a typed value must reject inputs the parser can produce but the consumer can't interpret.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py load_sessions silently miscoerces a quoted-string `depends_on: "NN"` by iterating it character-by-character: a two-digit `"12"` produces deps=[1,2] instead of either erroring or yielding [12], silently corrupting DAG topology so the session waits on the wrong parents and runs out of order. YAML 1.1 boolean spellings (`depends_on: yes`/`no`) raise TypeError but the existing message reads `got True` and never names the YAML 1.1 trap, leaving the user to guess the cause.
bugs.root_cause: load_sessions trusted `[int(d) for d in deps_raw]` to fail loudly for any non-list, but Python iterates strings char-by-char so a quoted-string scalar runs the comprehension to completion with each character as a separate dep id. Same audit class as bug-097/142/146/164 — every fm.get site that consumes a typed value must reject the shapes parse_frontmatter can produce but the consumer cannot interpret. depends_on was the unaudited site: touches has the isinstance(list) guard since forever, parallel_safe got it in bug-164, but depends_on still went straight from fm.get into the list comprehension.
bugs.fix: Reject `isinstance(deps_raw, bool)` first (mirroring bug-142/146 so the YAML 1.1 cause is named before the type-error surrogate), then `not isinstance(deps_raw, list)` with a message that distinguishes scalar-int and quoted-string from the legitimate flow-list/block-list shapes. Existing TypeError/ValueError except remains for the within-list case (e.g. `depends_on: [foo]`).

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py error-event handler corrupts the [ERROR] log line and the result-file error_detail field on two inputs the producer can legitimately emit. (1) When the event is `{"type":"error","error":null}`, `evt.get("error", {})` returns the literal None (the default fires only for ABSENT keys, not null values). The previous `str(err_data)` fallback then stringified None to the non-empty string "None", which passed the `if err_msg:` gate and wrote `[ERROR] None` to the session log AND propagated `error_detail: "None"` into the structured result.json — making the operator chase a phantom error. (2) When err_msg legitimately contains \n (multi-line CLI errors), classify_error in epic-result.sh:110 uses `grep -oE '^\[ERROR\] .+' | head -1` whose `.+` cannot cross a newline — only the first physical line ever lands in the .errtype file and from there into result.json's error_detail. Continuation lines survive in the raw exec log but disappear from the structured signal the orchestrator polls.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-148 ('every .get(key, default) site that consumes a typed value must reject inputs the parser/producer can produce but the consumer can't interpret'). The handler treated `evt.get("error", {})` as guaranteed dict-or-empty-dict, but the dict.get default doesn't fire for explicit null. The downstream `str(err_data) if not isinstance(err_data, dict) else err_data.get(...)` then accepted None, list, int — anything the producer might emit — and stringified them into the user-facing error reporting. The multi-line truncation is the symmetric defect at the consumer side: the producer wrote the whole message into the log but the line-oriented grep contract was never updated to either capture the full block or normalise at write-time.
bugs.fix: Switch to explicit type guards: accept dict (extract `message`, recheck it is a string), accept str directly, otherwise treat as no-error and skip both the log write and the status update. Then collapse multi-line err_msg to a single ` | `-separated line BEFORE writing `[ERROR] …` so classify_error's existing single-line grep contract captures the full message — same normalisation philosophy as bug-125 (slurp continuation lines into a one-line summary so result.json carries the actionable detail). Defensive against null/list/int err_data at the boundary; loud failure (no log write) is strictly better than silently corrupting error_detail.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py load_sessions silently coerces falsy non-list values for `touches` to an empty list. `touches: false`/`no`/`off` parses to bool False, `touches: 0` to int 0, `touches: ""` to empty string — all three were rewritten to `[]` by `fm.get("touches", []) or []` and then passed the isinstance(list) check. The session looked like it had no touches declared, so find_overlaps never warned about same-wave neighbours sharing the disk region the user tried to claim. Truthy non-list shapes (True from `touches: yes`, bare int, unquoted scalar) DID hit the existing isinstance error, but the message was a generic 'must be a list of globs' that did not name the YAML 1.1 boolean trap, leaving users mistyping a list as a scalar with a symptom-level error far from the cause.
bugs.root_cause: `fm.get("touches", []) or []` collapses any falsy non-list shape (False, 0, "") to `[]`, which then satisfies isinstance(list) and silently disables touches-overlap detection. The same `or []` swallows the diagnostic value of the existing isinstance guard, so the audit class identified by bug-097/142/146/164/165 (every fm.get site that consumes a typed value must reject inputs the parser can produce but the consumer can't interpret) was incomplete — `touches` was the next unaudited site after `parallel_safe` (bug-164) and `depends_on` (bug-165).
bugs.fix: Drop the `or []` fallback. Read raw via `fm.get("touches", [])`; reject bool first with a YAML 1.1 boolean-trap message; reject any other non-list shape with a flow-list/block-list remediation; otherwise accept the list. Mirrors the bug-164/165 pattern — bool case first (most common cause), then generic shape error.

bugs.error_message: validate-session-deliverables.py silently treats `produces: false`/`no`/`off`/`0`/`""` as 'no produces declared' and falls through to the metadata-only heuristic. A session whose author meant to declare deliverables but typed a scalar instead of a list bypasses the declared-deliverables validation entirely — the session passes if it touched any non-metadata path, defeating the whole point of `produces:`. Truthy non-list shapes hit the existing isinstance error but with a one-liner ('must be a list of paths or fnmatch globs') that did not name the YAML 1.1 boolean trap or suggest `skip_deliverables_check: true` for the legitimate opt-out case.
bugs.root_cause: `fm.get("produces", []) or []` collapses any falsy non-list shape to `[]`, which then makes `if produces:` at line 247 false, skipping declared-deliverables validation and routing through `_is_metadata_only`. Same audit class as bug-170 (which fixed the symmetric bug for `touches:` in epic-dag.py); this is the matching site in the validator that reads from epic-dag.py's parse_frontmatter.
bugs.fix: Drop the `or []` fallback. Read raw via `fm.get("produces", [])`; reject bool first with a YAML 1.1 boolean-trap message that points at `skip_deliverables_check: true` for the legitimate opt-out path; reject any other non-list shape; otherwise accept the list. Mirrors bug-170's pattern in epic-dag.py.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py load_sessions silently accepted non-string shapes for `model:` (the per-session model override). Two concrete shapes the bool-only str-key guard at line 595-603 missed: (1) `model: 5` — parse_frontmatter's int branch (line 371) coerces any bare-numeric scalar to `int`, the existing `model_raw or ""` left it alone, the regex pre-check at line 620 ran str(5)="5" through `[A-Za-z0-9._/+:-]+` which matches, the int landed in the SESSION row, run_cli passed `--model 5` to claude, and claude rejected the unknown model id with an opaque error far from the YAML cause; (2) `model:` empty followed by indented `- sonnet` items — parse_frontmatter assigns `[]` for an empty value column then appends the block-list items, so `model_raw = ['sonnet']`, `str(['sonnet'])` survived the str() coercion but failed the regex on the `[`/`]`/quote characters, surfacing 'contains characters that break the SESSION row's space-delimited format' — a symptom-level error pointing at the SERIALISER when the real cause was the user wrote a list, not a scalar. Same audit class as bug-097/142/146/164/165/170/171.
bugs.root_cause: The string-typed-fields guard at line 595-603 only rejected bool. Every other shape parse_frontmatter can emit (int from numeric scalar, list from empty key with indented sub-items) fell through to the downstream regex check at line 620, which surfaced a misleading 'characters that break the SESSION row' message regardless of whether the user wrote bad characters in a real string OR wrote the wrong shape entirely. Same defect class as the recently-audited fm.get sites for `touches:` (bug-170) and `produces:` (bug-171): every consumer must reject inputs the parser can produce but the consumer can't interpret, AND the diagnostic must name the parser cause (YAML 1.1 boolean trap, list-vs-scalar typo) so the user can fix the YAML, not the symptom.
bugs.fix: Extend the string-typed-fields loop to reject non-string types after the existing bool branch: continue on None (absent key), raise on bool with the existing YAML 1.1 message, then raise on non-str with a generic shape error that names the two concrete remediation paths (empty key with indented children → remove children; numeric scalar → quote it). The downstream `cli_raw or ""` and `model_raw or ""` patterns are now defence-in-depth — only strings can reach them — but kept for safety. Mirrors the audit-class pattern from bug-164/170/171.

bugs.error_message: epic-dag.py load_sessions silently accepted non-string shapes for `cli:` (the per-session CLI override). Two concrete shapes the bool-only str-key guard at line 595-603 missed: (1) `cli: 0` (or any falsy non-bool int) — parse_frontmatter's int branch coerces it to `0`, then `cli_raw = fm.get("cli", "") or ""` collapses `0 or ""` to `""`, falling silently through to the auto-detect path with the user's explicit (mis-typed) value vanished without diagnostic; (2) `cli:` empty followed by indented `- claude` — parse_frontmatter assigns `['claude']`, the value-set check at line 605 fired with 'cli must be claude or opencode, got ['claude']' but never named the list-vs-scalar cause. The bare empty `cli:` (no children) collapsed to `[]` then `[] or ""` to `""` — same silent auto-detect coercion as the int 0 case. Same audit class as bug-097/142/146/164/165/170/171/172.
bugs.root_cause: Same defect as bug-172 in the symmetric site: the string-typed-fields guard at line 595-603 only rejected bool, leaving int and list shapes to flow through `cli_raw = fm.get("cli", "") or ""` (which silently coerces every falsy non-string to `""`) and the value-set check (which only describes WHICH literal cli was wrong, not WHY the user's typed shape can't be one). Both branches lost or buried the YAML cause.
bugs.fix: Same loop extension as bug-172 covers cli too — the `for _str_key in ("title", "model", "cli")` loop now rejects every non-string shape with a YAML-aware message before the downstream value-set or regex check can run. After the loop, only strings (or absent → None) reach `cli_raw`, so the `or ""` is defence-in-depth. Mirrors bug-164/170/171.

bugs.error_message: validate-session-deliverables.py:229 silently ignored every shape parse_frontmatter can emit for `skip_deliverables_check:` that is NOT strictly bool True. The user typed an opt-out, the parser produced something the consumer couldn't interpret, the consumer fell through to the validation run — which then most likely FAILED the session because kickoff/docs-only sessions don't declare `produces:` and so hit the metadata-only heuristic. The user saw 'session committed only metadata/handoff files — no real deliverables' AFTER they had explicitly opted out, with no diagnostic naming the YAML cause. Concrete silent-ignore shapes: (1) `skip_deliverables_check: 1` — parse_frontmatter's int branch yields int 1; `1 is True` is False (Python: `1 == True` but NOT `1 is True`) → silent run. (2) `skip_deliverables_check: "true"` (quoted) — _unquote_scalar strips the quotes BUT only AFTER the YAML 1.1 boolean check, which keys on the unstripped raw v.lower() ∈ {true,yes,...}; the quoted `"true"` is not in that whitelist (it includes the quotes), falls through to the string branch, lands as the string "true", and `"true" is True` is False → silent run. Same for `"yes"`, `"on"`. (3) `skip_deliverables_check:` empty + indented `- true` block items — parse_frontmatter assigns `[]` for the empty value column then appends the block-list items, yielding `["true"]`; `["true"] is True` is False → silent run, with the user's items thrown away. (4) `skip_deliverables_check:` empty alone → `[]`; `[] is True` is False → silent run.
bugs.root_cause: The strict `is True` comparison rejects every non-bool truthy value the YAML parser can produce. parse_frontmatter emits int, str, list, and bool shapes; only bool True passes, so int 1 / str "true" / quoted "yes" / list shapes from typo'd indented children all silently fall through to the validation run as if the user never wrote the opt-out. Same audit class as bug-097/142/146/164/165/167/170/171/172/173: every .get(key, default)-style consumer that interprets a typed value must reject inputs the parser can produce but the consumer can't interpret, AND the diagnostic must name the parser cause so the user fixes the YAML, not the symptom. This was the last unaudited consumer site for the session-frontmatter shape — bug-170/171 fixed `touches:`/`produces:`, bug-172/173 fixed `model:`/`cli:`, bug-164 fixed `parallel_safe`, bug-165 fixed `depends_on`. `skip_deliverables_check` was the remaining strict-bool consumer left without a non-bool guard.
bugs.fix: Read raw via `fm.get("skip_deliverables_check")` (no default → None for absent). If non-None and not bool, print a YAML-aware error naming the int / quoted-string / indented-children remediations (use unquoted YAML 1.1 spellings — true/yes/on — to opt out, or omit the key / set false to run validation) and return rc=2. After the guard, `is True` is now exhaustive: only bool True triggers the opt-out path, bool False / absent fall through to validation as documented. Mirrors the bug-170/171 pattern — name the YAML 1.1 cause first, then accept the legitimate shape.

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py crashed with uncaught AttributeError on multiple producer-emittable JSON shapes. (1) `evt = json.loads(line)` succeeds for any valid JSON value, not just objects — `null` → None, `"str"` → str, `[…]` → list, `123` → int. The downstream `evt.get("type", "")` raises AttributeError on every non-dict, propagating out of the reader thread and breaking the producer pipe with SIGPIPE. claude/opencode then exits 141 and classify_error reports `cli_crash` instead of the real producer-protocol cause — the operator chases a phantom CLI failure when the real bug is a single bare `null` line in the stream. (2) Symmetric defect at four nested sites that all use the `evt.get(KEY, {})` pattern: `evt.get("content_block", {})` for content_block_start, `evt.get("delta", {})` for content_block_delta, `evt.get("part", {})` for OpenCode tool_use, `evt.get("part", {})` for OpenCode text. dict.get's default ONLY fires for ABSENT keys, not null values; if the producer emits `"content_block": null` / `"delta": null` / `"part": null`, the .get returns the literal None and the next `.get(...)` on it raises AttributeError — same crash chain.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-167: every `.get(key, default)` site that consumes a typed value must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret. The bug-167 fix patched the `evt.get("error", {})` site with an isinstance(dict) guard but missed every other `evt.get(KEY, {})` site that follows the same pattern, AND missed the symmetric guard at the OUTER event boundary (the json.loads result itself, before any .get call). Each of these is a one-line crash waiting for a producer to send a malformed/null value — common during version skew, transient stream-json bugs, or when an upstream framework wraps an error response in a null payload.
bugs.fix: Add isinstance(dict) guards at all five sites: (a) immediately after json.loads, `if not isinstance(evt, dict): continue` so the rest of the parsing assumes a dict; (b) drop the `{}` defaults from `evt.get("content_block")`, `evt.get("delta")`, and the two `evt.get("part")` calls, then guard each with `isinstance(..., dict)` before the next `.get(...)`. Mirrors the bug-167 fix pattern. Defensive against null/non-dict shapes at every boundary the producer crosses; the alternative — letting the reader thread crash and the pipe break — silently misclassifies the failure mode.

bugs.error_message: Missing error handling in unknown
bugs.root_cause: Code path had no error handling — exceptions would propagate uncaught
bugs.fix: Added try/catch block

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py crashes the reader main loop with TypeError: can only concatenate str (not 'NoneType') to str whenever the producer emits a Claude input_json_delta with `"partial_json": null`. dict.get's default fires only for ABSENT keys, not null values, so `delta.get("partial_json", "")` lands None and `input_buf += None` raises immediately. Truthy non-str shapes (`5`, `["x"]`, `{}`) crash the same way. The exception propagates out of the main loop, breaks the producer pipe with SIGPIPE, the AI CLI exits 141 and classify_error reports the surrogate `cli_crash` instead of the real producer-protocol cause.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-167/175/176: every consumer of a typed value must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret. The bug-176 audit added isinstance(dict) guards at the OUTER dict-shaped values (evt, content_block, delta, part) but never extended to the INNER scalar fields. `partial_json` is one of those scalar fields — the value is concatenated directly into `input_buf` without any shape check, and any non-str value crashes the str-concat.
bugs.fix: Read the value into a local, gate the `input_buf +=` on `isinstance(pj, str)`, and unconditionally re-call parse_target so the cached target stays correct. Comment names the producer-null cause and points at the full audit chain (bug-175/176/178). Verified by reproducing the original TypeError on the pre-patch tree against null/unhashable/int payloads, then confirming all five repro cases exit rc=0 on the patched tree.

bugs.error_message: Symmetric defect to bug-178 at two text-event sites: Claude `text_delta` with `"text": null` lands None and `log_file.write(None)` raises TypeError ('write() argument must be str, not NoneType'); OpenCode `text` events use the existing `if text:` guard which catches None and "" but allows truthy non-str (`5`, `["x"]`, `{}`) to slip through and crash log_file.write the same way. Both crash chains break the producer pipe with SIGPIPE and corrupt classify_error's verdict to `cli_crash`.
bugs.root_cause: Same scalar-shape audit gap as bug-178 — `delta.get("text", "")` and `part.get("text", "")` both feed log_file.write directly. The Claude branch had no shape guard at all; the OpenCode branch had a truthy guard that catches None but not non-str truthy values. Producer protocol error → consumer crash → SIGPIPE → cli_crash misclassification, identical to bug-167/175/176/178.
bugs.fix: On the Claude side gate the write/flush AND the activity transition on `isinstance(text, str)`. On the OpenCode side replace `if text:` with `if isinstance(text, str) and text:` so the same shape rejection applies. Repro suite covers `text: null` (Claude) and `text: 5` (OpenCode); both crash on the pre-patch tree and pass on the patched tree.

bugs.error_message: epic-progress.py crashes with `TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'` when the producer emits a tool name as a list or dict. Two sites: Claude `content_block_start.name` flows into `parse_target(current_tool, …)` → `patterns.get(current_tool)` (dict.get with an unhashable key); OpenCode `tool_use.part.tool` flows directly into `OPENCODE_TOOL_ALIASES.get(raw_tool, raw_tool)` and then `field_map.get(tool_name, "")` inside parse_opencode_target. Either crash propagates out of the main loop, breaks the pipe with SIGPIPE, and corrupts classify_error to cli_crash.
bugs.root_cause: Same scalar-shape audit gap as bug-178/179: `cb.get("name", "?")` and `part.get("tool", "?")` are typed-value consumers that never validated the shape of the value they received. Hashable non-str values (None, int) survive intact and merely produce ugly UI; unhashable shapes (list, dict) crash the next dict.get key-lookup. Same producer-protocol-error → consumer-crash → cli_crash chain as the rest of the audit class.
bugs.fix: Coerce non-str at the boundary in both events: `current_tool = raw_name if isinstance(raw_name, str) else "?"` for Claude, and `if not isinstance(raw_tool, str): raw_tool = "?"` before the OPENCODE_TOOL_ALIASES.get call for OpenCode. Downstream code stays str-safe and malformed events degrade to the existing `?` sentinel instead of crashing the reader. Repro suite verified.

bugs.error_message: Per-session and global model selection silently corrupted on `--cli opencode` whenever the model name is not in the small known-shorthand whitelist (sonnet/opus/haiku/gpt5/gpt5nano/gemini/glm/sonnet4). `map_model_shorthand`'s default-case branch calls `log "Model '<name>' is not a known OpenCode shorthand — using as-is"; echo "$model"`, but `log` is `echo -e "${BLUE}[epic]${NC} $*"` which writes to stdout — and every call site captures stdout via `$(...)` (run-sessions.sh:288 global, epic-session.sh:174 per-session model override, epic-session.sh:184 per-session cli-flip remap). The captured value becomes the ANSI-coloured `\e[0;34m[epic]\e[0m Model 'foo' is not a known OpenCode shorthand — using as-is\nfoo` (109 bytes for `claude-sonnet-4-5` instead of 17), flowed verbatim into `model_flag=(-m "$session_model")` / `(--model "$session_model")` at epic-session.sh:208/210. opencode/claude rejects the multi-line argument with an opaque 'unknown model id' error far from the YAML / CLI cause — and on Bash 3.2 macOS `echo -e` may even emit the literal `-e` flag too. Fires for any non-shorthand model name passed as a fully-qualified-but-slashless string (e.g. `--model claude-sonnet-4-5`, `--model bedrock-claude`, any custom or newer model id) and for any session-frontmatter `model:` override that isn't in the case list.
bugs.root_cause: Captured-stdout discipline violation: `map_model_shorthand`'s contract is to return ONLY the mapped model string on stdout because every call site is `var=$(map_model_shorthand …)`. The function nevertheless calls `log` (which emits to stdout, not stderr — only `err` redirects to >&2) for the unknown-model diagnostic, polluting the captured value. Same audit class as the bug-167/175/176/178/179/180 producer-consumer protocol audits, but applied to the SHELL function-return contract instead of the JSON-event contract: every helper consumed via `$(...)` must reserve stdout for the canonical return value and route diagnostics to stderr. The other captured helpers in the codebase (`_realpath`, `extract_prompt`, `build_handoff_section`, `find_handoff_for`, `format_elapsed`, `classify_error`) all already obey this and only echo their canonical output — `map_model_shorthand` was the lone violator, missed because the unknown-model branch is silent on success-path runs.
bugs.fix: Append `>&2` to the log call so the diagnostic prints to the user's terminal but does not contaminate the captured value: `log "Model '$model' is not a known OpenCode shorthand — using as-is" >&2; echo "$model"`. Verified by reproducer: captured length drops from 109 bytes (ANSI-coloured log message + model) to 17 bytes (just `claude-sonnet-4-5`); stderr diagnostic remains visible to the user.

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py crashes the OpenWolf JSON merge driver and records .wolf/buglog.json as unresolvable on six malformed-but-parseable shapes the bug-148 `or []` guard didn't reject. Repro grid (all crashed pre-patch, all clean post-patch): (1) `"bugs": {"a": 1}` (truthy non-list dict) — `_bugs_of` returns the dict via `or []`, `for bug in <dict>` iterates KEYS as strings, `bug.get("id")` raises `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'get'`; (2) `"bugs": "malformed"` — same chain via character iteration; (3) `"bugs": 5` — `for bug in 5` raises `TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable`; (4) `"bugs": [42, {...}]` — list iteration is fine but `42.get("id")` raises AttributeError on the first non-dict item; (5) `"bugs": ["oops", {...}]` — same crash on the string item; (6) `"bugs": [{"id": ["list-id"], ...}]` — passes the dict-item filter but `used_ids = {bug.get("id") ...}` tries to add a list to a set, raising `TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'`, with the same chain at `by_id[bid]` (dict subscript with unhashable key) and the final `sorted(..., key=lambda b: b.get("id", ""))` (Python 3 TypeError comparing list and str). Every chain ends the same way: driver exits 1, git records the file as unresolvable, the user sees a `.wolf/buglog.json` merge conflict the auto-merge driver was supposed to silently handle.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-148/154/167/175/176 — every consumer of `.get(key, default)` must reject inputs the parser/producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret. The bug-148 fix used `src.get("bugs") or []` which catches `None` and missing key but NOT truthy non-list shapes (dict, str, int) — `or []` only fires on falsy values, so a truthy malformed value passes through verbatim and crashes the next-stage iteration. Symmetric to bug-176 in epic-progress.py where the bug-167 fix had patched only the `evt.get("error")` site and missed every other `evt.get(KEY, {})` site plus the outer `isinstance(evt, dict)` guard at the json.loads boundary. The id-collection sites (`used_ids`, `ours_by_id`, `theirs_by_id`, `by_id[bid]`) compounded the issue: `bug.get("id")` on a non-dict bug crashes, and `bug.get("id")` returning a non-string non-empty value (list/dict/int) makes the id unhashable as a set member or dict key. Schema drift / partially-flushed writes / hand-edits / a buggy hook are all realistic producers — the merge driver exists precisely to be robust against parallel-write divergence, so it must be robust against the schema variations those parallel writes can produce.
bugs.fix: Rewrite `_bugs_of` to require an outer `isinstance(src, dict)` guard, an inner `isinstance(bugs, list)` guard, and a list-comprehension filter `[b for b in bugs if isinstance(b, dict)]` so iteration always yields dicts. Add a new `_bug_id(bug) -> str` helper that returns the id only when it is a non-empty string, else '' — funnels malformed ids (None, list, dict, int, empty string) through the same fresh-id rescue path as missing ids (bug-105) instead of crashing the driver, and prevents unhashable values from ever reaching `set.add` / `dict[key]`. Replace every `bug.get("id")` callsite with `_bug_id(bug)`: ancestor_occ collection (line 96), ours_by_id/theirs_by_id dict comprehensions (line 117-118), used_ids set comprehension (line 124), and the main merge loop's bid extraction (line 133). Verified by reproducing each TypeError/AttributeError on the pre-patch tree (6 crash cases) and confirming all 6 cases return clean merged dicts on the patched tree, with the bug-098/105/137/148 happy-path tests still passing (occurrences=ours+theirs-ancestor=9, id-collision-different-sig renumbers to bug-002, missing-id rescue allocates bug-101).

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py merge_buglog crashes the OpenWolf JSON merge driver with `TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'str'` (and symmetric variants for list/int/dict) when two parallel sessions both touched the same bug-id and the duplicate-bug branch reaches `bug.get("last_seen", "") > existing.get("last_seen", "")`. dict.get's default fires only for ABSENT keys, not null values: a producer that emits `"last_seen": null` (partial-flush by a SIGKILL'd hook, hand-edit, schema-drift artefact, buggy producer) lands None, and `None > "..."` raises TypeError in Python 3. Truthy non-str shapes (`"last_seen": 5`, `["x"]`, `{"a":1}`) raise the same TypeError on the heterogeneous compare. Repro: ours='2026-01-01' / theirs=None on a true-duplicate signature — driver exits 1, git records `.wolf/buglog.json` as unresolvable, the user sees the same merge-conflict failure mode bug-148/182 went out of their way to silence at the OUTER bugs/id level.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-148/154/167/175/176/178/179/180/182 — every consumer of `.get(key, default)` must reject inputs the parser/producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret. Bug-182 patched `_bugs_of` and `_bug_id` to gate non-conforming OUTER shapes (the `bugs` value, the `id` field on each bug item) but stopped there; the INNER scalar fields the merge loop consumes (`last_seen` for the newer-wins tie-break, `occurrences` for occurrence accumulation — see bug-184) were left trusting the producer to send well-formed scalars. dict.get's default-firing-only-on-absent-keys semantics is the same trap that bit bug-167 (`evt.get("error", {})`), bug-175 (`evt.get("content_block", {})` etc.), bug-178 (`delta.get("partial_json", "")`) and bug-179 (`delta.get("text", "")`): a `null` value silently passes through the default and the next stage crashes on the type mismatch.
bugs.fix: Add a `_last_seen(bug) -> str` helper mirroring `_bug_id`'s shape-coercing pattern: returns the value only when it is a string, else `''`. Coerced empties still compare correctly via lexicographic ordering on strings only — the malformed entry simply never wins the newer-wins tie-break, and the well-formed side's metadata is kept (analogous to bug-182's malformed-id falling through to extras with a fresh id rather than crashing). Replace `bug.get("last_seen", "") > existing.get("last_seen", "")` with `_last_seen(bug) > _last_seen(existing)`. Verified against 4 repro shapes (None, list, int, dict) plus the bug-098 happy-path that exercises the tie-break with two valid string timestamps — the well-formed side wins on every malformed input, the bug-098 case still selects '2026-01-03' over '2026-01-02'.

bugs.error_message: merge-wolf-json.py merge_buglog crashes the OpenWolf JSON merge driver with `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for -: 'str' and 'int'` (and symmetric `+` / `<` / `min` variants for list/dict/str shapes) at the duplicate-bug occurrence-accumulation site `summed = max(1, ours_occ + theirs_occ - base_occ)` and at the ancestor-fallback `min(ours_occ, theirs_occ)` (bug-154 path when ancestor was unreadable). The previous `bug.get("occurrences", 1) or 1` guard catches None and 0 (falsy → 1) but the `or 1` short-circuit on the FIRST truthy value keeps any truthy non-int verbatim: `"occurrences": "5"` (truthy str) lets `"5" + "3" = "53"` (silent str concat) which then crashes on `"53" - 0`; `"occurrences": [1, 2]` (truthy list) lets `[1] + [2] = [1, 2]` (list concat) which then crashes on `[1, 2] - 0`; `min([1, 2], 3)` crashes the bug-154 fallback path with TypeError on the heterogeneous compare. All chains end the same way as bug-148/182/183: driver exits 1, git records `.wolf/buglog.json` as unresolvable, the user sees a merge conflict the auto-merge driver was supposed to handle silently.
bugs.root_cause: Symmetric to bug-183 — same audit class (bug-148/154/167/175/176/178/179/180/182) extended to the second INNER scalar field. `or 1` only handles falsy values; truthy malformed shapes pass through unchanged and the next-stage arithmetic crashes. Three distinct call sites consume `occurrences`: the ancestor_occ population path (line ~147), the bug-154 ancestor-unreadable fallback (line ~169-170), and the duplicate-bug accumulation (line ~208-209). All three were vulnerable; a malformed value at any one site corrupts the merge.
bugs.fix: Add a `_occurrences(bug) -> int` helper that returns a positive int (>=1, defaulting to 1). Rejects `bool` explicitly because `bool` is an `int` subclass that `isinstance(True, int)` would otherwise accept (we don't want `True` to count as 1 — a producer mistake should surface as a clean default, not silently pretend to be valid data). Clamps to `>= 1` so the existing `summed = max(1, ours_occ + theirs_occ - base_occ)` invariant downstream is preserved by construction. Replace all four call sites: ancestor_occ population (line ~147), bug-154 fallback's two `.get("occurrences", 1) or 1` reads (line ~169-170), and the duplicate-bug branch's two reads (line ~208-209). Verified against 3 repro shapes (str, list, mixed-with-ancestor) — the malformed side coerces to 1, well-formed-side accumulation arithmetic produces the expected counts (e.g. ours=5, theirs=3, ancestor='2'→1 → summed=max(1, 5+3-1)=7), and the bug-098 happy-path (ours=4, theirs=5, ancestor=3 → summed=6) is unchanged.

bugs.error_message: epic-ui.py live dashboard thread crashes with `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get'` whenever a status-file session entry lands the literal None — the dict.get default fires only for ABSENT keys, not null values, so a status file with `"sessions": {"1": null}` (hand-edit, schema drift from a future writer that emitted partials, a buggy out-of-band hook overwriting the file outside the lock protocol) returns None at _get's `self.status.get(str(sid), {})`, and the next `d.get('status', 'pending')` raises AttributeError — the dashboard's _build_lines() / _redraw() crashes mid-render, the main loop dies, the live progress display goes dead, and the user loses visibility into running waves until they re-launch the runner. Symmetric site `_print_changes` (non-TTY mode iterating self.status.items() + st_data.get('status', 'pending')) crashes the same way on the same trigger. Outer-level `load_status` has the same defect: `json.load(f).get('sessions', {})` returns the literal None when the file has `"sessions": null` (key exists, value is null); a non-dict return type then breaks every downstream caller.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-167/175/178/180/183/184 extended to the status-file consumer in epic-ui.py. dict.get(KEY, default) only returns the default for ABSENT keys, NOT for keys with a null value (or any non-dict value). load_status's `.get('sessions', {})`, _get's `self.status.get(str(sid), {})`, and _print_changes's `st_data.get('status', 'pending')` all assumed the producer would only emit dict shapes; under any producer-emitted null/non-dict, the next attribute-style access raises AttributeError and kills the UI thread.
bugs.fix: Three coercion guards mirroring the bug-176/180 INNER-vs-OUTER pattern: load_status coerces non-dict sessions to {} before returning so downstream callers can trust the type; _get coerces non-dict d to {} so per-field defaults below the guard apply cleanly; _print_changes skips non-dict st_data so the iteration continues past corrupt entries instead of dying mid-tick. Verified by reproducing all three crash paths on the pre-patch tree (sessions=null at outer, sessions[1]=null at inner, sessions=[list] at outer) and confirming all three return clean defaults / skip cleanly on the patched tree, with well-formed entries still routed through the original render path.

bugs.error_message: mark_session (epic-session.sh Python heredoc) and update_status_file (epic-progress.py) crash with AttributeError / TypeError on producer-emitted null shapes the consumer can't interpret. mark_session line 318 `entry = data['sessions'].setdefault(sid, {})` fails three ways: (a) data is non-dict (file is `null`/list/str): `data['sessions']` raises TypeError on subscript; (b) data['sessions'] is None or non-dict: `.setdefault(...)` raises AttributeError; (c) data['sessions'][sid] is null: `setdefault(sid, {})` returns None (default fires only for ABSENT keys, not null values), then `entry['status'] = st` raises TypeError ('NoneType does not support item assignment'). The mark_session heredoc has no try/except around the body, so any of these propagate out, the Python exits non-zero, and the shell side doesn't check the exit code — the status update is silently dropped, the corrupted file is left intact, and every subsequent mark_session call fails the same way until manual repair. update_status_file's symmetric chain `data.setdefault('sessions', {}).setdefault(key, {}).update(update)` has identical failure modes; its outer `except Exception: pass` swallows the crash but the broken state persists in the file and every subsequent update call fails the same way — the user loses live progress for the rest of the run with no diagnostic.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-167/175/178/180/183/184/185 extended to the status-file WRITERS. Every consumer of `.get(key, default)` AND `.setdefault(key, default)` must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret. setdefault has the same default-fires-only-for-absent-keys semantics as get — a present-but-null key returns the literal None, and the next attribute-style access (`.setdefault` chain, `.update`, item assignment) raises. The previous fixes audited the OUTER container shapes (data being a dict, sessions being a dict via setdefault) but stopped there; the INNER session entry was left trusting the producer to emit a dict at every key. Producer scenarios: hand-edit (rare), schema drift across toolkit versions, a future buggy writer that emits null in a partial-flush, or a user hook that touches the status file outside the lock+os.replace protocol.
bugs.fix: Three-level isinstance(dict) coercion at every read site, mirroring the bug-148/154/182 _bugs_of pattern: `isinstance(data, dict)` (rebuild data={} if file root is non-dict), `isinstance(sessions_obj, dict)` (rebuild and re-attach if data['sessions'] is null/non-dict), `isinstance(entry, dict)` (rebuild and re-attach if sessions[sid] is null/non-dict). Apply at both mark_session's heredoc and update_status_file. The writer rebuilds the missing structure rather than crashing on it, the next os.replace makes the file well-formed again, and downstream readers see the recovered shape. Verified by reproducing all crash chains on the pre-patch tree (data=null, sessions=null, sessions[sid]=null, all four combinations across both consumers) and confirming all return rc=0 with the malformed shape transparently rebuilt; well-formed input is preserved (fields not in the update payload, like slug, are kept).

bugs.error_message: Missing error handling in _normalize_entry
bugs.root_cause: Code path had no error handling — exceptions would propagate uncaught
bugs.fix: Added try/catch block

bugs.error_message: epic-ui.py live dashboard render thread crashes mid-tick on producer-emitted non-conforming INNER scalar fields in the per-session status entry. Five distinct TypeError/ValueError chains, all triggered by `dict.get(key, default)` returning a null/non-conforming value (the default fires only for ABSENT keys, not for null/list/dict/str values): (a) `"elapsed": null` lands None at `_get`'s `d.get('elapsed', 0.0)`; the renderer then calls `fmt_elapsed(None)` which evaluates `int(None)` and raises `TypeError: int() argument must be a string, a bytes-like object or a real number, not 'NoneType'`; the same chain fires for `"elapsed": "abc"` (ValueError on int) and `"elapsed": [1,2]` (TypeError); kills `_build_lines()` / `_redraw()`. (b) `"step": null` → `f'{None:2d}'` raises TypeError on the format spec; `"step": "abc"` → ValueError 'Unknown format code d for object of type str'. (c) `"tool": [1,2]` (truthy non-str) passes the `tool or ''` falsy-only guard, then `f'{tool:<10}'` raises `TypeError: unsupported format string passed to list.__format__`. (d) The `_print_changes` non-TTY path hits the same `"elapsed": "abc"` chain at `fmt_elapsed(st_data.get('elapsed', 0))`. Every chain propagates out of the UI thread's main loop unhandled, the dashboard goes dead, and the user loses live progress for the rest of the run until they re-launch the runner.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-167/175/178/179/180/183/184/185/186 — every `.get(key, default)`-style consumer must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret. Bug-185 patched the OUTER container shapes (file root non-dict, sessions container non-dict, single session entry non-dict) but stopped there; the INNER scalar fields (`elapsed`, `step`, `tool`, `target`) on each session entry were left trusting the producer to send well-formed scalars. This is exactly the bug-176 → bug-180 / bug-182 → bug-183/184 progression in epic-progress.py and merge-wolf-json.py respectively, now extended to the live UI's read side. Producer scenarios: hand-edit (rare), schema drift across toolkit versions (a future writer that emits null in a partial-flush before the lock+os.replace path was added), a downgrade from a future version with a different scalar shape, or a buggy out-of-band hook that touches the status file outside the lock protocol. The fact that mark_session and update_status_file always write strict types under happy-path is irrelevant to the audit: the consumer must defend against any producer-emittable shape, since the file is shared protocol state.
bugs.fix: Add module-level helper `_normalize_entry(d) -> dict` that coerces every scalar field to its expected type using isinstance() guards: `status` to str-or-'pending', `step` to int-or-0 (rejecting bool explicitly because bool is an int subclass that isinstance(True, int) would otherwise accept and silently pretend was 1/0), `tool`/`target` to str-or-'', `elapsed` to (int|float)-or-0.0 (also rejecting bool). Funnel both `_get` (TTY render path) and `_print_changes` (non-TTY append path) through the helper so every malformed field degrades to its happy-path default rather than crashing the tick. Verified by reproducing all six crash chains on the pre-patch tree (elapsed=null/str, step=null/str, tool=list, plus the non-TTY elapsed=str repro) and confirming all six render OK on the patched tree, with the well-formed happy-path (status='done', step=12, tool='Edit', target='src/foo.py', elapsed=45.2) still passing through unchanged.

bugs.error_message: epic-ui.py live dashboard render thread crashes mid-tick on producer-emitted non-finite float (NaN / +Inf / -Inf) in the per-session `elapsed` field of the status JSON entry
bugs.root_cause: _normalize_entry's `isinstance(elapsed, (int, float))` check accepts NaN and ±Inf because both are valid floats (`isinstance(float('nan'), float) is True`), so the bug-188 inner-scalar audit's existing `bool`/`int`/`float`/`str` shape rejection never fires for the non-finite subspace. fmt_elapsed then calls `int(secs)` on the non-finite value: `int(NaN)` raises `ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer`, `int(±Inf)` raises `OverflowError: cannot convert float infinity to integer`. Either kills `_build_lines()` / `_redraw()` and the live UI thread; the dashboard goes dead for the rest of the run with no diagnostic. Python's `json.load` accepts the non-standard `NaN`/`Infinity`/`-Infinity` tokens by default (parse_constant=None) and `json.dump` emits them by default (allow_nan=True), so update_status_file and mark_session round-trip them verbatim — once a producer (hand-edit, schema drift from a future writer that emits non-finite from buggy timer arithmetic, out-of-band hook with wall-clock skew) lands NaN/Inf in the status file, every subsequent UI tick crashes until manual repair. Same audit class as bug-185/186/188 — every consumer of `dict.get(key, default)` must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret, and that audit must extend to every type-narrowing predicate the consumer applies, not just to the outer container shapes.
bugs.fix: Extend the bug-188 _normalize_entry guard to also reject non-finite floats: `if isinstance(elapsed, bool) or not isinstance(elapsed, (int, float)) or not math.isfinite(elapsed): elapsed = 0.0`. Add `import math` to the existing imports. Order of clauses matters: bool is rejected first (since `bool` is an int subclass and `math.isfinite(True)` is True, which would let `True` slip through if checked last); type-narrowing runs second so the `math.isfinite` call only ever sees an int or float (avoiding `TypeError: must be real number, not NoneType` on non-numeric shapes); finite check runs last on the now-safe value. Verified by reproducing the original ValueError/OverflowError chains on NaN/Inf/-Inf inputs and confirming all three (plus bool/None/str/list shapes from bug-188) coerce to elapsed=0.0 / `  0s` rendering on the patched tree, with the well-formed happy-path inputs (45.2 → 45s, 5 → 5s, 0 → 0s) still passing through unchanged.
