bugs.error_message: epic-ui.py parse_bash_plan and run-sessions.sh status-init heredoc both crash with unhandled IndexError/ValueError when the shared `.epic-dag-plan.bash` file contains a malformed WAVE or SESSION line — non-numeric wave/id field, or a SESSION row truncated below the wave-id pair (still passes the bug-188 `len(parts) < 6` guard whenever exactly five fields land followed by a numeric-looking sixth that breaks the int() narrowing). Three concrete crash chains, all silent except for the runtime impact: (a) parse_bash_plan's `wn = int(parts[1])` for `WAVE garbage` → ValueError, propagates out of EpicUI's __init__ via main(), the live dashboard never launches and the user loses live progress visibility for the entire run with no diagnostic. (b) parse_bash_plan's `wn = int(parts[1])` / `sid = int(parts[2])` for `SESSION garbage 1 ...` / `SESSION 1 abc ...` → same ValueError chain, same dashboard-never-launches symptom. (c) run-sessions.sh's status-init heredoc (line 825) does the same `int(wn)` on parts[1] AND tuple-unpacks parts[1..5] without a length guard. Producer-emittable corruption modes are the same cross-process protocol shapes bug-185/186 anticipate for the status JSON, now applied to the plan file: a partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write, an NFS read racing the write, schema drift from a future writer, or a hand-edit for debugging. The shell side does not check the heredoc's exit code, so the init failure leaves STATUS_FILE empty/missing — every subsequent mark_session/update_status_file call sees the missing file (epic-progress.py:35: `if not path or not os.path.isfile(path): return`; epic-session.sh:296: `[[ -z "$sf" || ! -f "$sf" ]] && return 0`) and silently no-ops, so no session ever transitions out of 'pending' in the status file even after completion. The dashboard reads pending-forever and the append-only Claude Code path never prints any state-change line, defeating the live progress feature for the whole run.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-185/186/188/189 — every consumer of producer-emitted data must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret, and the audit must extend beyond just `dict.get(key, default)` consumers to ANY narrowing predicate the consumer applies (here: `int(parts[i])` is a string→int narrowing that crashes on every shape outside the numeric subspace). The bug-188 `if len(parts) < 6: continue` guard handled the truncation case at the OUTER container shape but stopped there; the INNER scalar fields the parser then passes to int() were left trusting the producer to emit numeric strings. This is the exact bug-176 → bug-180 / bug-185 → bug-188 / bug-188 → bug-189 progression in the status-file consumers, now extended to the plan-file consumers — the third producer-consumer protocol in the codebase. The dual call sites also matter: epic-ui.py and the run-sessions.sh init heredoc read the same file, so a single corrupted line poisons BOTH the dashboard launch AND the status-file initialisation, and the second failure cascades through every downstream mark_session/update_status_file call.
bugs.fix: Mirror the bug-185/186/188 coerce-and-skip pattern at every int() call site that consumes a producer-emitted scalar from the plan file. In epic-ui.py parse_bash_plan: wrap the WAVE-line `int(parts[1])` in try/except (ValueError, IndexError) → continue (the IndexError covers a hypothetical bare `WAVE` without a trailing space — `line.startswith('WAVE ')` rules that out today, but defending the parser locally insulates against future format changes); wrap the SESSION-line `int(parts[1])` and `int(parts[2])` in try/except ValueError → continue (the existing `len(parts) < 6` guard already covers IndexError on parts[1]/[2]). In run-sessions.sh's status-init heredoc: add the same `len(parts) < 6` guard before the unpacking AND wrap `int(parts[1])` / `int(parts[2])` in try/except ValueError → continue, so a malformed SESSION row no longer poisons the entire status-file initialisation. The well-formed lines around the malformed one are still parsed and registered, so the dashboard and status file degrade gracefully (dropping ONLY the malformed sessions) instead of failing wholesale. Verified by reproducing all three crash chains on the pre-patch tree (`WAVE garbage`, `SESSION garbage 1 ...`, `SESSION 1 abc ...`) and confirming all three skip cleanly on the patched tree, with the well-formed happy-path (META wave_count=2, two well-formed WAVE+SESSION pairs) still parsing identically — wave_count=2, sessions={1, 2}, waves=[[1], [2]].

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh bash-side SESSION-row parser at line 462-482 crashes the entire runner with `garbage: unbound variable` (rc=1) under the runner-wide `set -euo pipefail` whenever the shared `.epic-dag-plan.bash` file contains a SESSION line whose wave or id field is non-numeric. Symmetric defect to bug-192: the SAME plan file is consumed by THREE call sites and bug-192 only patched two of them. (a) bash-side parser at run-sessions.sh:462-482 (this bug) — runs FIRST in the boot sequence, builds WAVE_IDS / SESSION_FILE_BASENAME / SESSION_SLUG_BY_ID / SESSION_DEPS_BY_ID / SESSION_PARALLEL / SESSION_WAVE_OF / SESSION_MODEL_BY_ID / SESSION_CLI_BY_ID. The line `WAVE_IDS[$sw]=...` evaluates `$sw` in arithmetic context (bash array subscripts are arithmetic expressions); a string like `garbage` is a variable lookup that resolves to 0 only when `set -u` is OFF — under the runner's `set -u` it raises `garbage: unbound variable` and the runner exits non-zero before the bug-192-patched heredoc / dashboard ever launches. The user sees a cryptic bash error far from the YAML / plan-file cause. (b) Same chain for `SESSION 1 abc ...` — sw=1 passes, but the next line `SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[$sid]=$sfile` has $sid=abc, same arithmetic-subscript-as-variable-lookup, same set -u crash. (c) Truncated SESSION rows where `set -- $_rest` lands fewer than 6 fields leave `sslug`/`sparallel`/etc. empty — under `set -u` the bare `${5:-}` was already needed to avoid an unbound-variable trip on the original `sslug="$5"`. Producer-emittable corruption modes are exactly bug-192's: partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write of the plan file, NFS read racing the write, schema drift from a future writer, hand-edit for debugging.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-185/186/188/189/192 — every consumer of producer-emitted data must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer cannot interpret, AND that audit must extend to every narrowing predicate the consumer applies. Bug-192 audited two of the three plan-file consumers (the Python parse_bash_plan in epic-ui.py and the Python status-init heredoc in run-sessions.sh) — both narrow strings to ints via `int(parts[i])`. The bash-side parser does the SAME narrowing implicitly: bash array subscripts ARE arithmetic, so `WAVE_IDS[$sw]` is a string→int narrowing. It crashes on every shape outside the numeric subspace, and under `set -u` the failure mode is more catastrophic than the heredoc's silent exit-non-zero — the runner itself exits before the bug-192-protected consumers run. Because the bash side runs FIRST, bug-192's heredoc fix is dead code on a poisoned plan: the bash parser kills the runner before the heredoc spawns. The bug-192 audit thread is incomplete until this third site is patched.
bugs.fix: Mirror bug-192's coerce-and-skip pattern at the bash-side parser. Two changes inside the SESSION case: (1) pad the field assignments with `${N:-}` defaults (sw/sid/sfile/sdeps/sslug/sparallel) so a truncated row with fewer than 6 fields doesn't trip `set -u` on the bare `$5` / `$6` itself BEFORE the validity check fires; (2) add `if ! [[ "$sw" =~ ^[0-9]+$ && "$sid" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then continue; fi` immediately after the field unpacking and BEFORE any array subscript uses $sw / $sid. Well-formed lines around the malformed one still register, so the runner degrades gracefully (dropping ONLY the malformed sessions) instead of failing wholesale — same coerce-and-skip discipline as bug-192. Verified by reproducing all three crash chains on the pre-patch tree (`SESSION garbage 1 ...`, `SESSION 1 abc ...`, truncated `SESSION 1`) and confirming all three skip cleanly on the patched tree, with a well-formed two-session epic still parsing identically (sid=1 wave=1 slug=foo, sid=2 wave=2 slug=bar).

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh accepts `--max-parallel 0` (and `"maxParallel": 0` from .epic-config.json) at validation time and then hangs forever in the wave-fanout throttle loop. Concrete chain: (1) the early validation at run-sessions.sh:141-147 uses regex `^[0-9]+$`, which matches `0`; (2) the config loader at run-sessions.sh:188-190 uses regex `[0-9]+` for `"maxParallel"`, which also matches `0`; (3) the wave loop at run-sessions.sh:937-942 throttles via `while [[ ${#JOB_PIDS[@]} -ge $MAX_PARALLEL ]]; do reap_finished_jobs; [[ ${#JOB_PIDS[@]} -ge $MAX_PARALLEL ]] && sleep 1; done`. With MAX_PARALLEL=0 the predicate `array_length >= 0` is mathematically always true (every non-negative integer >= 0, and bash array length is always >= 0 — including the empty-array starting state). The loop never exits, no session ever spawns, and the runner hangs at the start of wave 1 consuming every minute of the wave-timeout's idle budget before SIGKILL ends the wait. The user sees a hung process with no diagnostic — the dashboard launches and shows pending-forever, but the actual cause (a numerically-valid but semantically-invalid concurrency ceiling) is never named.
bugs.root_cause: The runner has four numeric flags whose value column accepts 0 — `--timeout 0`, `--retry 0`, `--wave-timeout 0`, `--max-parallel 0` — and three of them have well-defined zero semantics (timeout=disabled, retry=no retries, wave-timeout=auto-derive). Only --max-parallel needs a positive concurrency ceiling because the throttle predicate is a saturating `>= MAX_PARALLEL` check, not a `>` check. The shared `^[0-9]+$` regex in both the cmdline validator and the config-file regex is too permissive for this one flag; it accepts 0 as a syntactically-valid integer when the consumer requires >= 1.
bugs.fix: Add an explicit `if [[ "$MAX_PARALLEL" -lt 1 ]]; then err ...; exit 1; fi` check AFTER the config loader runs (line 211, between the closing `fi` of the config block and the `Detect CLI` section) so a bad `.epic-config.json "maxParallel": 0` is caught alongside a bad `--max-parallel 0`. The error message names both the cause (would hang the throttle loop indefinitely) and the remediation (use 1 for serial execution). Verified by confirming `--max-parallel 0` now exits rc=1 with a clear diagnostic, while `--max-parallel 1`, `--max-parallel 2`, and `--max-parallel 4` all complete --dry-run cleanly with rc=0.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh bash-side SESSION-row parser at lines 481-528 accepts truncated rows whose sw and sid fields are numeric but whose remaining fields (sfile, sdeps, sslug, sparallel) are missing — e.g. a `SESSION 1 5` line with only 3 fields after the SESSION keyword. The numeric-only check added by bug-193 (`if ! [[ "$sw" =~ ^[0-9]+$ && "$sid" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then continue; fi`) passes because $1=1 and $2=5 are both digits, and the `${N:-}` field-defaulting in the assignment line silently collapses missing fields to empty strings. The malformed entry then registers a session with empty SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[5] and SESSION_SLUG_BY_ID[5]. Downstream the wave loop fans the session out, run_one_session builds session_path="$TRUNK_SESSIONS_DIR/" (just the directory, fname is empty), extract_prompt runs awk against a directory and returns empty, and the session fails with rc=2 and the misleading log line "ERROR: could not extract prompt from <directory>". The user sees a symptom-level error pointing at the perfectly-valid prompt-extraction code path instead of naming the malformed plan as the cause; on inspection the plan file is corrupted but neither the runner banner nor the failed-session diagnostic mentions it. Same producer-emittable corruption modes as bug-192/193: a partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write of the plan file, an NFS read racing the write, schema drift from a future writer that emits SESSION rows in a different format, or a hand-edit for debugging.
bugs.root_cause: bug-193's stated intent included a length check — its commit message and in-source comment both say "Also reject empty truncated rows (fewer than 6 fields after split) for parity with the heredoc's `if len(parts) < 6: continue`". But the implementation only added two things: (1) `${N:-}` defaulting on the field assignments so a truncated row doesn't trip set -u on bare `$5` / `$6` BEFORE the validity check fires, and (2) the numeric regex on sw and sid. The length check itself was never written, so the bash side stayed asymmetric with the heredoc's Python guard at run-sessions.sh:887-895 (`if len(parts) < 6: continue`) and the parse_bash_plan guard in epic-ui.py:144-145 (`if len(parts) < 6: continue`). All three call sites consume the SAME shared plan file, so the same producer-emittable shapes must be rejected uniformly — a row the Python consumers correctly drop is still loaded into the bash arrays, leading to the misleading "could not extract prompt" failure described above. Same audit class as bug-192/193: every consumer of producer-emitted data must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret, AND multiple consumers of the same data must apply consistent rejection rules.
bugs.fix: Add the missing length check immediately after the numeric regex guard in the bash-side parser. After `set -- $_rest` the field count is exposed via `$#`, so the equivalent of the Python `if len(parts) < 6: continue` is `[[ $# -lt 6 ]] && continue`. The well-formed lines around the truncated one still register, so the runner degrades gracefully (dropping ONLY the malformed sessions) instead of registering them with empty file/slug and failing later with a misleading message — matching the coerce-and-skip discipline already used by bug-192/193. Verified by reproducing the pre-patch chain (`SESSION 1 5` row registered with empty sfile, sslug, sparallel; downstream session would have failed with "could not extract prompt from <directory>"); on the patched tree the truncated row is rejected cleanly while the well-formed siblings still parse identically (sid=5 sfile='session-05-foo.md' etc.). Happy-path two-session epic (session-01-foo + session-02-bar with depends_on: [01]) still parses to the same 2-wave plan under --dry-run.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh's META wave_count parser at line 478 takes the value verbatim: `WAVE_COUNT="${line#META wave_count=}"`. When a producer-emittable corruption (partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp`, NFS read race, schema drift from a future writer, hand-edit for debugging) lands a non-numeric value like `META wave_count=garbage`, the string "garbage" flows into two arithmetic-context consumers downstream: line 534's `[[ -z "$OPERATOR_PATH" || "$WAVE_COUNT" -eq 0 ]]` zero-check, and the wave loop's `for (( wn=1; wn<=WAVE_COUNT; wn++ ))` iterator. Bash arithmetic treats a non-numeric operand as a variable-name reference: `[[ "garbage" -eq 0 ]]` looks up a variable named `garbage`, which is unset, and under the runner's `set -u` exits the entire runner with `garbage: unbound variable` (rc=1) BEFORE the existing zero-check at line 534 can fire its intended "DAG plan is empty or malformed" diagnostic. The user sees a cryptic bash error and the epic never starts; the dashboard never launches and there is no way to tell from the error message that the plan file is corrupted. Reproduced by feeding `META wave_count=garbage` into the same case-statement parser; the line-534 comparison crashes with rc=1 and the literal text `garbage: unbound variable`.
bugs.root_cause: Symmetric audit gap to bug-193 — bug-193 caught bash arithmetic-as-int narrowing on the SESSION row's wave/id subscripts, but the META wave_count field consumes the same shared plan file and applies the same narrowing predicate (bash arithmetic in `[[ -eq ]]` and `(( ))`) WITHOUT validating the input. The META parser at line 478 only does a prefix strip — it does not verify the trailing value is a non-negative integer the way the SESSION-row parser now does for sw/sid. Same audit class: every consumer of producer-emitted data must reject inputs the producer can emit but the consumer can't interpret, AND that audit must extend to every narrowing predicate the consumer applies — bash arithmetic-as-int is a string→int narrowing on the WAVE_COUNT field too. The producer (epic-dag.py emit_bash) always emits `META wave_count=<int>` via `print(f"META wave_count={len(plan['waves'])}")`, so the well-formed shape is well-defined; only the post-write corruption modes the bug-192/193 audit already enumerated can land a non-numeric value here.
bugs.fix: Validate the META wave_count value at parse time using the same `^[0-9]+$` regex the SESSION-row parser uses for sw/sid. If the raw value is non-numeric (or empty), leave WAVE_COUNT at its default 0; the existing line-534 guard `if [[ -z "$OPERATOR_PATH" || "$WAVE_COUNT" -eq 0 ]]; then err "DAG plan is empty or malformed"; exit 1; fi` then fires with the proper diagnostic instead of the cryptic `garbage: unbound variable` from set -u. Verified by feeding `META wave_count=garbage` and `META wave_count=` (empty) into the patched parser: WAVE_COUNT stays 0 and the line-534 check would exit cleanly with the user-facing error; well-formed `META wave_count=3` still sets WAVE_COUNT=3 unchanged. Same coerce-and-skip discipline as bug-192/193 — malformed input degrades gracefully into the existing empty-or-malformed error path instead of crashing with set -u.

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 2 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 6→12 lines (2 removed)

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh's numeric-flag validation regex `^[0-9]+$` accepts leading-zero values (`08`, `09`, `010`, `019`...) that bash arithmetic later silently re-interprets as octal. Three concrete failure modes: (1) `--max-parallel 010` silently reads as decimal 8 — user gets ⅕ less parallelism than asked for with no diagnostic; (2) `--max-parallel 08`/`09` raise `value too great for base` to stderr at every arithmetic site (bug-194/108 guards at lines 226/410 AND the wave throttle `[[ ${#JOB_PIDS[@]} -ge $MAX_PARALLEL ]]` on every wave-fanout iteration), with `[[ ]]` returning false on the bash error so the throttle never engages and ALL sessions in the wave fan out concurrently regardless of the cap; (3) `--wave-timeout 08` crashes the runner mid-wave with `wave_timeout: unbound variable` when `wave_timeout=$((_effective_wtm * 60))` fails on the octal decode and the next `[[ $wave_timeout -gt 0 ]]` references the unbound result under set -u. Symmetric audit gaps in the META wave_count parser (corruption-emitted `010` silently truncates a 10-wave plan to 8) and SESSION row sw/sid parser (corruption-emitted `010` silently registers in WAVE_IDS[8] / SESSION_*[8] instead of [10], DAG silently mis-routes). Same producer-emittable corruption modes as bug-192/193/195/196 for the META and SESSION sites.
bugs.root_cause: Bash arithmetic context interprets leading-zero strings as octal (POSIX). The `^[0-9]+$` validation regex is too permissive for the consumer — it accepts every digit shape the user can type, but bash arithmetic can only correctly read decimal-without-leading-zero values. Same audit class as bug-194 (numeric flag the validator accepts but the consumer can't interpret correctly): every numeric value that flows into bash arithmetic must produce the user's apparent intent, not be silently semantically-different. The audit must extend to BOTH cmdline flags AND producer-emitted scalars (META wave_count, SESSION row sw/sid) AND .epic-config.json extracts.
bugs.fix: Normalize via `10#` immediately after each regex passes, forcing base-10 interpretation regardless of leading zeros (`10#08` = 8, `10#010` = 10, `10#0` = 0). At the cmdline validation loop (line ~141), use `printf -v "$_arg_name" '%d' "$((10#$_val))"` to reassign each named integer flag in place (bash 3.2+ compatible). At the .epic-config.json extracts (lines ~176-204), wrap each `BASH_REMATCH[1]` in `$((10#...))`. At the META wave_count parser, normalize `WAVE_COUNT=$((10#$_wc_raw))`. At the SESSION row sw/sid validation, add `sw=$((10#$sw)); sid=$((10#$sid))` after the regex passes. Verified: `--max-parallel 08`/`09`/`010`/`4` all return rc=0 with no bash errors on the patched tree; `--max-parallel 0` still rejected by the bug-194 guard; happy-path `--dry-run` and `--show-dag` parse identically.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh's stale-worktree-cleanup loop at line ~715 reads DAG_TMP for a second pass (collecting current session ids) and runs `set -- $line; case "$1"` without first guarding `[[ -z "$line" ]]`. With an empty/blank `$line`, `set -- ` (no args) clears positional params, and the next `"$1"` references an unset positional under the runner-wide `set -u` — raising `$1: unbound variable` and crashing the entire runner with rc=1 before any worktree is touched. Reproduced by injecting a blank line into a well-formed DAG_TMP fixture: pre-patch crash with `$1: unbound variable` rc=1; post-patch the well-formed SESSION rows around the blank line still parse to `current_session_ids=' 1 2'`.
bugs.root_cause: epic-dag.py emit_bash never emits blank lines under happy path, but the same producer-emittable corruption modes bug-192/193/195/196 already enumerate (partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write, NFS read racing the write, schema drift from a future writer that adds blank-line padding for readability, hand-edit for debugging) all leave a blank line in an otherwise well-formed plan file. Both Python consumers of the plan file (`parse_bash_plan` in epic-ui.py and the status-init heredoc) call `line.strip()` before any `startswith` check, so blank lines skip cleanly there. The bash-side SESSION-row parser at line ~474 also handles blank lines cleanly via `case "$line" in ... "SESSION "*) ... esac` (none of the patterns match an empty string, so the whole case falls through). This cleanup loop was the only consumer running with `set -- $line` AHEAD of any whitespace/non-empty check — same audit class as bug-192/193/195/196, the only bash-side reader still vulnerable to the corruption shapes those bugs already documented.
bugs.fix: Add `[[ -z "$line" ]] && continue` at the top of the loop body before `set -- $line`. Same coerce-and-skip discipline as bug-192/193/195/196 — the well-formed lines around the blank one still register, so the runner degrades gracefully (dropping ONLY the blank line) instead of failing wholesale with the misleading `$1: unbound variable` diagnostic that doesn't name the malformed plan as the cause.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh's stale-worktree-cleanup loop at line ~798 reads DAG_TMP for a second pass (collecting current session ids) and runs `set -- $line; case "$1" in SESSION) current_session_ids="$current_session_ids $3" ;; esac` — with bare `$3` access. bug-199 patched the same loop's blank-line crash by adding `[[ -z "$line" ]] && continue` before `set --`, but the truncated-SESSION-row case was missed. A producer-emittable corruption (partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write of the plan file, NFS read racing the write, schema drift from a future writer, hand-edit for debugging) can leave a SESSION line with fewer than 3 fields — e.g. a bare `SESSION` line, or `SESSION 1` (only sw, no sid) — and after `set -- $line` the positional params have only 1 or 2 entries. The case pattern `"$1" == SESSION` matches, and the next bare `$3` reference raises `$3: unbound variable` under the runner-wide `set -euo pipefail`, crashing the entire runner with rc=1 BEFORE any worktree is touched. The user sees a cryptic bash error (`run-sessions.sh: line 800: $3: unbound variable`) and the epic never starts — the misleading diagnostic doesn't name the malformed plan as the cause, and the cleanup loop's role (collecting session ids to skip during stale-worktree removal) is opaque enough that the user has no signal to look at the plan file. Symmetric audit gap to bug-199 — bug-199 caught the empty-line shape but not the truncated-row shape; both arise from the same producer-emittable corruption modes bug-192/193/195/196/199 already enumerate. The bash-side SESSION-row parser at line 549 (bug-195) added a `[[ $# -lt 6 ]] && continue` length guard for the same producer corruption, but the cleanup loop here is a separate consumer and didn't share that guard. Same audit class as bug-185/186/188/189/192/193/195/196/199 — every consumer of producer-emitted plan-file data must reject inputs the consumer can't interpret, and the audit must extend to every narrowing predicate the consumer applies (here: bare `$3` is a string→positional narrowing under `set -u` that crashes on every shape with fewer than 3 fields).
bugs.root_cause: bug-199's audit thread stopped at empty-line shape; same loop has a second narrowing predicate (`$3` access under `set -u`) that crashes on truncated SESSION rows. The cleanup loop is the third bash-side consumer of the shared plan file (alongside the bug-193-patched main parser and the bug-199-patched empty-line handler), and it inherited only the empty-line guard — the length-or-default guard that the main parser got via bug-195's `[[ $# -lt 6 ]] && continue` was not extended to this site. Default `${3:-}` is the minimum coerce-and-skip discipline matching the surrounding audit class: an empty default is a no-op for the regex match at line 818 (`[[ " $current_session_ids " =~ " $stale_id " ]]`), so the well-formed SESSION rows in the same file still register their sid and the cleanup loop degrades gracefully (dropping ONLY the malformed rows from the skip list) instead of failing wholesale.
bugs.fix: Replace bare `$3` with `${3:-}` at run-sessions.sh:800 so a truncated SESSION row's missing `$3` defaults to empty rather than tripping `set -u`. The empty default is a no-op for the downstream regex match against `stale_id` (which is always purely-numeric via the worktree-name extraction at line 811-814), so well-formed sessions in the same file still register and the malformed rows simply contribute nothing to the skip list. Verified by reproducing the pre-patch crash on `SESSION` (alone) and `SESSION 1` (only sw) lines: pre-patch runner exits rc=1 with `$3: unbound variable`; post-patch the well-formed SESSION rows around the malformed ones still parse to current_session_ids=' 1  5 6' (the 5 came from `SESSION 1 5` where `$3` is the sid, the 6 from a well-formed `SESSION 2 6 ...`). Happy-path two-session epic (--dry-run) parses identically to pre-patch — same coerce-and-skip discipline as bug-199.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh and run-sprint.sh's value-bearing flag parsers access `$2` without first checking that the user supplied a value. Each value flag case branch is a one-liner like `--start)  START_FROM="$2"; shift 2 ;;` — with the runner-wide `set -euo pipefail`, running the script with such a flag as the LAST argument (e.g. `run-sessions.sh foo/ --start`, `run-sprint.sh --branch`) causes `"$2"` to reference an unset positional and raise `$2: unbound variable`, crashing the script BEFORE any validation can fire. The user sees a cryptic bash error naming a positional parameter index — `run-sessions.sh: line 109: $2: unbound variable` — not the flag they typed, so they have no signal that they forgot a value. Same UX class as bug-194/198 (numeric-flag values the validator could detect but the bash error masked), now extended to argv-shape itself. Affects every value-bearing flag in both scripts: run-sessions.sh's `--start`, `--end`, `--max-parallel`, `--branch`, `--base`, `--model`, `--cli`, `--timeout`, `--retry`, `--wave-timeout` (10 sites at lines 109-130), and run-sprint.sh's `--sprint-config`, `--branch`, `--base`, `--model`, `--cli`, plus the shared `--timeout|--retry|--max-parallel|--wave-timeout` pass-through (6 sites at lines 100-114). The `--flag=value` branches in run-sprint.sh are unaffected because `${1#*=}` parameter expansion always succeeds (an empty value just produces an empty string, validated downstream). The bug fires before the existing numeric validator (bug-198's `printf -v` normalization) and before the existing `[[ -z "$SESSIONS_DIR" ]]` checks, so neither layer of validation gets a chance to surface a meaningful error.
bugs.root_cause: Each value-bearing case branch was written as a single-line `FOO="$2"; shift 2`, trusting that `$2` exists. Under `set -u`, an unset positional is an error — but the diagnostic comes from bash internals (`line N: 2: unbound variable`), not from the script's `err()` helper, so the user has no signal that they're missing a value for a specific flag. Same audit class as bug-194/198 (a flag value the parser couldn't interpret correctly) extended to the `unset` case rather than the malformed-value case. The script's argv-parsing layer must treat `$2` as a producer-emittable shape the consumer can't interpret when it's absent, exactly the way the plan-file parsers already treat truncated SESSION rows (bug-195/200).
bugs.fix: Add a small helper `require_flag_value() { (( $# < 2 )) && { err "Missing value for $1"; exit 1; }; }` to each script and call it inline at the start of every value-bearing case branch BEFORE the bare `$2` reference: `--start)  require_flag_value "$@"; START_FROM="$2"; shift 2 ;;`. The `(( $# < 2 ))` test is true exactly when the flag is the last argument with no value to consume — the crash precondition. The function uses the script's existing `err()` helper so the diagnostic gets the project's coloured `[epic] Missing value for --start` / `[sprint] Missing value for --branch` formatting instead of bash's bare positional-index error. Verified: pre-patch `bash run-sessions.sh --start` crashed with `$2: unbound variable`; post-patch it exits rc=1 with `[epic] Missing value for --start`. Same for `--end`, `--max-parallel`, `--branch`, `--base`, `--model`, `--cli`, `--timeout`, `--retry`, `--wave-timeout` on run-sessions.sh; same for `--sprint-config`, `--branch`, `--base`, `--model`, `--cli`, `--timeout|--retry|--max-parallel|--wave-timeout` on run-sprint.sh. Happy-path `--start 5 --max-parallel 3 --timeout 5 --retry 2 --wave-timeout 10 --dry-run` on a real two-session fixture still parses identically (no behavioural change for valid invocations). The `--flag=*` branches in run-sprint.sh are intentionally unguarded because their `${1#*=}` form never trips `set -u`.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh status-init heredoc at line 1046-1069 does not normalize the SESSION-row sid before using it as the JSON status-file key. parts[2] is read verbatim — `sid, slug = parts[2], parts[5]` — so a producer-emittable corruption like `SESSION 1 010 session-10-foo.md - foo 1 - -` (partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write of the plan file, NFS read race, schema drift from a future writer, hand-edit for debugging — same modes the bug-192/193/195/196/197/199/200 audit chain enumerates) lands `sessions['010'] = {status: 'pending', ...}` in the status JSON written at init time. Every subsequent mark_session call writes `sessions['10']` — mark_session's heredoc converts via `key = str(session_id)` where session_id is the bash-side sid that bug-197 already normalized via `$((10#$sid))` to the integer 10. The status file ends up with TWO entries for the same session: `'010'` (pending forever, orphaned) and `'10'` (the live entry receiving running/done/failed updates). The dashboard's parse_bash_plan keys via `int(parts[2])` and looks up the status entry via `str(int(parts[2]))=='10'` — so the orphan never affects the user-visible dashboard rendering, but it persists in the status file for the entire run's duration and is visible to any external watcher that polls the JSON without round-tripping every key through int(). Symmetric audit gap to bug-197's bash-side parser fix at run-sessions.sh:616-617 (which `10#`-normalized the SAME field for the array-subscript consumer at SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[$sid] / WAVE_IDS[$sw] but not for this Python heredoc consumer), and to bug-203's cleanup-loop fix (the THIRD consumer of the SAME plan file's SESSION rows). Same audit class as bug-194/196/197/198 — every numeric value that flows from a producer-emitted scalar into a downstream consumer must be normalized at the consumer boundary, not just at one of multiple consumer sites. The `int(parts[2])` validation already in place at line 1058 (added by the bug-192-class audit chain) confirms parseability but did not normalize the string used as the dict key — the round-trip `str(int(parts[2]))` is the minimal canonicalization that strips leading zeros while preserving the rest of the string-key invariant the producer/consumer protocol requires.
bugs.root_cause: bug-197's normalization audit thread covered the bash-side parser at line 569-651 (SESSION row sw/sid → array subscripts), the .epic-config.json regex extracts at line 234-262, the META wave_count parser at line 555-563, and the cmdline numeric flag validator at line 161-198 — but stopped at the bash boundary. The Python heredoc that initializes the shared status-file (line 1046-1069) is a SEPARATE consumer of the SAME plan file, reading parts[2] verbatim as the dict key. Producer corruption shapes that bug-197 was supposed to coerce-and-normalize at every consumer boundary still land verbatim into the status JSON's sessions key namespace from this site, creating an asymmetry between the init's key set ('010') and the runtime updater's key set ('10'). Same producer-corruption modes as bug-192/193/195/196/197/199/200 (partial-flush, NFS read race, schema drift, hand-edit). The string-canonicalization fix mirrors the bash-side `$((10#$sid))` invariant at the consumer protocol's other endpoint, so init-time and runtime keys agree.
bugs.fix: Replace `sid, slug = parts[2], parts[5]` with `sid, slug = str(int(parts[2])), parts[5]` at run-sessions.sh:1061. The `int(parts[2])` validation already in place at line 1058 (introduced by the bug-192-class audit chain) guarantees the value is parseable as a base-10 integer; round-tripping through int strips leading zeros so `'010'` becomes `'10'` (matching the bash-side `$((10#$sid))` normalization), `'00'` becomes `'0'`, and well-formed values (`'10'`, `'5'`) round-trip unchanged. Verified by reproducing the pre-patch corruption: `SESSION 1 010 session-10-foo.md - foo 1 - -` → init writes `sessions['010']`; post-patch the same row writes `sessions['10']`, agreeing with mark_session's `str(session_id)` key. Well-formed `SESSION 1 5 session-05-bar.md - bar 1 - -` round-trips to `sessions['5']` unchanged on both pre-patch and post-patch trees.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh stale-worktree-cleanup loop at line ~837 reads SESSION rows from DAG_TMP for the second pass and accumulates `current_session_ids="$current_session_ids ${3:-}"` — the `${3:-}` default added by bug-200 prevents the truncated-row crash, but the value is appended verbatim without `10#` normalization. A producer-emittable corruption (partial-flush from a SIGKILL'd `cp` mid-write of the plan file, NFS read race, schema drift, hand-edit — same modes bug-192/193/195/196/197/199/200 enumerate) like `SESSION 1 010 session-10-foo.md - foo 1 - -` lands `current_session_ids=' 010'`. The downstream stale_id at line ~851 IS already normalized via `stale_id="$((10#$stale_id))"` (so the worktree directory name `epic--name--s10-foo` produces `stale_id=10`), but the substring match `[[ " $current_session_ids " =~ " $stale_id " ]]` then compares ` 010 ` against pattern ` 10 ` — the regex requires a SPACE before `10` but in the haystack the `10` is preceded by `0`, so the match silently fails. The cleanup loop classifies the worktree as orphaned even though it belongs to the current run's DAG, the safety guards (is_worktree_in_use, has_unmerged) gate whether `git worktree remove --force` proceeds, and a worktree the runner is about to recreate risks getting force-removed if its branch happens to share HEAD with trunk and no in-use process holds it. Symmetric audit gap to bug-197's bash-side parser fix at line 616-617 (which `10#`-normalized the same field for the array-subscript consumer at SESSION_FILE_BASENAME[$sid] / WAVE_IDS[$sw] but not for this cleanup-loop consumer — the THIRD consumer of the SAME SESSION row data after the main parser and the heredoc init). Same audit class as bug-194/196/197/198/202 — every numeric value that flows from a producer-emitted scalar into a downstream consumer must be normalized at the consumer boundary.
bugs.root_cause: bug-197 normalized sw/sid at the main bash-side parser (line 616-617) and bug-200 added the `${3:-}` truncation guard at this cleanup loop (line 837), but neither thread carried the `10#` invariant into the cleanup loop's accumulator. The loop then mixed two encodings of the same numeric field — raw decimal-string from `$3` versus `10#`-normalized integer from the worktree-name regex — and the substring match relies on lexical equality. Same producer-corruption modes as bug-192/193/195/196/197/199/200/202; same downstream-asymmetry pattern as bug-202 where one consumer normalizes and another doesn't. The numeric-regex pre-check mirrors the bug-193 SESSION-row regex check so a non-numeric corruption falls through cleanly instead of letting `$((10#abc))` raise under `set -e`.
bugs.fix: Replace `case "$1" in SESSION) current_session_ids="$current_session_ids ${3:-}" ;; esac` with a numeric-validating, `10#`-normalizing branch: `case "$1" in SESSION) if [[ "${3:-}" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then current_session_ids="$current_session_ids $((10#${3}))"; fi ;; esac` at run-sessions.sh:836-863. Verified: pre-patch `SESSION 1 010 ...` → `current_session_ids=' 010'` and `[[ ' 010 ' =~ ' 10 ' ]]` is FALSE (worktree `--s10-...` wrongly classified as orphan); post-patch the same input produces `current_session_ids=' 10'` and the match succeeds. Truncated `SESSION` / `SESSION 1` rows still skip cleanly via the regex guard (mirrors bug-200's defaulting). Well-formed `SESSION 2 5 ...` registers `' 5'` unchanged. A non-DAG worktree (`stale_id=99`) still correctly fails the match.

bugs.error_message: PR body's 'Stats' shortstat over-counts whenever the user's local <default> branch lags behind origin
bugs.root_cause: Auto-PR shortstat at line 1638 used `${DEFAULT_BRANCH}...HEAD` (bare local branch name). After the auto-rebase at line 1589 lands HEAD on top of origin/<default>, the merge-base of (local <default>, HEAD) is the LOCAL tip — and `git fetch --prune` updates remote-tracking refs without fast-forwarding the local branch, so users in worktree workflows routinely have local <default> behind origin. The triple-dot diff then walks back to the lagging local tip and absorbs every commit that landed on origin between the two refs into the shortstat. Verified on a fixture (local main 2 commits behind origin/main, epic adds 1 file): pre-patch reports `3 files changed`, post-patch reports `1 file changed`. Symmetric audit gap to bug-122 in cleanup_merged_epic_branches (epic-git.sh:255-258) which already prefers `origin/$default_branch` when it resolves; the auto-PR site never got the same treatment.
bugs.fix: Resolve a DEFAULT_REF that prefers `origin/$DEFAULT_BRANCH` (when it verifies via `git rev-parse --verify --quiet`) and falls back to `$DEFAULT_BRANCH` otherwise, then feed `${DEFAULT_REF}...HEAD` into the shortstat. Mirrors the cleanup_merged_epic_branches pattern exactly.

bugs.error_message: Success-path 'Next step: gh pr create --base main' suggestion was hardcoded to 'main', producing an unrunnable command on master/develop/trunk-default repos
bugs.root_cause: The user-facing advice at line 1752 (printed in the success path when AUTO_PR was disabled or gh wasn't available) hardcoded `--base main` even though BASE_BRANCH was already resolved at line 943-945 to the actual default — either the user's `--base` value, the value of `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD`, or the literal fallback `main`. On a repo whose default is `master` / `develop` / `trunk` the user would copy-paste the suggestion and `gh pr create` would fail with `branch main does not exist on remote` — silently misleading advice the user has no way to fix. Symmetric audit gap to bug-122 (cleanup_merged_epic_branches in epic-git.sh) which already prefers the resolved default over hardcoded `main`; this user-visible string was the remaining unaudited site.
bugs.fix: Replace the hardcoded `main` with `$BASE_BRANCH`. The variable is always set at the success-path tail because the resolution block at line 943-945 runs unconditionally before any worktree setup or wave loop — `[[ -z "$BASE_BRANCH" ]]` falls through to `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` then to literal `main`, so `$BASE_BRANCH` is guaranteed non-empty downstream.

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
bugs.root_cause: 4 lines replaced/restructured
bugs.fix: Rewrote 14→38 lines (4 removed)

bugs.error_message: session_completed_on_branch (epic-git.sh:290-333) prefers the LOCAL `$BASE_BRANCH` ref over `origin/$BASE_BRANCH` when both verify, inverting the local-vs-origin preference that bug-122 (cleanup_merged_epic_branches at lines 255-258) and bug-204 (the auto-PR shortstat at run-sessions.sh:1651-1654) already established for symmetric call sites. The auto-rebase at run-sessions.sh:1589 lands the trunk worktree's HEAD on top of `origin/$REBASE_DEFAULT`, and `git fetch --prune` (cleanup_merged_epic_branches at the run's tail and any user-driven fetch beforehand) updates the remote-tracking ref but does NOT fast-forward the local one — so on a resume run AFTER a prior auto-rebase, the trunk's HEAD ancestry passes through origin's tip-at-rebase-time, NOT through local `$BASE_BRANCH`. The rev-range `$BASE_BRANCH..$branch` then computes `merge-base(local-base, $branch) = local-base-tip` (an ancestor of HEAD because origin's tip-at-rebase-time descends from local-base), and `git log` walks back to that lagging local tip — absorbing every commit that landed on origin between the local tip and origin's tip-at-rebase-time into the subject scan. If any of those absorbed commits are `feat: Session N <sep>...` from a previously-merged epic on the SAME repo (the exact false-positive class bug-080 went out of its way to prevent), the helper returns 0 for sessions that NEVER ran on this branch, and the runner's end-of-epic check at run-sessions.sh:1512 silently classifies the run as ALL_SESSIONS_DONE — triggering the cleanup path at lines 1518-1576 that `git rm -r` deletes `docs/claude-sessions/<epic>/` and `docs/roadmap/<epic>/`, destroying the session prompts and roadmap handoffs the user still needs for the next resume attempt. Same producer/consumer audit class as bug-122/204: every site that walks `<base>..<branch>` against a base that the auto-rebase moves to `origin/<base>` must prefer the remote ref over the local one, because the local ref's lag is the common case in worktree workflows. session_completed_on_branch was the third local-vs-origin call site the bug-122 / bug-204 audit chain missed.
bugs.root_cause: Symmetric audit gap to bug-122 / bug-204. The bug-080 fix scoped session_completed_on_branch's subject scan to `${BASE_BRANCH}..${branch}` to keep previously-merged epics' `feat: Session N` commits out of the rev-range, but the local-vs-origin preference order was inverted: when both refs verify, the helper picks `$BASE_BRANCH` (local), exactly the variant the bug-122 cleanup helper and the bug-204 PR-stats site already documented as wrong. After auto-rebase moves HEAD onto `origin/$REBASE_DEFAULT`, the rev-range walks back through every commit between the lagging local tip and origin's tip — and any `feat: Session N` subject in that gap from a sibling merged epic falsely matches, the session is reported as already done, the cleanup path destroys scaffolding the user still needs.
bugs.fix: Mirror the bug-122/bug-204 pattern exactly. Resolve `base_ref` by trying `origin/$BASE_BRANCH` FIRST via the same `git rev-parse --verify --quiet` probe; only fall through to the local `$BASE_BRANCH` when the remote-tracking ref is missing (worktree-only checkouts). Preserves the bug-080 scope (the rev-range is still bounded), preserves the worktree-only-checkout case the original guard added (the elif still fires when origin/$BASE_BRANCH is unset), and closes the resume-after-rebase false-positive that the inverted preference let through. Verified against the bug-122 fix at epic-git.sh:255-258 and bug-204 at run-sessions.sh:1651-1654 — same conditional shape (`if rev-parse --verify --quiet origin/$X; then ref=origin/$X; fi`), just adapted to the existing two-ref else-if scaffolding so the worktree-only fall-through is preserved.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh's auto-rebase target (line 1585), on-default-branch guard (line 1605-1606), diff-stats base ref (line 1651), and the auto-`gh pr create` invocation (line 1672) all derive their notion of "the base branch" from `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef -q '.defaultBranchRef.name'` (the repo's gh-default), NOT from the runner's already-resolved `$BASE_BRANCH` (the user-supplied `--base` value, or origin/HEAD's target, or the literal `main` fallback — see line 943-945). For users who pass `--base develop` (or any non-gh-default) on a repo whose gh-default is main, this means: (a) auto-rebase fetches origin/main and rebases the develop-based epic onto origin/main — replays main's commits-not-in-develop on top of the epic, almost certainly producing conflicts or wrong-base history that the .wolf-resolve loop bails on with non-.wolf conflicts; (b) auto-PR is opened against main (gh's auto-default for `gh pr create` when --base is omitted) instead of develop — entirely wrong target branch, the user has to either close it and recreate manually or use `gh pr edit --base develop` to fix it; (c) diff stats are computed against `origin/main...HEAD` instead of `origin/develop...HEAD` — the PR body's "Stats" section absorbs every commit in develop-not-in-main, inflating the totals with churn that is not part of the epic; (d) the on-default-branch guard at line 1606 compares CURRENT_BRANCH against gh's default rather than the actual PR target — degenerate but semantically wrong. bug-205 fixed the human-readable success-path advice string at line 1760 to use `$BASE_BRANCH` ("Next step: gh pr create --base $BASE_BRANCH --head $BRANCH") — the script's own message named the right target — but the audit chain stopped at the user-visible string and missed every actual implementation site. The script's help at line 34 documents `--base BRANCH` as "Base branch for trunk (default: repo default)", and the trunk worktree IS branched off `$BASE_BRANCH` at line 959, so the user's `--base` value is honored at trunk creation but silently dropped at the rebase / PR-target / stats sites — the script does the right thing on the way in and the wrong thing on the way out. Same audit class as bug-122/204/207 (every consumer of "the base branch the user wanted" must use BASE_BRANCH, not gh's repo default); fourth call site that the bug-205 audit chain missed even though it fixed the symmetric advice string in the same function block.
bugs.root_cause: Symmetric audit gap to bug-205. The runner has TWO orthogonal sources of "the default branch": (1) `$BASE_BRANCH`, resolved at line 943-945 from `--base` → origin/HEAD → "main", which represents the user's chosen PR target and is used for trunk worktree creation (line 959) and the success-path advice string (line 1760, fixed in bug-205); (2) `$REBASE_DEFAULT` / `$DEFAULT_BRANCH`, re-derived inline from `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef` at lines 1585 and 1605, which represents the repo's GitHub-side default branch and silently overrides BASE_BRANCH for every actual rebase/PR/stats operation. The two sources agree only when the user did not pass `--base` and origin/HEAD matches the repo default (the common case, which is why the bug stayed silent). bug-205 patched only the user-visible advice string; the four implementation sites were left untouched.
bugs.fix: Replace the two `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef` queries with `$BASE_BRANCH` directly (lines 1585 and 1605) and add `--base "$BASE_BRANCH"` to the `gh pr create` invocation (line 1672). After the change, REBASE_DEFAULT, DEFAULT_BRANCH, and the gh PR target all agree with the trunk worktree's actual base. The existing local-vs-origin probe at lines 1651-1655 (bug-204) still works unchanged because DEFAULT_BRANCH now equals BASE_BRANCH — the `origin/$DEFAULT_BRANCH` rev-parse becomes `origin/$BASE_BRANCH`, which matches the bug-204 / bug-207 pattern. The on-default-branch guard at line 1606 still detects the degenerate self-merge case (CURRENT_BRANCH == BASE_BRANCH) but now compares against the user's actual target rather than gh's repo-wide default. Verified by inspection: pre-fix `--base develop` on a main-default repo rebased onto origin/main, opened a PR against main, computed stats against main; post-fix the same invocation rebases onto origin/develop, opens a PR against develop, computes stats against develop. No behavioral change in the common case (user did not pass --base, BASE_BRANCH == origin/HEAD == gh-default). The `command -v gh &>/dev/null` guard at line 1584 is now technically vestigial for the rebase block (gh is no longer called there) but kept for minimum-scope; relaxing it so auto-rebase runs without gh is a separate UX improvement.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh:1800 failure-path resume advice omits --base $BASE_BRANCH. When a user invokes the runner with --base develop (or any non-default base) and the epic fails mid-run, the printed Resume command (basename run-sessions.sh $ORIG_SESSIONS_DIR --start $FIRST_FAILED_ID --branch $BRANCH) drops the --base flag entirely. The user copy-pastes it as-is, the resumed run hits the BASE_BRANCH default-resolution at line 943-945, BASE_BRANCH falls back to origin/HEAD's target (typically main) or the literal main fallback, and every base-consuming site silently switches: the auto-rebase at line 1599 replays the develop-based epic onto origin/main (bug-208's exact failure mode reintroduced via the user-facing advice channel), the auto-PR at line 1704 opens against main instead of develop, the diff-stats at line 1676 compute against the wrong target inflating PR body churn (bug-204's symmetric site), and session_completed_on_branch (epic-git.sh:290 / bug-207) scopes its rev range \..\ against the wrong base — false-positiving sessions whose feat: Session N subjects appear in the lagging local-vs-origin gap of the new fallback. Same audit gap as bug-205 (which fixed the success-path Next step advice at line 1792 to use $BASE_BRANCH but stopped at the success path) and bug-208 (which fixed every implementation site but stopped at the implementation). The failure-path resume advice was the third user-facing string in the bug-205/207/208 chain that named a base-branch consumer without using the runner's resolved BASE_BRANCH.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bug-205/207/208 — every consumer of "the base branch the user wanted" must use the runner's already-resolved $BASE_BRANCH variable (line 943-945: --base value, or origin/HEAD target, or main fallback) rather than re-deriving the default at the consumer site or omitting the value entirely. The success-path Next step advice (line 1792) and the failure-path Resume with advice (line 1800) are symmetric user-facing strings and must use the same resolution. Bug-205 fixed the success path; this is the symmetric failure path the audit chain missed. The file's comment at line 1789-1791 explicitly notes that "BASE_BRANCH is always resolved by the time we reach this success-path tail (the resolution block at line 943 runs unconditionally before any worktree setup)" — that invariant holds for both tails because both branches of the EPIC_FAILED if/else execute after the line-943 resolution.
bugs.fix: Replace the failure-path advice string at run-sessions.sh:1800 with  so the printed command names the same base the original run used. BASE_BRANCH is unconditionally resolved at line 943-945 by the time we reach this failure-path tail, so the value is always non-empty. On a repo whose default is main and the user did not pass --base, the suggestion includes --base main (verbose but harmless and 100% reproducible). On a repo whose default is master/develop/trunk OR the user passed --base develop, the suggestion includes the actual target — the resumed run rebases, PRs, and scopes against the right base, closing the silent-base-switch chain.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:1584 auto-rebase block was gated on `command -v gh &>/dev/null` even though every line inside the block uses only `git fetch` and `rebase_with_wolf_resolve` — neither of which calls gh. After bug-208 replaced the `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef` query inside this block with `$BASE_BRANCH`, the gh check became vestigial. Users on machines without gh (CI runners, slim Docker images, devs who use git but not gh) silently got auto-rebase skipped: the if-block never fired, REBASE_RESULT stayed `skipped`, the local branch was pushed un-rebased, and the resulting PR was likely not fast-forward-mergeable. No diagnostic named missing gh as the cause because the user was asking for the rebase, not a PR (gh is legitimately needed only by the auto-PR block immediately below). bug-208's own commit message flagged this leftover: 'The `command -v gh &>/dev/null` guard at line 1584 is now technically vestigial for the rebase block (gh is no longer called there) but kept for minimum-scope; relaxing it so auto-rebase runs without gh is a separate UX improvement.' This patch is that separate improvement.
bugs.root_cause: Vestigial gate left in place during bug-208's minimum-scope fix. bug-208 replaced the only gh consumer inside the auto-rebase block (the `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef` query) with the runner's already-resolved $BASE_BRANCH, but didn't relax the surrounding `if $AUTO_REBASE && command -v gh &>/dev/null; then` gate that originally existed because the block needed gh. The auto-PR block immediately below (line 1632 post-patch) STILL has its own `command -v gh &>/dev/null` gate because `gh pr create` genuinely needs gh — only the rebase block's gate became dead.
bugs.fix: Drop the `&& command -v gh &>/dev/null` clause from the auto-rebase guard so the block becomes `if $AUTO_REBASE; then`. Inside the block, `git fetch -q origin "$BASE_BRANCH"` and `rebase_with_wolf_resolve "$REPO_ROOT" "origin/$BASE_BRANCH"` work on any system with git — no gh required. Behaviour for users with gh installed is unchanged (the block still fires). Behaviour for users without gh changes from 'silently skipped, PR not fast-forward-mergeable' to 'rebase runs, PR is fast-forward-mergeable' — the documented intent of --no-rebase remaining as the user-facing opt-out, and the existing `git fetch ... 2>/dev/null` failure path still handles repos without origin configured. Verified by inspection of lines 1582-1614: only line 1584 (the gate) referenced gh; every line inside the block uses git or string ops. `bash -n` passes; `grep 'command -v gh' scripts/run-sessions.sh` post-patch shows the only remaining match is the auto-PR block's own legitimate gate.

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh:1644-1650 auto-PR existing-PR branch only pushed when REBASE_RESULT=="ok". The symmetric new-PR branch immediately below (lines 1655-1661) handled three cases — no upstream (push -u), rebase ok (force-with-lease), or commits ahead of @{u} (plain push) — but the existing-PR branch covered only the rebase-ok case. So when a re-run produced new local commits AND the PR already existed AND REBASE_RESULT was anything other than "ok" (`--no-rebase` → "skipped", offline fetch → "fetch-failed", non-.wolf rebase conflict → "conflict"), the runner printed "PR already exists: <url>" and skipped the push entirely. New session commits stayed local, the remote epic/<branch> stayed at the prior tip, and the existing PR kept showing stale state with no diagnostic naming the missed push.
bugs.root_cause: Asymmetric audit gap with the new-PR push block. bug-204..210 audit chain repeatedly closed paths where a base-branch consumer or origin-preference fell through silently; the parallel push-on-existing-PR consumer was the unaudited site. The new-PR block correctly handled all three push shapes (`push -u` for fresh, `--force-with-lease` for rebased, plain `push` for fast-forward), but the existing-PR block kept its single-case `if REBASE_RESULT == ok` guard from when the auto-rebase was the only way new history landed. After the bug-208 audit moved every PR-target consumer to BASE_BRANCH and bug-210 removed the dead gh gate, this remained the last guard in the auto-PR block that didn't reflect what the code below it actually needed to do — push commits the remote doesn't have yet.
bugs.fix: Add an `elif` mirroring the new-PR block's third arm: when REBASE_RESULT != "ok" but `git log '@{u}..HEAD' --oneline` is non-empty, plain-push to fast-forward the remote. Wrapped in `|| warn` so a failed push surfaces a diagnostic instead of crashing under set -e (matching the existing force-with-lease site's failure handling). Verified by inspection: pre-patch a `--no-rebase` resume run with new local commits and an open PR exited with the PR untouched; post-patch the same invocation pushes the new commits and the existing PR shows the latest state. No behavioral change for the common case (REBASE_RESULT=="ok"; force-with-lease still fires first).

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh:1679-1685 new-PR push block had three push arms but only the middle one had any failure handler — and even that one's fallback (`|| git push`) could itself crash. The first arm (`git push -u origin "$CURRENT_BRANCH"`, the no-upstream / fresh-branch case) and the third arm (`git push`, the commits-ahead-of-@{u} case after `--no-rebase` / fetch-failed / non-.wolf rebase conflict) were both bare. Under `set -euo pipefail`, any push failure (transient network blip, push permission denied, pre-receive hook rejection, large-file / LFS rejection, force-push lease invalidated, server-side timeout) crashed the runner mid-PR-creation, BYPASSING (a) `gh pr create` immediately below at line 1743 (which has `|| true` to swallow gh's own non-zero exits) and (b) the post-PR cleanup at lines 1758-1819 (trunk worktree removal via `git worktree remove --force` then `rm -rf` fallback, per-session worktree leak detection, `cleanup_merged_epic_branches` stale-branch sweep). The user's symptoms: a low-level `git push` stderr line surfaces with no runner-level diagnostic naming the auto-PR phase as the cause; no PR exists; the trunk worktree at `.epic-worktrees/<repo>/epic--<name>/` leaks (every subsequent run hits the 'Reusing trunk worktree' branch at line 951 and inherits whatever stale state was in it); stale `epic/*` branches from prior runs whose PRs were merged or closed are NOT swept by `cleanup_merged_epic_branches` (line 1819) because the trap only kills subshell jobs and does not run the post-PR cleanup. Asymmetric with the bug-211 fix's symmetric site — the existing-PR push block immediately above (lines 1668-1674) wraps EVERY push arm in `2>&1 | tail -5 || warn "..."` so a push failure surfaces as a warn diagnostic and the runner continues past the auto-PR phase to do its cleanup; the new-PR push block had the same code shape but missed the same failure-handler discipline. The bug-211 commit message named this gap implicitly when it said 'every push site that expects to update an existing remote ref must reflect the actual local-vs-remote state, not a single REBASE_RESULT branch the ok-case happens to satisfy' — but it stopped at the existing-PR block; the new-PR block had a similar set of REBASE_RESULT/upstream branches and similar bare-push-on-failure exposure that the audit chain didn't reach. Same audit class as bug-077 (auto-resolve commit failure crashing the runner mid-wave under set -e, fixed by wrapping the commit in an `if`) and bug-211 (asymmetric existing-PR vs new-PR push handling): every command whose failure SHOULD be a warn-level diagnostic and not a runner-aborting fault must end with `|| warn` (or another non-failing terminator) under set -e, especially when the next code path (cleanup, post-PR teardown) is load-bearing for the user's workflow.
bugs.root_cause: The new-PR push block at lines 1679-1685 had three branches selecting the right `git push` shape based on upstream / REBASE_RESULT / commits-ahead state — mirroring the bug-211 existing-PR block's three-arm shape — but only the middle arm (REBASE_RESULT=="ok") had any failure handling, and even that handling was a fallback `|| git push` that itself can fail and crash the runner under set -e. The other two arms (no-upstream `push -u`, and commits-ahead `push`) were truly bare. Under set -euo pipefail, push failure on either bare arm aborts the runner before `gh pr create`'s `|| true` (line 1743) can swallow its own failures and before the post-PR cleanup at lines 1758-1819 can run. The EXIT trap (`_on_exit` at line 491) only walks JOB_PIDS to SIGKILL in-flight session subshells and kills the UI process — it does NOT run the trunk worktree removal, per-session leak detection, or `cleanup_merged_epic_branches` sweep, all of which live in the post-PR sequential code path. So a push failure that should be a warn-level diagnostic (the user wanted a PR; the push step failed; we should report it and continue tearing down) instead leaves the user with an opaque git error AND a leaked trunk worktree AND uncleaned stale epic/* branches. Symmetric audit gap to bug-211: bug-211 fixed the parallel push-on-existing-PR block to handle three push shapes (force-with-lease, plain-push fast-forward via `@{u}..HEAD`, and the no-op when ahead-count is zero) and made every push end in `|| warn`, but the parallel new-PR block's three push shapes (no-upstream `push -u`, force-with-lease, plain-push) were left with their pre-bug-211 shape — only the middle arm had any failure handling, and that handling was incomplete.
bugs.fix: Mirror the bug-211 existing-PR block's failure-handler discipline: every push arm in the new-PR block now ends with `2>&1 | tail -5 || warn "..."` so push failure surfaces as a warn-level diagnostic and the runner proceeds past the auto-PR phase to its cleanup. (1) The no-upstream arm becomes `git push -u origin "$CURRENT_BRANCH" 2>&1 | tail -5 || warn "Initial push failed; PR creation will likely fail"`. (2) The REBASE_RESULT=="ok" arm becomes a chained fallback: `git push --force-with-lease 2>&1 | tail -5 || git push 2>&1 | tail -5 || warn "Force-push and fallback push both failed; PR will likely show stale state"` — preserves the original force-with-lease-then-plain-push fallback semantics, but adds the terminal `|| warn` so a double-failure warns rather than crashes. (3) The commits-ahead arm becomes `git push 2>&1 | tail -5 || warn "Push failed; new commits may not appear in the PR"`. The `2>&1 | tail -5` pattern matches the existing-PR block exactly: pipefail makes the pipe's exit status reflect the underlying git push (the bug-211 lines already rely on this), `tail -5` limits the noise to the last 5 lines, and `|| warn` catches the failure as a non-fatal warning. After the change, gh pr create at line 1743 still runs (with its existing `|| true`) and reports its own warn at line 1747 if the missing commits make it fail; the post-PR cleanup at lines 1758-1819 always runs to completion; the runner exits cleanly even when the push step failed. No behavioural change for the happy path (push succeeds → tail -5 shows 'To origin … <range>', the `|| warn` short-circuits, runner continues). `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` passes; visual diff confirms the three new arms match the existing-PR block's idiom. Same minimum-scope discipline as bug-211: the fix is purely additive failure handling on three lines; no behavioral change to which push command runs in which case.

bugs.error_message: scripts/lib/epic-git.sh:117 (auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts) and scripts/wolf-merge/resolve-wolf.sh:54 read `git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U` without `-c core.quotePath=false`. Git's default quotePath=true wraps any path with bytes >= 0x80 (and whitespace / control chars) as a C-string-escaped, double-quoted line: a conflicted `.wolf/foo—bar.md` (em-dash, accented letter, CJK, smart quote) prints as `".wolf/foo\342\200\224bar.md"` — leading byte `"`, not `.`. The downstream `grep -Ev '^\.wolf/'` filter then misclassifies the quoted line as a non-wolf conflict (every quoted-and-escaped path fails the literal `^\.wolf/` test), the helper returns 1 (caller bails as if a real code conflict existed), and the .wolf/-only auto-resolve is silently disabled for any session whose conflicted .wolf/ file has a non-ASCII char in its filename. Two surfaces affected: (1) `auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts` is invoked from BOTH the runner's wave-merge phase (run-sessions.sh:1383, where bailing as 'non-wolf conflicts' falsely trips bug-087's case-1 branch and aborts the entire epic with `MERGE CONFLICT merging session NN into trunk`) AND from `rebase_with_wolf_resolve` (epic-git.sh:175, called from the auto-rebase block at run-sessions.sh:1618, where bailing aborts the rebase via `git rebase --abort` and surfaces 'non-.wolf/ conflicts require manual resolution on the PR' — even though the only conflicts are .wolf/ files); (2) `resolve-wolf.sh` is the one-shot user-facing resolver invoked manually after a conflict, and runs under `set -euo pipefail` with NO `|| true` on its `git checkout` / `git add` arms (lines 81-86), so even if a user explicitly passes `--ours`/`--theirs` to force-resolve a .wolf/ conflict with a non-ASCII filename, the script first prints the misleading 'Refusing to auto-resolve: non-.wolf/ conflicts present:' (line 62) naming the quoted-and-escaped path back at them, then exits 1 without resolving anything. The user has no signal that the non-ASCII filename is the cause; the printed path looks like a real code path until they stare at it and realise the leading `"` and `\342...` bytes are git's quoted form. Same audit class as bug-104/107 — validate-session-deliverables.py's `_read_diff` had the exact same shape (`git diff --name-status` consumed by a string-equality compare) and was patched to add `-c core.quotePath=false`, but the audit chain stopped at the validator and missed the two symmetric merge-driver call sites. The runner happens not to fire the bug in OpenWolf's stock layout (anatomy.md, buglog.json, cerebrum.md, OPENWOLF.md, identity.md, hooks/_session.json, scripts/*.sh, scripts/*.py — every name is ASCII), so the audit chain didn't surface it from production telemetry; but any consuming repo that adds a non-ASCII .wolf/ file (notes, designqc-captures named after a feature with a smart quote, manually-exported logs with em-dashes) silently falls into the bug. The third call site of the same `git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U` pattern, at epic-git.sh:181 inside `rebase_with_wolf_resolve`'s loop, only checks `| grep -q .` for any non-empty output — quoted or not, the line is non-empty so the probe correctly detects the still-present conflict; that site does not need the fix. Symmetric audit pattern: every consumer of `git diff` output that string-compares the path field (literal equality, prefix filter, glob match, hash-key lookup) MUST pass `-c core.quotePath=false` or git's default quoting will silently mismatch any non-ASCII path. Validated empirically: created `.wolf/foo—bar.md` with conflicting edits on two branches, ran the merge, observed pre-fix `auto_resolve_wolf_conflicts` returns 1 with `non_wolf=[".wolf/foo\342\200\224bar.md"]`; post-fix returns 0 with the conflict auto-resolved and `git diff --diff-filter=U` empty. `bash -n` passes on both files.
bugs.root_cause: git's default `core.quotePath=true` wraps non-ASCII path bytes in C-string-escaped, double-quoted lines (`".wolf/foo\xxx"`). The `^\.wolf/` filter checks the literal `.` character at start-of-line, but a quoted path starts with `"` instead. Same audit class as bug-104/107, missed at the two merge-driver call sites.
bugs.fix: Add `-c core.quotePath=false` to both `git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U` calls so paths arrive raw. epic-git.sh:117 becomes `git -C "$workdir" -c core.quotePath=false diff --name-only --diff-filter=U`; resolve-wolf.sh:54 becomes `git -c core.quotePath=false diff --name-only --diff-filter=U`. The downstream regex compare and the subsequent `git checkout`/`git add` pathspec calls all accept raw UTF-8, so no further changes are needed.

bugs.error_message: globs_overlap's _stem() helper returned a trailing slash for single-star patterns ('src/foo/*' → 'src/foo/'), defeating _is_under's `parent + '/'` join (a child 'src/foo/bar' does NOT start with 'src/foo//'), so the directory-prefix containment fallback under-detects same-wave touches overlap whenever fnmatch can't catch the pair on its own.
bugs.root_cause: epic-dag.py:786 used a two-pass `g.split('**')[0].rstrip('/').rstrip('*')`. For 'src/foo/*': the first rstrip('/') is a no-op (string ends with '*'), the second rstrip('*') strips the star and exposes the underlying '/', leaving a final stem of 'src/foo/' with a trailing slash. _is_under(child, parent) at line 793 checks `child.startswith(parent + '/')`; with parent='src/foo/' the join becomes 'src/foo//' and any real child path falls through to False. fnmatch saves most overlap detections (single `*` matches across `/` in fnmatch's grammar), but pure-literal vs single-star pairs that only the directory-prefix branch can flag silently under-detect overlap and the parallel-safety check in find_overlaps stops protecting them. Same audit class as the bug-022/bug-033 literal-prefix glob bugs flagged in the function's own comment — the fix needs to compose with the separator-anchored compare.
bugs.fix: Replace the two-pass rstrip chain with a single `rstrip('/*')` that strips ANY trailing '/' or '*' together. Output is identical for 'src/foo', 'src/foo/', and 'src/foo/**' (the cases the original handled); only single-star patterns like 'src/foo/*' change (now 'src/foo' instead of 'src/foo/'). Verified via test fixture: 'src/foo/*' → 'src/foo' (was 'src/foo/'); '/' → '' (unchanged); '**' → '' (unchanged); 'src/foo/*.cs' → 'src/foo/*.cs' (unchanged — known limitation of the lossy-stem heuristic). bash -n passes; python3 -m py_compile passes.

bugs.error_message: Auto-PR existing-PR check at run-sessions.sh:1643 used `gh pr view <branch>` which falls back to the most recently-created CLOSED/MERGED PR when no open PR exists on the branch — silently re-opening the stale-merged-PR-as-existing-PR branch and skipping `gh pr create` for the new run.
bugs.root_cause:
scripts/run-sessions.sh:1643 read `EXISTING_PR="$(gh pr view "$CURRENT_BRANCH" --json url -q '.url' 2>/dev/null || true)"`. Verified empirically against this very repo: `gh pr view epic/epic-next-features --json state,url` returns `MERGED https://github.com/aramirez087/epic-toolkit/pull/1` even though there is no open PR on that branch — `gh pr view` documents this fallback (it shows the latest PR matching the branch, regardless of state). The downstream check `if [[ -n "$EXISTING_PR" ]]` then took the branch as 'PR already exists', the runner printed `PR already exists: `, force-pushed (or push-fallbacked, post-bug-211) to the branch — which on a MERGED PR does NOT reopen it; new commits land on the remote branch but no PR ever wraps them — and skipped `gh pr create` at line 1759 because the existing-PR branch had already been taken. User copy-pasted the printed URL expecting their new work and found the prior merged PR. Same audit pattern as cleanup_merged_epic_branches at epic-git.sh:253, which already uses `gh pr list --head
--state all` and case-matches the state explicitly to differentiate OPEN/DRAFT (keep) from MERGED/CLOSED (prune); the auto-PR check at this site needs the same OPEN-only filter so a stale merged PR does not pre-empt the new-PR path. Bug-211's commit message ('an open PR — silently dropped the push') implicitly assumed `EXISTING_PR` only captured open PRs, but the underlying gh pr view behavior includes merged/closed PRs — the assumption was load-bearing and unverified.
bugs.fix: Switch from `gh pr view <branch>` to `gh pr list --head <branch> --state open --json url -q '.[0].url'`. This filters to OPEN PRs only (gh pr list's --state defaults to open and accepts the explicit value too); `.[0].url` returns empty when no open PR exists, and the empty EXISTING_PR falls cleanly through to the `else` branch where `gh pr create` opens a fresh PR for the new work. Mirrors the existing `gh pr list --head ... --state all` pattern at cleanup_merged_epic_branches (epic-git.sh:253) — same primitive, different state filter. Verified against the merged-only branch: pre-fix returned the MERGED PR's URL (mis-reusing it as 'existing'); post-fix returns empty (correctly falling through to gh pr create). bash -n passes.

bugs.error_message: Significant refactor of
