bugs.fix: Rewrote 32→44 lines (20 removed)

bugs.error_message: run-sessions.sh:1689-1695 existing-PR push block was missing the no-`@{u}` arm that the symmetric new-PR block at line 1712 already had. When the local branch had no upstream tracking configured (most common cause: trunk worktree created fresh from BASE_BRANCH at line 959 while an open PR for the same branch name predated this run), all three existing arms failed silently: `push --force-with-lease` errors with `fatal: The current branch <name> has no upstream branch.` and the `|| warn` reports the misleading 'Force-push failed; PR may still show conflicts' instead of naming the upstream-missing cause; `git log '@{u}..HEAD'` cannot resolve `@{u}`, the `2>/dev/null` swallows the error, the elif test is False, no push fires, and the runner reports `PR already exists: <url>` with no diagnostic — the user copy-pastes the URL expecting their new commits and finds the prior PR's stale state.
bugs.root_cause: Symmetric audit gap to bug-204..212. Bug-211's commit message said 'Three cases' (REBASE_RESULT=ok / commits-ahead / nothing-ahead) and assumed `@{u}` was always configured by the time the existing-PR branch fired. The new-PR block immediately below ALREADY had the no-`@{u}` arm at line 1712 because brand-new branches obviously need upstream tracking — but the same condition fires in the existing-PR scenario when (a) the local branch was just created from BASE_BRANCH (line 959 path) and a remote PR for the same branch name predated this run from a prior runner instance / a different machine / manual `gh pr create`, (b) the user manually deleted the local branch (`git branch -D`) but kept the remote PR open, or (c) `git worktree add` reused a local branch that itself had never had upstream tracking set. In all three sub-cases EXISTING_PR resolves (the gh query is against the remote, not local) but the local branch has no `@{u}`. Bug-211 fixed the case-3 arm (commits-ahead-of-`@{u}`) but didn't audit case 1 (no-`@{u}`); the same audit class as bug-204..212 — every push site that expects to update an existing remote ref must reflect the actual local-vs-remote state, including the 'no upstream tracking configured' case.
bugs.fix: Add a fourth arm at the top of the existing-PR if/elif chain that detects no-`@{u}` and runs `push -u origin "$CURRENT_BRANCH" 2>&1 | tail -5 || warn ...`, mirroring the new-PR block's first arm at line 1712. The new arm goes FIRST (highest priority) because without upstream tracking the next two arms both fail. The fallback `|| warn` includes guidance naming the most likely cause (local branch created from BASE_BRANCH while remote PR has divergent history → non-FF rejection) and the inspection command (`git log $CURRENT_BRANCH..origin/$CURRENT_BRANCH`) so the user has a clear next step. The `2>&1 | tail -5` shows git's actual error (e.g., 'Updates were rejected because the tip of your current branch is behind') verbatim. Updates the leading comment to enumerate FOUR cases, names the bug-211 audit gap explicitly, and references the symmetric new-PR arm. `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the happy path (existing-PR with @{u} configured + REBASE_RESULT=ok / commits-ahead / nothing-ahead all behave identically); change is scoped to the previously-silent gap when @{u} is unconfigured.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:1562-1575 success-path scaffolding-cleanup commit body claimed `Removes docs/claude-sessions/${_EPIC_SLUG}/ and any per-epic roadmap handoffs` regardless of whether `--keep-session-docs` actually preserved those files. With `--keep-session-docs` the dual-removal block at lines 1546-1560 is skipped, so the only staged deletions at the commit site are the dotfile artefacts swept at line 1539 (.session-*-plan.md, .epic-*.json, STATUS_FILE, DAG_PLAN_FILE, .lock, .tmp). The OK message branch at line 1570 already differentiated the two cases ('session docs preserved via --keep-session-docs' vs 'PR diff contains only product code'), but the commit body was a single template that fired in BOTH branches. Reviewers reading the commit message in `git log` / the PR's commit list got a wrong picture of what shipped: a 'chore: remove epic session scaffolding' commit whose tree shows the docs/claude-sessions/* tree intact. A future operator bisecting for 'when did session scaffolding last get removed' lands on a commit that didn't actually remove it, and an automated PR-description scraper that pattern-matches the commit subject inflates a misleading scaffolding-removal claim into the PR body.
bugs.root_cause: Asymmetric audit gap with the OK-message branch. The branch-on-flag pattern was applied at line 1570 (OK message) but not at line 1563 (commit body) — the commit body was a single static `-m` argument, while the OK message used an `if $KEEP_SESSION_DOCS; then ... else ... fi` to vary its text. Same audit class as bug-205 (success-path 'Next step' advice naming the wrong base branch — a single-template user-facing string that didn't reflect the actual code path) and bug-211/212/217 (push-site messages that hard-coded one case while the surrounding code branched on local-vs-remote state): every user-facing string the runner emits must reflect the actual code path it took, not a single template that happens to match the most common case.
bugs.fix: Branch the `git commit -m` body on `$KEEP_SESSION_DOCS` to mirror the OK-message branch immediately below. The keep-session-docs branch uses subject `chore: remove epic orchestrator artefacts` and a body naming the dotfile artefacts that were actually swept (`.session-*-plan.md, .epic-*.json, status and lock files`) plus an explicit note that session prompts and roadmap handoffs are preserved. The default branch keeps the original subject `chore: remove epic session scaffolding` and body verbatim. The OK message at line 1570 stays unchanged — it already differentiated the two cases correctly. `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the happy path (no --keep-session-docs: identical commit body and OK message); change is scoped to the previously-misleading flag-on path. Same minimum-scope discipline as bug-205/211/212/217 — the fix is purely additive branching on `$KEEP_SESSION_DOCS`; the dotfile-cleanup loop at line 1536-1541 and the dual-removal block at line 1546-1560 are unchanged. The narrative buglog entry is appended at bug-218 per the OpenWolf auto-hooks convention; tags include `found-by-bug-hunt`, `audit-gap`, `commit-message`, `symmetric-to-bug-205`, `keep-session-docs` to flag this as the symmetric closing of the bug-205 audit chain on the cleanup-commit-body side.

bugs.error_message:
scripts/lib/epic-git.sh:253 (cleanup_merged_epic_branches) read `.[0].state` from `gh pr list --head
--state all` to decide whether to delete a stale local epic/* branch. `.[0]` is the MOST-RECENT PR by createdAt — so a more-recently-closed PR (e.g. user opened a duplicate-base PR that was later closed, a stale draft auto-closed by a bot, or a second PR retargeted to a different base and then abandoned) hid an older still-OPEN PR against a different base. The case branch matched MERGED|CLOSED and `git branch -D` deleted the local branch while a remote PR was actively using it. The remote branch survives because the open PR keeps it on origin (the user can always re-fetch), but the local one is gone with no diagnostic; any uncommitted local work on that branch only survives in the reflog. Symmetric audit class to bug-215 in run-sessions.sh:1692, where `gh pr view ` likewise returned the most-recent CLOSED/MERGED PR when an OPEN one existed against a different base — that fix switched to `gh pr list --state open` so an OPEN PR pre-empts a more-recent CLOSED one. The bug-215 audit chain reached the auto-PR existing-PR detector but stopped before this cleanup site; cleanup_merged_epic_branches kept the original `.[0]`-takes-most-recent shape.
bugs.root_cause:
`gh pr list --head
--state all --json state -q '.[0].state'` returns the most-recently-created PR's state, not whether ANY PR for the head branch is still open. With multi-base PR history (rare but legitimate — e.g. branch was re-targeted between develop and main, or a duplicate PR was opened against a different base and closed), the most-recent state can be MERGED/CLOSED while an older PR against a different base remains OPEN. The case branch then falsely classifies the branch as safe-to-delete.
bugs.fix: Cache `git remote get-url origin` once at the top of the function (it was already re-queried per-iteration in the inline subshell) and add an explicit `--state open` pre-check: `gh -R "$origin_url" pr list --head "$br" --state open --json id -q 'length'` returns the count of open PRs across every base. If the count is >= 1 (regex `^[1-9][0-9]*$` matches), continue without checking further — never delete a branch with any open PR. Otherwise fall through to the existing `--state all` query, which is now guaranteed to return only MERGED/CLOSED/empty (since OPEN was excluded). The existing case branches stay correct without further changes. Use jq's `length` over `.[0].id` so the empty-array case yields the literal '0' (a value the regex safely rejects) instead of jq's `null` rendering, which gh's `-q` flag emits as an empty string. Add a leading docblock that names the symmetric bug-215 audit gap, the user-facing impact (local branch deleted while remote PR still uses it), and the multi-PR scenarios that trigger it, so future audits don't re-introduce the same shape. `bash -n scripts/lib/epic-git.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the single-PR happy path (one MERGED PR → length=0 → fall-through → case matches MERGED → delete; one OPEN PR → length=1 → continue, identical to pre-fix behaviour where .[0].state=OPEN fell through the case unmatched).

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:1692 auto-PR existing-PR detector queried `gh pr list --head "$CURRENT_BRANCH" --state open --json url -q '.[0].url'` without `--base "$BASE_BRANCH"`. The query returned ANY OPEN PR for this head — across every base — and `.[0].url` (sorted by createdAt descending) took the most-recent. Multi-base scenario: user previously ran `--base develop --branch epic/foo` and the PR is still OPEN against develop, then re-runs the same epic with `--base main --branch epic/foo` (different intent — rebased redirection, different release-train base, or a fresh main-targeted run after the develop PR was abandoned but not closed). The unfiltered query found the develop PR, the runner reported `PR already exists: <develop-url>`, pushed to epic/foo, and the develop PR silently absorbed the new commits — no main PR was ever created, and no diagnostic named the cause. User copy-pasted the printed URL expecting the new work and found the develop PR holding it.
bugs.root_cause:
`gh pr list --head
` filters by HEAD branch only. GitHub allows multiple OPEN PRs from the same head against different bases (one of the few legitimate cross-base flows). The runner's `gh pr create --base "$BASE_BRANCH"` call below at line 1820 (post-bug-208) creates against the user's actual --base, but the existing-PR detector at line 1692 didn't reflect the same target. When BASE_BRANCH differs between runs of the same --branch (the user changed --base or resumed without --base while the original used a non-default --base), the unfiltered detector finds the wrong PR. Same audit class as bug-122/204/207/208 (every consumer of 'the base branch the user wanted' must use BASE_BRANCH, not gh's repo default) and bug-215 (every existing-PR detector must reflect the user's actual intent, not just the head branch).
bugs.fix: Add `--base "$BASE_BRANCH"` to the `gh pr list` call so the existing-PR detector and the new-PR `gh pr create` call below at line 1820 agree on the target. The detector now matches a PR only when both head AND base coincide with the user's intent — multi-base PRs on the same head branch correctly fall through to the `else` arm where `gh pr create` opens a fresh PR against the requested base. Update the leading docblock to enumerate the multi-base scenario and reference the bug-122/204/207/208 audit chain for consumers of BASE_BRANCH, so future audits don't re-introduce the same shape. `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the single-base happy path (user runs the same --base on every run: same PR matches, identical detector behaviour). The fix is purely additive — one new flag passed to `gh pr list`; the surrounding push/no-upstream/force-with-lease branches and the new-PR `else` block are unchanged.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sprint.sh:199 Python sprint-config validator rejected \t and \n in dir/model fields but missed \r — Windows-edited sprint.json (Notepad / autocrlf=true / IDE forcing CRLF on save) silently smuggled \r through to bash, surfacing as misleading 'Epic directory not found: foo\r' or '--model sonnet\r' failures that did not name CRLF as the cause.
bugs.root_cause: Audit-gap symmetric to bug-024/025 (CRLF normalization in YAML/markdown parsers). The audit chain reached the YAML and markdown parsers — parse_frontmatter normalizes CRLF, extract_prompt's awk strips trailing \r per record — but missed the JSON sprint-config consumer in run-sprint.sh. The Python parser at line 199 only rejected \t (TSV-delimiter conflict) and \n (line-delimiter conflict). \r passed through verbatim because (1) Python's json.loads preserves it inside string values, (2) the producer's `print(d + '\t' + m)` emits the \r as part of the field, (3) the bash side's `read -r` does NOT strip trailing CR (the -r flag disables backslash interpretation, not CR stripping — that requires explicit `${var%$'\r'}` or `tr -d '\r'`), and (4) every downstream consumer (BRANCH assignment, `--branch "$BRANCH"` arg to run-sessions.sh, `--model "$_model"` arg, `[[ -d "$_d" ]]` directory check) treats \r as part of the literal value. Three failure modes: (a) BRANCH="epic/foo\r" passed to run-sessions.sh — git worktree creation may succeed (\r is technically allowed in branch names per check-ref-format) but worktree path renders weirdly and downstream `gh pr create --base $BRANCH` may fail with an opaque ref-not-found error; (b) _dir="docs/claude-sessions/foo\r" — `[[ -d $_d ]]` returns false, error message reads 'Epic directory not found: docs/claude-sessions/foo<CR>' which most terminals render as 'Epic directory not found: docs/claude-sessions/' (the CR overwrites the start of the line) hiding the actual cause; (c) _model="sonnet\r" — passed to `--model sonnet\r`, which trips epic-dag.py:422's `^[A-Za-z0-9._/+:-]+$` regex (\r is not in the class) emitting a confusing 'model={!r} contains characters that break the SESSION row' error pointing at the SERIALIZER instead of the JSON. Same defect class as bug-097/142/146/164/165/170/171/172/173/175 — every consumer of a typed value must reject parser-produced shapes it can't interpret AND name the parser cause so the user fixes the JSON, not the symptom.
bugs.fix: Extend the rejection check at scripts/run-sprint.sh:192/216 to include \r alongside the existing \t/\n rejection, with a YAML-aware diagnostic naming the Windows-editor cause and the LF re-save remediation. Also add a parallel check for the top-level 'branch' field (previously unguarded — the BRANCH carrier was the worst-case vector since BRANCH propagates through every downstream git/gh invocation including the trunk worktree path, the per-session worktree paths via BRANCH_SANITIZED, every `git -C "$REPO_ROOT"` ref reference, and the eventual `gh pr create --base "$BASE_BRANCH" --head "$BRANCH"` call). Use `any(ch in <field> for ch in ('\t', '\n', '\r'))` so the same audit pattern can be extended uniformly when the next non-printable surface (vertical tab, form feed, NUL) is discovered. Diagnostic includes repr() of the offending value so the user can see the literal \r in the output even when their terminal would otherwise render it as line-overwrite. `bash -n scripts/run-sprint.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the LF happy path (sprint configs without CR pass through unchanged); change is scoped to the previously-silent gap when CR is present.

bugs.error_message: scripts/lib/epic-git.sh:222-235 session_completed_on_branch silently re-introduced bug-080 (false-positive 'Session N already done' on prior epic's `feat: Session N` commit on base history) when neither origin/$BASE_BRANCH nor local $BASE_BRANCH resolved — base_ref stayed empty, the rev-range scoping was bypassed, and `git log --format='%s' $branch` walked the entire branch history including BASE_BRANCH's ancestry.
bugs.root_cause: Audit-gap closure for bug-080. The original bug-080 fix scoped the subjects walk to `${base_ref}..${branch}` so prior epics' `feat: Session N` subjects in BASE_BRANCH history could not falsely match. But the resolution chain at lines 222-234 had a silent fallback: if BOTH `origin/$BASE_BRANCH` AND local `$BASE_BRANCH` failed to resolve, base_ref stayed '' and the assignment `if [[ -n "$base_ref" ]]; then rev_range="${base_ref}..${branch}"; fi` was simply skipped, leaving rev_range='$branch' — the EXACT bare-branch shape bug-080 was patched against. Three real-world conditions trigger the unresolvable-base case: (1) BASE_BRANCH defaulted to 'main' via the run-sessions.sh:680-682 fallback (`git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` returned non-zero — no `origin` remote configured, or remote exists but `origin/HEAD` symbolic ref missing because the user cloned with `--no-tags` / a tool-managed mirror / a custom fetch refspec) but the repo actually uses `master`/`develop`/`trunk` and has no local `main`; (2) user passes `--base typo` (a misspelled or removed branch name) — neither `origin/typo` nor `typo` resolves; (3) repo is a brand-new init with no `origin` and no commits yet on what would become the default branch. In all three, base_ref='' silently degrades the bug-080 fix back to its pre-patch shape. The user-visible blast radius is the same as bug-080: any `feat: Session N` commit reachable from $branch's history (which includes everything in BASE_BRANCH because $branch was branched from it) makes session_completed_on_branch return 0, the per-session run is silently skipped at epic-session.sh:333, the merge phase merges an empty branch into trunk (no-op — the bootstrap commit's tree is already on trunk), MERGED_SESSIONS records the no-op as 'merged session NN', the final ALL_SESSIONS_DONE check at run-sessions.sh:1178 also returns true (same false-positive logic), the scaffolding-cleanup commit fires, the auto-PR is created — and the PR ships with NO actual session work because every session was silently skipped. Same defect class as bug-080 itself, bug-104/107/213 (`-c core.quotePath=false` audit chain) — every patched site must verify its precondition is satisfied, not assume the patch is unconditional. The in-memory `${SESSION_STATUS[$sid]:-} == done` short-circuit at line 212 still works correctly (it only matches sessions completed in THIS run), so the false-positive specifically affects RESUME scenarios where a prior run's sessions are looked up via the git log scan — the worst case is when the user resumes a previously-failed run and the runner thinks every session is already done, exiting with 'Epic completed successfully' having executed nothing.
bugs.fix: When base_ref is empty after the resolution attempts (both origin/$BASE_BRANCH and local $BASE_BRANCH failed), return 1 ('not completed') instead of falling through to the bare-branch walk. Conservative: a session re-runs (wasted compute is recoverable) instead of silently skipping (data loss — user thinks it ran when it didn't). The in-memory SESSION_STATUS check above the rev-range logic still short-circuits sessions completed in THIS run, so the only behavioural change is for sessions claimed-done by a previous run on a now-broken base setup — which is exactly the bug-080 false-positive surface. Restructure: hoist `local base_ref=""` out of the `if [[ -n BASE_BRANCH ]]` block so the post-block `if [[ -z "$base_ref" ]]; then return 1; fi` guard can see it, then unconditionally assign `rev_range="${base_ref}..${branch}"` (no longer needs the `if [[ -n base_ref ]]` wrap because the early return guarantees base_ref is non-empty by this point). Add a leading docblock naming the bug-080 audit lineage, the three real-world unresolvable-base conditions (no origin/HEAD + non-`main` default; --base typo; brand-new init), the user-visible blast radius (resume false-positive → silent skip → no-op merges → empty PR), and the in-memory short-circuit that limits the impact to RESUME scenarios so future audits don't re-introduce the bare-walk fallback as an 'optimization'. `bash -n scripts/lib/epic-git.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the happy path (BASE_BRANCH resolves: identical rev_range, identical subjects walk, identical match logic — the early return at the new `if [[ -z base_ref ]]` only fires when the original code's `if [[ -n base_ref ]]` was about to skip the rev_range assignment); change is scoped to the previously-silent gap.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:67 usage() ran sed -n '2,43p' "$0" but the comment header listing CLI options grew to line 46 — three options (--wave-timeout MINS, --fresh, -h/--help) were silently truncated from `bash run-sessions.sh --help` output. Users discovered --wave-timeout / --fresh by reading the source or by trial-and-error from error messages, and the help text appeared to be missing the standard -h/--help convention.
bugs.root_cause: Audit-gap symmetric to bug-205/218 (user-facing strings that hard-coded one shape while the surrounding code/headers grew). The sed line range `2,43p` was correct for the original header, but three options (--wave-timeout at line 44, --fresh at line 45, -h/--help at line 46) were appended without updating the range. The companion run-sprint.sh:64 `sed -n '2,47p'` was kept correct (line 47 IS the last comment line there), so the audit drift was specific to run-sessions.sh. Same defect class as bug-218 (commit body fired in BOTH branches while OK message branched correctly): every consumer of a hard-coded line range / case template must be re-examined when the surrounding code grows.
bugs.fix: Change `sed -n '2,43p'` to `sed -n '2,46p'` so the help output covers the full comment header. Add a leading docblock naming the audit lineage (bug-205/218) and the failure mode (silently truncated options) so future audits don't re-introduce the drift when more options are added — the convention is now explicit: keep the sed range in sync with the last `# ` line above `set -euo pipefail`. Verified by running `sed -n '2,46p' scripts/run-sessions.sh | sed 's/^# \{0,1\}//' | tail -10` — the last 10 lines now include all three previously-truncated options. `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` passes; no behavioural change for any code path other than --help, which now prints the additional three lines that were previously silent.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:1422 success-path printed `Next step  : gh pr create --base $BASE_BRANCH --head $BRANCH` UNCONDITIONALLY — even after AUTO_PR had successfully created a new PR (line 1351-1353) or pushed to an existing PR (line 1278-1297). Users running with the default AUTO_PR=true saw `Pull request created: <url>` immediately followed by `Next step: gh pr create ...` and either (a) ran the suggested command and got `gh: a pull request for branch 'X' into 'Y' already exists` from the duplicate, or (b) doubted whether the auto-PR actually succeeded and re-ran the script with --no-pr to be safe.
bugs.root_cause: Audit-gap symmetric to bug-205/218 — every user-facing string the runner emits must reflect the actual code path it took, not a single template that happens to match the most common case. The bug-205 fix corrected the BASE_BRANCH placeholder in the same line (it used to hard-code 'main') but the surrounding case-shape — that the message fires regardless of AUTO_PR success — was not in scope for that fix. Four exits land at line 1422: (1) AUTO_PR=false (--no-pr): outer `if $AUTO_PR && command -v gh` is false, no PR work happened, message is appropriate. (2) AUTO_PR=true but gh missing: elif `$AUTO_PR` warns and skips, message is appropriate. (3) AUTO_PR=true, gh found, on default branch: warn at line 1359 skips PR work, message is appropriate. (4) AUTO_PR=true, gh found, EXISTING_PR found OR PR_URL starts with http: a PR was successfully handled, message is MISLEADING — the user already has a PR they should review, and `gh pr create` would fail with a duplicate error or create a confusing second PR against the same branch. Same defect class as bug-218 (cleanup-commit body fired in BOTH branches while OK message branched correctly), bug-211/212/217 (push-site messages that hard-coded one case while the surrounding code branched on local-vs-remote state), and bug-205 (Next step naming the wrong base).
bugs.fix: Add an `AUTO_PR_HANDLED=false` flag initialized just before the auto-PR block at line 1269. Set it true at the two PR-handled sites: after `ok "PR already exists: $EXISTING_PR"` (line 1279) and after `ok "Pull request created: $PR_URL"` (line 1353). The flag stays false in all four cases where the user genuinely needs the manual `gh pr create` advice (AUTO_PR=false, gh missing, on-default-branch skip, and `gh pr create` failure where PR_URL didn't start with http). Gate the `Next step` line at 1422 on `if ! $AUTO_PR_HANDLED; then ... fi`. The Branch-ready message above (line 1419) stays unconditional — the user always wants to know what branch is ready. Add a leading docblock at the AUTO_PR_HANDLED initialization and at the conditional naming the audit lineage (bug-205/218), the four code paths and which trigger the conditional, and the misleading-output failure mode so future audits don't re-introduce the unconditional shape. Note that EXISTING_PR-with-failed-push still sets AUTO_PR_HANDLED=true: the PR exists on remote regardless of whether the local push succeeded, and the user's correct action is to fix the push (the `|| warn` already names this) and review the existing PR — not to call `gh pr create`. `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` passes; no behavioural change for the non-AUTO_PR-success paths (4 of 5 success-path exits print identical output); change is scoped to the previously-misleading flag-on path. Same minimum-scope discipline as bug-205/218 — the fix is purely additive: one variable initialization, two assignments, and one if-guard around the existing message.

Verified by reading run-sessions.sh:689-691: when the user passes no --base, BASE_BRANCH is set from `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` parsed via `sed 's@^refs/remotes/origin/@@'`, falling back to literal `main` only if that produces an empty string. bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bugs 254/255/257: when the sprint command was added (commit 64078a0), the Multi-epic example in the per-command instruction files was not updated to reference it. bugs.error_message: docs/epic-guide.md:17-19 'Claude Code marketplace installs expose namespaced commands: /epic-toolkit:epic.generate and /epic-toolkit:epic. OpenCode uses the un-namespaced equivalents: /epic.generate and /epic.' enumerated only TWO of the three slash-command pairs the toolkit ships. The third pair, /epic-toolkit:sprint and /sprint (the multi-epic orchestrator that runs N epics back-to-back on a shared trunk and opens ONE PR for the whole sprint), was completely absent from this paragraph even though it is the documented and preferred path for multi-epic work everywhere else (README.md after bug-254, commands/epic.md+.opencode/commands/epic.md after bug-259, CLAUDE.md+AGENTS.md after bug-257). Users reading the canonical long-form guide right after install would learn the toolkit ships exactly two commands per CLI and never discover /sprint exists, or worse, would assume the manual two-invocation workaround documented further down (line ~189) is the only way to chain epics.
bugs.root_cause: Same audit class as bugs 254/255/257/259: when /sprint was added (commit 64078a0) the developer updated the long-form sections that enumerate workflows but missed the introductory namespaced-vs-unnamespaced commands paragraph at lines 17-19 of epic-guide.md. The earlier sweep (bug-255) patched the Multi-Epic Workflows section deeper in the same file (~line 194) to surface /sprint, but the introductory two-line summary at the top of the file kept its original two-command enumeration. The introductory paragraph is what most readers see first when they hit the guide from the README link, so a stale enumeration here outweighs the corrected references later in the same document.
bugs.fix: Rewrote docs/epic-guide.md:17-19 to enumerate all three command pairs symmetrically — Claude Code: /epic-toolkit:epic.generate, /epic-toolkit:epic, /epic-toolkit:sprint (with a parenthetical naming the multi-epic orchestrator + one-PR behaviour); OpenCode: /epic.generate, /epic, /sprint. Doc-only; no behavioural impact.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:63 `warn() { echo -e "${YELLOW}[epic]${NC} $*"; }` wrote warnings to STDOUT, while `err()` on the very next line correctly used `>&2`. The companion script scripts/run-sprint.sh:60 defines the same helper as `warn() { echo -e "${YELLOW}[sprint]${NC} $*" >&2; }` — i.e. with the redirect — so the two top-level entry points of the toolkit disagreed about whether warnings are diagnostic output. Concrete consequences: (a) users redirecting `bash run-sessions.sh ... 2>diagnostics.log` to capture problem signals would lose every warn() line (24+ call sites: timeout-not-available, --no-worktree forces --max-parallel 1, max-parallel clamping, stale-worktree-in-use, deliverables-validation-failed, push-failed, force-push-failed, worktree-cleanup-leaks, gh-cli-not-found, etc.); (b) warns interleave with the [EPIC_RESULT_START]/[EPIC_RESULT_END] structured-output block that command files (commands/epic.md, .opencode/commands/epic.md) parse from the runner's stdout to extract `RESULT_FILE=`, exit_code, error_type — every warn() emitted in the post-write_epic_result cleanup phase (run-sessions.sh:1396-1429: trunk worktree removal failure, per-session worktree leak, 'git worktree said: ...' tail) lands inside the same stream as the result block; (c) inconsistency with run-sprint.sh, which also captures and forwards run-sessions.sh's stdout, means a sprint user sees warns mixed with the sprint orchestrator's `log` lines instead of with its `err`/`warn` diagnostics on stderr.
bugs.root_cause: The `warn()` helper was written without `>&2` even though every neighbouring entry point convention (Unix tradition: warnings on stderr; companion run-sprint.sh defining the same helper WITH `>&2`; the local err() on the next line WITH `>&2`) signals stderr is the right destination. The miss was a single missing redirect — likely a copy-paste from log()/ok() (which legitimately go to stdout for normal informational output) without thinking about the warn semantic. The bug survived this long because most warn() call sites fire on terminals where stdout and stderr both render to the same TTY, so the visible behaviour looked identical; only stream-redirection cases exposed the asymmetry.
bugs.fix: Added `>&2` to scripts/run-sessions.sh:63's warn() definition so it now reads `warn() { echo -e "${YELLOW}[epic]${NC} $*" >&2; }`, matching scripts/run-sprint.sh:60 and the local err() on the next line. All 24+ existing warn() call sites now route to stderr without any other change. Verified by `grep -n '^warn\|^err\|^log\|^ok\|^dim' scripts/run-sessions.sh scripts/run-sprint.sh` showing both warns now end in `>&2`. No behavioural impact for users on a TTY; users redirecting `2>` now capture warnings alongside errors as intended; orchestrators parsing the [EPIC_RESULT_START] block from stdout no longer risk warn-lines polluting the structured output.

bugs.error_message: README.md:77-90 'What it does' section enumerated only TWO numbered commands — `/epic-toolkit:epic.generate` (item 1) and `/epic-toolkit:epic` (item 2) — but the README's own opening at line 7 declares 'Adds three slash commands' (corrected by bug-254) and the Files block at lines 185-188 lists all three (epic.md, epic.generate.md, sprint.md). The third command, `/epic-toolkit:sprint` (multi-epic orchestrator that runs N epics back-to-back on a shared trunk and opens ONE PR for the whole sprint), was completely absent from the 'What it does' explainer despite being present in every other README enumeration (lines 7-17 bullets, lines 33-36 install examples, line 49 OpenCode mention, lines 185-188 Files block). The 'What it does' section is the README's primary teaching surface for understanding the toolkit's capabilities — a reader who skips the bullet list at the top and lands on this section would learn the toolkit ships exactly two user-visible commands and miss the multi-epic workflow entirely.

The earlier sweep (bug-254) patched four other sites in this same file, but the 'What it does' numbered-list section was not in the audited set — it described the toolkit as a two-step workflow (generate → run) that pre-dates the sprint primitive. The introductory bullets at lines 7-17 already covered all three commands after bug-254, so the README looked symmetric on a quick scan even though this section continued to omit /sprint.
bugs.fix: Added a third numbered item to README.md:77-90 'What it does' section describing /epic-toolkit:sprint as 'runs N epics back-to-back on a shared trunk branch and opens a single PR for the whole sprint. Each epic is a normal /epic-toolkit:epic run; the wrapper enforces sequential execution, forwards --no-pr to every epic except the final one, and emits a single sprint-level summary.' The wording mirrors commands/sprint.md:5 and AGENTS.md:16/CLAUDE.md:16's command summaries so the three enumerations stay synchronized. Doc-only; no behavioural impact.

bugs.error_message: AGENTS.md was missing the '## Documentation lookups' section that CLAUDE.md ships at lines 50-52. The section instructs the agent to use the `context7` MCP server for fetching current library/framework/SDK/API/CLI/shell-semantics docs (Bash, shellcheck, Python stdlib, gh CLI, git plumbing, etc.) instead of relying on training data. Without it in AGENTS.md, AGENTS.md-aware AI coding agents (Codex, OpenCode, and others — the audience AGENTS.md:10 explicitly addresses after bug-258) had no signal that this project expects context7 lookups for library docs, while the parallel Claude Code surface had the convention. `diff -u CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md` showed only two intended divergences (the file header tool-naming on lines 8-10 — bug-258) plus this unintended drift (the entire trailing section). Same audit class as bugs 256/257/258 — symmetric AGENTS.md ↔ CLAUDE.md drift; the two files are intentional siblings that should agree on every project-policy section, with tool-neutral wording in AGENTS.md when CLAUDE.md uses Claude-specific terminology.
bugs.root_cause: When the 'Documentation lookups' policy was added to CLAUDE.md, the parallel addition to AGENTS.md was missed — the same drift class as bug-256 (Codex vs claude on the Requirements line) and bug-257 (sprint missing from the project paragraph). The CLAUDE.md → AGENTS.md mirror is the project's documented convention for keeping both agent guides in sync, but mirror updates have to be made by hand on every section change and the bar is easy to miss. The fix is purely additive on AGENTS.md, with tool-neutral phrasing because the Claude Code-specific MCP tool names (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id`, `mcp__context7__query-docs`) differ across hosts (Codex, OpenCode each have their own MCP tool naming).
bugs.fix: Added a '## Documentation lookups' section to AGENTS.md after the Requirements section, mirroring CLAUDE.md's policy with tool-neutral wording: 'Resolve the library ID first via the host's context7 resolution tool, then query the docs.' instead of CLAUDE.md's Claude-specific 'Resolve the library ID first (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id`) then query (`mcp__context7__query-docs`).' The rest of the section (when to use, what to skip, the training-data-drifts framing) is identical to CLAUDE.md so the two guides stay synchronized on the policy itself. Doc-only; no behavioural impact for the toolkit, but Codex/OpenCode agents now receive the same context7 nudge that Claude Code agents already do.

bugs.error_message: AGENTS.md:40 and CLAUDE.md:40 both invoked the DAG previewer with `python scripts/epic-dag.py --show docs/claude-sessions/<name>/` while every other documentation site that ships a python invocation uses `python3` explicitly: commands/epic.generate.md:207 (`python3 "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/epic-dag.py" ... --show`), .opencode/commands/epic.generate.md:207 (`python3 scripts/epic-dag.py ... --show`), docs/epic-prompt-template.md:158 (`python3 "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/epic-dag.py" ... --show`). The runner itself (scripts/run-sessions.sh:307-315) probes python3 first and falls back to python only when python3 is absent — i.e. the project's preferred interpreter is python3 by convention. macOS 12.3+ removed the system `python` symlink (Python 2 deprecation), and a fresh Homebrew install of python@3 ships only `python3` unless the user opts into the unversioned `python` symlink. So a user copy-pasting the AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md preview block onto stock macOS would hit `command not found: python` when every other doc would work — an asymmetric, host-dependent failure that hides the cause behind a generic shell error.
bugs.root_cause: The two top-level agent guides drifted from the rest of the docs on python interpreter convention. Likely written before the runner consolidated on python3 (or before the .opencode/commands and commands/ docs were standardized to python3), and never re-audited. Auto-hooks logged the surface change (bug-263, bug-264 — 'Wrong reference: python should be python3') but those entries don't capture the cross-doc consistency rationale or the macOS 12.3+ portability angle, so this narrative entry stands as the audit record alongside them.
bugs.fix: Replaced `python` with `python3` on AGENTS.md:40 and CLAUDE.md:40 so both preview blocks now read `python3 scripts/epic-dag.py --show docs/claude-sessions/<name>/`. Matches every other doc site that names a python invocation (commands/epic.generate.md, .opencode/commands/epic.generate.md, docs/epic-prompt-template.md) and the runner's own python3-preferred resolution at scripts/run-sessions.sh:307. Doc-only; no behavioural impact for users on systems where `python` already resolves to python3, but stock macOS 12.3+ users now have a working preview command.

bugs.error_message:
scripts/epic-dag.py `_strip_inline_comment` computed `value_start = line.find(':') + 1` from the FIRST `:` in the line, even when that `:` sat INSIDE an inline `# ...` comment. Concrete repro: a malformed frontmatter line `key  # has : in comment` made `line.find(':')` land at position 11 (inside the comment), value_start jumped to 12, the scan loop started from the `i` of `in comment` — well past the `#` at position 5 — and the inline-comment strip never fired. The full untouched line then flowed back to parse_frontmatter, where the early `raw.lstrip().startswith('#')` check did NOT fire (the line starts with `k`), and `raw.partition(':')` partitioned at the comment's `:` — producing the bogus dict entry `{'key  # has': 'in comment'}` in the parsed frontmatter. Verified empirically: parsing `---
session: 3
key  # has : in comment
title: "Auth API"
---` returned `{'session': 3, 'key  # has': 'in comment', 'title': 'Auth API'}`. The bogus key is downstream-ignored by load_sessions (which only reads known keys: session, title, depends_on, touches, parallel_safe, model, cli, produces, skip_deliverables_check), so no observable runtime crash — but the parser's contract is violated: a line the user wrote as a comment-with-stray-colon is silently materialised as a key/value pair, polluting the frontmatter dict. Same shape as bug-117/123/124/126/132/134/135/136 — every YAML parser site that consumes a token boundary must respect comment markers, not just quote/flow boundaries.
bugs.root_cause: The audit chain at bugs 117/123/124/126/132/134/135/136 covered three context kinds for `#`-strip safety (scalar-quoted, flow-list/flow-map, block-list-item) but never addressed the case where the `:` THE PARSER USES TO LOCATE VALUE-START is itself inside an inline comment. The function's value_start logic assumes `line.find(':')` returns the key/value separator, but find() returns the FIRST `:` regardless of context. In well-formed YAML the first `:` IS the separator, but malformed key-only lines with stray colons in their inline comments break the assumption. The bug survived because real session frontmatter is tightly templated (well-formed key:value pairs, no stray-colon comments), so the audit chain never produced a failing case from production telemetry.
bugs.fix: Added a pre-scan from position 1 to the first `:` (or end of line) for `# ` (`#` preceded by space or tab). If found, return `line[:j].rstrip()` immediately — the `:` cannot be the key/value separator because a comment marker precedes it. Unquoted YAML keys have no quoted regions, so a plain linear scan suffices for the key portion; quoted-value handling stays in the main loop unchanged. Verified with the repro case (`{'session': 3, 'title': 'Auth API'}` — bogus key absent) and regression-checked on existing cases (key:value with inline comment, quoted values containing `#`, flow-lists with quoted commas, block-list items, value-after-colon with `:` in comment) — all parse correctly. `bash -n` and `python3 import` clean.

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:557 `unset _seq_tmp _old_wave_count _new_wave _ids _sid _seq_wn _seq_sid _seq_model _seq_cli` omitted `_old_wn`, the C-style for-loop counter declared at line 525 (`for (( _old_wn=1; _old_wn<=_old_wave_count; _old_wn++ ))`). After the SEQUENTIAL block runs, `_old_wn` leaks into the global namespace alongside the other transient `_seq_*` / `_old_*` helpers the unset block was meant to clean up. The runner uses `set -u`, so the leaked variable doesn't cause unbound-variable crashes; the symptom is purely hygiene drift — `_old_wn` survives until script exit and would shadow any later code path that happened to declare or read a same-named variable without `local`. Same audit class as the implicit hygiene contract every other transient in the SEQUENTIAL block follows (every `_seq_*`/`_old_*` is created inside the if-block and unset on exit) — `_old_wn` was the only loop counter in the block that escaped.
bugs.root_cause: The `_old_wn` counter was added when the SEQUENTIAL block was authored to iterate over original waves, but the unset list at the end of the block was written to track named helpers (`_old_wave_count`, `_new_wave`, `_seq_*`) rather than every transient introduced. C-style for-loop counters are easy to miss in unset audits because they're declared as part of the loop syntax, not as a separate `local` declaration. The miss survived because `set -u` doesn't flag namespace pollution, only undefined reads.
bugs.fix: Added `_old_wn` to the unset list at scripts/run-sessions.sh:557 so it now reads `unset _seq_tmp _old_wave_count _old_wn _new_wave _ids _sid _seq_wn _seq_sid _seq_model _seq_cli`. Matches the cleanup discipline applied to every other transient in the SEQUENTIAL block. `bash -n` clean; no behavioural change in the happy path (the variable was never read after the loop).

bugs.error_message: scripts/run-sessions.sh:276 comment claimed `map_model_shorthand` is 'called at line 246, before sourcing' — but line 246 is `exit 1` inside the `--cli` override validation block, NOT a call site. The actual earliest call (the one that requires the function to be defined before the `source "$_LIB_DIR/…"` block at line 333) is `MODEL="$(map_model_shorthand "$MODEL" "$CLI_CMD")"` at line 302. The line number drifted whenever this file shifted — same comment-rot class as bug-225 (do not hard-code result-file paths in command docs because they drift) but inside source comments rather than user docs.
bugs.root_cause: Hard-coded absolute line number in a maintainer-facing source comment, with no mechanism to keep it synced when the surrounding code grows. The comment rationale (function must be defined before lib sourcing) stayed accurate, but the bare `line 246` reference rotted as the file expanded between the original write and now (current early call is at line 302, line 246 is now `exit 1` inside the `--cli` override validation block — a misleading pointer that confuses any reader who follows it). Audit class: every absolute line-number reference in a source comment is a future-rot site by construction; either drop the number (rely on the rationale) or anchor it to a stable token grep can find. `grep -rn "line [0-9]\{3\}" scripts/` surfaced this site (only one in the codebase; epic-git.sh:244's `(line ~212)` reference still happens to be accurate today, kept as-is because the `~` already marks it approximate).
bugs.fix: Replaced the comment at scripts/run-sessions.sh:276 with a future-proof rationale: name the actual mechanism (function called as part of model resolution above the `source "$_LIB_DIR/…"` block) and the failure mode (previous `line 246` had drifted to an unrelated exit site), then explicitly state the convention going forward — keep the rationale, drop the line number. No behavioural change; sed range in `usage()` (the only OTHER hard-coded line range in this file, at scripts/run-sessions.sh:77) was already protected by bug-240's audit-coverage comment so it's regenerated when options change. `bash -n scripts/run-sessions.sh` clean. Same future-proofing pattern as bug-225's RESULT_FILE-printing fix.
